- https://www.npr.org/2025/05/01/nx-s1-5339845/trump-waltz
- https://www.foxnews.com/politics/next-us-national-security-advisor-heres-who-trump-might-pick-replace-waltz
- https://www.latimes.com/world-nation/story/2025-05-01/trump-national-security-adviser-mike-waltz-is-out-in-first-major-shakeup-of-trumps-second-term
- https://www.nytimes.com/2025/05/01/us/politics/michael-waltz-signal-leak-trump.html
- https://www.jpost.com/breaking-news/article-852274
questions.resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as "Yes" if Michael Waltz is the officially serving U.S. National Security Advisor on May 15, 2025, 11:59 PM ET. It will resolve as "No" if he has resigned, been dismissed, or been formally replaced by a successor (acting or permanent) by this date and time. For the purpose of this question: Serving U.S. National Security Advisor refers to the individual officially designated by the President of the United States to hold the title of Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs, or National Security Advisor, and performing the duties associated with that role. This includes both permanent and acting appointments. Officially announced means a public statement or publication from the White House or the President of the United States.
questions.resolution_source: The primary resolution source will be official announcements published on the White House website (whitehouse.gov), specifically in the "Releases" or "Statements and Releases" sections. In the event that whitehouse.gov is unavailable or does not contain definitive information by the resolution date, consistent reporting from at least two of the following major, reputable news organizations will be used: The Associated Press (apnews.com), Reuters (reuters.com), The New York Times (nytimes.com), or The Washington Post (washingtonpost.com). These secondary sources must explicitly cite official White House sources or provide clear evidence of Michael Waltz's status as National Security Advisor.
questions.fine_print: Partial Action: Not applicable for a binary question regarding a specific individual holding a specific role. Reversal: If Michael Waltz is removed from the position and subsequently re-appointed as the serving U.S. National Security Advisor on or before May 15, 2025, the question will resolve based on his status at 11:59 PM ET on May 15, 2025. Subordinate Action: The appointment or dismissal of the National Security Advisor is a presidential prerogative. Any official announcement regarding Michael Waltz's status must be made by the President, the White House Press Secretary, or another authorized White House official. Source Unavailability: If whitehouse.gov is temporarily or permanently unavailable, resolution will rely on consistent reporting from at least two of the specified secondary news sources. Government Shutdown: A government shutdown may delay official publications on whitehouse.gov. In such a case, resolution will rely on consistent reporting from at least two of the specified secondary news sources. Different Legal Form: The National Security Advisor is an appointed position that does not require Senate confirmation. Any official announcement of appointment, resignation, or dismissal, regardless of specific phrasing (e.g., "reassigned," "stepping down," "named interim"), will be considered definitive for resolution.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: During President Donald Trump's first term (January 2017 - January 2021), there were four individuals who served as National Security Advisor: Michael Flynn (24 days), H.R. McMaster (approx. 13 months), John Bolton (approx. 17 months), and Robert O'Brien (until the end of the term, approx. 16 months). The question asks about Michael Waltz's status on May 15, 2025, which is approximately 115 days (just under 4 months) into President Trump's second term (assuming an inauguration date of January 20, 2025). Of the four NSAs in Trump's first term, Michael Flynn did not serve for 115 days. The other three (McMaster, Bolton, O'Brien) served for longer than 115 days. Therefore, the historical frequency of a National Security Advisor *not* serving for at least 115 days into their tenure in a Trump administration is 1 out of 4, or 0.25. This base rate reflects the historical turnover in this specific role under President Trump.

questions.title: Will Marco Rubio be appointed as either acting or permanent National Security Advisor before June 1, 2025?
questions.background: In any administration, there is a short list of contenders for top national security jobs. Secretary of State Marco Rubio is a key principal on the National Security Council and one of the administration's most visible foreign policy figures. While it is not common for a sitting Secretary of State to also take on the role of National Security Advisor, it is not without precedent. As the administration navigates a complex global landscape, speculation is ongoing about who holds the most influence with the President and who might be tapped for new roles in the event of a shuffle.
questions.question_text: Will Marco Rubio be appointed as either acting or permanent National Security Advisor at any point before June 1, 2025, 11:59 PM ET?
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of March 5, 2025, Marco Rubio is serving as the Secretary of State. The current National Security Advisor (NSA) is Mike Waltz. There is historical precedent for a single individual holding both the Secretary of State and National Security Advisor roles simultaneously, most notably Henry Kissinger under Presidents Richard Nixon and Gerald Ford in the 1970s. As of the research date, there are no public reports or rumors circulating about an imminent change in the National Security Advisor position or Marco Rubio being considered for such a role. Key stakeholders include the President (who holds the authority to appoint the NSA), Marco Rubio, and Mike Waltz. The National Security Advisor is a staff position within the Executive Office of the President and does not require Senate confirmation, allowing the President greater flexibility and speed in making such an appointment. The Secretary of State is a Cabinet position and requires Senate confirmation.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources for this question include official White House announcements, which would typically be via a White House press release, official statement, or social media post from the President. Major news outlets such as The Associated Press, Reuters, The New York Times, The Washington Post, CNN, and Fox News would report on such a high-profile appointment immediately. Appointments to positions like National Security Advisor are made on an as-needed basis, typically following a resignation, dismissal, or the beginning of a new administration; there is no fixed publication schedule. New information would primarily appear through White House press briefings and official communications channels, as well as breaking news alerts from major domestic and international news agencies and official social media accounts of the President and the White House.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of March 5, 2025, Marco Rubio is not the National Security Advisor; Mike Waltz holds this position. The most notable historical precedent for holding both the Secretary of State and National Security Advisor roles simultaneously is Henry Kissinger, who served in both capacities from 1973 to 1975. As of the research date, there are no public projections or forecasts specifically predicting Marco Rubio's appointment as National Security Advisor before June 1, 2025.

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution more likely include the President's implied trust in Marco Rubio, given his position as Secretary of State and a visible foreign policy figure, suggesting a potential desire to consolidate power or streamline decision-making. The NSA role does not require Senate confirmation, making an appointment faster and less politically contentious. The historical precedent of Henry Kissinger holding both roles demonstrates its possibility. Factors that make a NO resolution more likely include Rubio's already demanding role as Secretary of State, which involves extensive travel and diplomatic responsibilities, potentially leading to concerns about overstretch. Mike Waltz is the incumbent National Security Advisor, and there are no public reports as of March 5, 2025, indicating his imminent departure. Potential for perceived conflicts of interest or dilution of focus if one person holds both top diplomatic and national security advisory roles also exists. Additionally, other qualified candidates would likely be considered if the NSA position became vacant.

## Sources
- https://www.cnn.com/2025/03/21/politics/rubio-has-competition-for-the-role-of-americas-top-diplomat/index.html
- https://www.newsweek.com/marco-rubio-major-said-cuts-elon-musk-2042136
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves YES if Marco Rubio is officially announced as either the acting or permanent National Security Advisor of the United States by the White House, the President, or a major, reputable news organization (e.g., The Associated Press, Reuters, The New York Times, The Washington Post, CNN, Fox News) reporting on an official White House announcement, at any point before June 1, 2025, 11:59 PM ET. Resolves NO otherwise. Definitions: National Security Advisor (NSA): The Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs, a staff position within the Executive Office of the President. Appointed: An official designation by the President of the United States. This can be announced via a White House press release, an official statement from the President, or a post on the President's official social media accounts. Acting National Security Advisor: An individual temporarily designated to perform the duties of the National Security Advisor. Permanent National Security Advisor: An individual officially designated to hold the full, ongoing responsibilities of the National Security Advisor. Before June 1, 2025, 11:59 PM ET: The appointment must be announced and publicly verifiable by this date and time. The time zone is Eastern Time.
questions.resolution_source: Primary Source: Official White House announcements, including whitehouse.gov press releases (https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/statements-releases/), official statements, or posts from the President's official social media accounts. Secondary Source (for confirmation): Reporting from at least two of the following major, reputable news organizations confirming an official White House announcement: The Associated Press (AP), Reuters, The New York Times (nytimes.com), The Washington Post (washingtonpost.com), CNN (cnn.com), or Fox News (foxnews.com).
questions.fine_print: Action Reversed: If Marco Rubio is appointed as NSA before June 1, 2025, and then subsequently removed or resigns before June 1, 2025, the question will still resolve YES. The key is whether the appointment occurs at any point before the deadline. Done by Subordinate: The appointment must be made by the President of the United States. An announcement by a subordinate official will only count if it explicitly states it is on behalf of the President and is subsequently confirmed by the President or the White House. Source Temporarily Unavailable: If the primary resolution source (whitehouse.gov) is temporarily unavailable, secondary sources will be used. If all specified sources are unavailable for an extended period, Metaculus will use its best judgment to determine the outcome based on the most reliable information available. Government Shutdown: A government shutdown will not prevent the resolution of this question. Official announcements and news reporting are expected to continue. Different Legal Form: Any official designation by the President that clearly indicates Marco Rubio is serving as either acting or permanent National Security Advisor will count, regardless of the specific legal phrasing, as long as it is publicly announced and verifiable.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: Historically, it is extremely rare for a sitting Secretary of State to also be appointed as National Security Advisor. The most notable precedent is Henry Kissinger, who held both roles simultaneously from 1973 to 1975. This is a single instance in over 50 years since the NSA role was formalized in 1953. Based on this, the historical frequency is very low, approximately 0.02 (1 instance / 50 years).

questions.title: By April 1, 2025, what will be the status of reclassifying federal jobs under the new "Schedule F" authority?
questions.background: On January 20, 2025, his first day in office, President Trump signed an executive order reinstating a policy akin to the "Schedule F" authority from his first term. The order aims to strip civil service protections from potentially tens of thousands of federal employees in policy-related roles, making them easier to hire and fire. While the executive order is signed, the next crucial step is the actual reclassification of positions by the Office of Personnel Management (OPM) and individual agencies. This process is expected to face significant resistance from federal employee unions and legal challenges. It remains to be seen how quickly and extensively the administration can or will implement this new authority.
questions.question_text: By April 1, 2025, which of the following best describes the implementation status of the executive order re-establishing Schedule F authority for federal employees?
options: Aggressive Implementation: OPM will have issued final guidance and at least 10,000 positions will have been formally reclassified by federal agencies.
Moderate Implementation: OPM will have issued final guidance, and between 1,000 and 9,999 positions will have been formally reclassified.
Initial Implementation: OPM will have issued final guidance, but fewer than 1,000 positions will have been formally reclassified.
Guidance Only: OPM will have issued interim or proposed guidance for reclassification, but no positions will have been formally reclassified.
Take no action: OPM will not have issued any formal guidance for implementation, and no positions will have been reclassified.
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
On January 20, 2025, his first day in office, President Trump reportedly signed an executive order reinstating a policy akin to the "Schedule F" authority from his first term. This order aims to reclassify potentially tens of thousands of federal employees in policy-related roles, moving them from the competitive service to an "excepted service" category, making them easier to hire and fire. As of January 22, 2025, the executive order has been signed, but the actual reclassification process by the Office of Personnel Management (OPM) and individual agencies has not yet begun. No positions have been formally reclassified. The immediate next crucial step is for OPM to issue guidance to federal agencies on how to identify and reclassify positions under this new authority. Historically, on October 21, 2020, President Trump issued Executive Order 13957, "Creating Schedule F in the Excepted Service," which directed agencies to review positions for reclassification. This order was rescinded by President Biden on January 22, 2021, via Executive Order 14003, "Protecting the Federal Workforce." Key stakeholders include the President/Administration, OPM, federal agencies, federal employee unions (e.g., AFGE, NTEU), legal advocacy groups, and Congress. The regulatory/legal framework involves the President's authority under 5 U.S.C. § 3302 to create exceptions to the competitive service. Legal challenges, expected from unions and advocacy groups, will likely argue that Schedule F undermines merit system principles and statutory protections for federal employees.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include the Office of Personnel Management (OPM) for official guidance and potential data on reclassified positions (OPM.gov), the Federal Register (FederalRegister.gov) for official rules and executive orders, White House press releases (WhiteHouse.gov), individual agency websites for internal updates, and news media/legal journals for reporting and analysis. There is no fixed publication schedule for reclassification numbers; however, OPM typically publishes formal guidance in the Federal Register. Information on the number of reclassified positions, if made public, would likely come from OPM or through congressional oversight/reporting. New information will primarily become available via OPM.gov, FederalRegister.gov, White House communications, and major news outlets.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of January 22, 2025, zero (0) positions have been formally reclassified under the new Schedule F authority. The executive order was reportedly signed on January 20, 2025, and the process of issuing guidance and agency action takes time. Under the original Schedule F (2020-2021), while full implementation was cut short, agencies had begun identifying positions. Reports indicated that by January 2021, some agencies had submitted lists to OPM, with estimates ranging from thousands to tens of thousands of positions identified for potential reclassification, though the exact number of formally reclassified positions before the rescission was limited. For example, the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) reportedly identified 88% of its permanent workforce for Schedule F conversion (Source: "Trump administration rushed to implement Schedule F before Biden took office," Government Executive, January 21, 2021). Projections for the total number of potentially affected positions based on the previous Schedule F ranged from 50,000 to 80,000. The previous Schedule F allowed agencies approximately 90 days to identify and submit positions, suggesting that significant reclassifications within the 70-day window (Jan 22 - April 1) would require extremely rapid action. Legal experts consistently predict immediate and robust legal challenges, which could significantly delay or halt implementation.

## Trends
Factors that make "Aggressive Implementation" (Options A, B, C) more likely include: a strong presidential mandate for rapid implementation, OPM quickly issuing comprehensive and clear final guidance, federal agencies moving swiftly to identify and reclassify positions, and the absence of successful legal challenges (e.g., injunctions) by April 1, 2025. Pre-planning by the administration with draft OPM guidance could also accelerate the process. Factors that make "Guidance Only" or "Take no action" (Options D, E) more likely include: immediate and significant legal challenges seeking injunctions to block the executive order or its implementation, OPM facing internal challenges or political resistance in developing and issuing guidance, agencies being slow to act due to legal concerns or administrative burden, the inherent complexity and subjectivity of identifying positions for reclassification, a lack of clarity or new complexities in the executive order, and the new administration prioritizing other immediate policy goals.

## Sources
- https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2020/10/26/2020-23825/creating-schedule-f-in-the-excepted-service
- https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2021/01/26/2021-01967/protecting-the-federal-workforce
- https://www.govexec.com/management/2021/01/trump-administration-rushed-implement-schedule-f-biden-took-office/171448/
questions.resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as the option that best describes the status as of April 1, 2025, 11:59 PM ET, based on the following definitions and conditions: "Final guidance": Refers to a "final rule," "final regulation," or similar official document issued by the Office of Personnel Management (OPM) and published in the Federal Register (www.federalregister.gov) that explicitly provides instructions or procedures for federal agencies to reclassify positions under the new Schedule F authority. "Interim or proposed guidance": Refers to a "proposed rule," "interim rule," "notice of proposed rulemaking," or similar official document issued by OPM and published in the Federal Register (www.federalregister.gov) that solicits public comment or provides preliminary instructions regarding the implementation of the executive order re-establishing Schedule F authority. "Formally reclassified positions": Refers to positions that have been officially moved from the competitive service to the excepted service under the new Schedule F authority by federal agencies, following OPM guidance. The number of such positions must be publicly reported by OPM (www.opm.gov), individual federal agencies on their official websites, or a credible, official government source (e.g., a congressional report citing OPM data). If multiple numbers are reported, the highest credible number from an official source will be used. If no specific number is reported, or if reports only refer to "identified" or "proposed" positions without formal reclassification, the count will be considered 0. Resolution Conditions for Each Option: A. Aggressive Implementation: Resolves as Option A if OPM has issued "final guidance" (as defined above) AND at least 10,000 positions have been "formally reclassified" (as defined above) by federal agencies. B. Moderate Implementation: Resolves as Option B if OPM has issued "final guidance" (as defined above) AND between 1,000 and 9,999 positions have been "formally reclassified" (as defined above). C. Initial Implementation: Resolves as Option C if OPM has issued "final guidance" (as defined above) BUT fewer than 1,000 positions (i.e., 1 to 999) have been "formally reclassified" (as defined above). D. Guidance Only: Resolves as Option D if OPM has issued "interim or proposed guidance" (as defined above) for reclassification, but no positions (i.e., 0) have been "formally reclassified" (as defined above). This option also applies if OPM has issued "final guidance" but no positions (i.e., 0) have been "formally reclassified." E. Take no action: Resolves as Option E if OPM has not issued any "final guidance" or "interim or proposed guidance" (as defined above) for implementation, and no positions (i.e., 0) have been "formally reclassified" (as defined above).
questions.resolution_source: For OPM Guidance (Final, Interim, or Proposed): The Federal Register (www.federalregister.gov). For Number of Formally Reclassified Positions: Official reports or public statements from the Office of Personnel Management (OPM) (www.opm.gov), individual federal agency websites, or credible congressional reports citing OPM data.
questions.fine_print: Partial Action: For guidance, "final guidance" requires a document explicitly labeled as a "final rule" or "final regulation" by OPM in the Federal Register. "Interim or proposed guidance" requires a document explicitly labeled as such. For reclassified positions, the number will be counted as officially reported by the specified sources. Reports of "identified" or "proposed" positions that are not formally reclassified will not count towards the reclassification numbers. Action Reversed: If guidance is issued or positions are formally reclassified, but the action is subsequently reversed, challenged, or enjoined by a court, the status as of April 1, 2025, 11:59 PM ET, will determine the outcome. A reversal or injunction that takes effect after the resolution date will not affect the resolution. Done by Subordinate: OPM guidance, even if signed by an OPM official rather than the President directly, will count as action by the administration. Reclassification actions taken by individual federal agencies following OPM guidance will count towards the reclassified position numbers. Resolution Source Unavailable: If the primary resolution sources (Federal Register, OPM.gov, or relevant agency websites) are temporarily unavailable on the resolution date, resolution will be postponed until they become available or until an alternative official government source (e.g., a White House press briefing transcript, official congressional testimony, Government Accountability Office report) provides the necessary information clearly indicating the status as of April 1, 2025. Government Shutdown: If a government shutdown delays the publication of guidance or reclassification numbers, the question will be resolved based on the information available by the resolution date. If information is delayed but subsequently released and clearly indicates the status as of April 1, 2025, the question will be resolved based on that information. Different Legal Form: Any official document from OPM that serves the purpose of guiding agencies on Schedule F implementation, regardless of its specific legal nomenclature (e.g., "directive," "memorandum," "bulletin"), if published in the Federal Register or on OPM.gov, will be considered for "guidance" if its content clearly functions as such.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: The original Schedule F (Executive Order 13957) was issued on October 21, 2020, and rescinded on January 22, 2021, a period of approximately three months. During this time, OPM issued the initial executive order, and agencies began the process of identifying positions for reclassification. However, reports from January 2021 indicated that while some agencies had submitted lists of identified positions (e.g., OMB identified 88% of its workforce for potential conversion), the number of formally reclassified positions before the rescission was "limited." This suggests that within a similar timeframe (the current question covers approximately 70 days from January 20 to April 1, 2025), achieving large-scale formal reclassifications (1,000 or more) is historically challenging, especially with anticipated legal and administrative hurdles. Based on this precedent, a base rate of 0.3 for 'Initial Implementation' (1-999 reclassified) is assigned, reflecting the possibility of some formal reclassifications but not extensive ones. The base rate for 'Guidance Only' (0.45) is higher, reflecting the likelihood of OPM issuing guidance without significant formal reclassifications by the deadline. 'Aggressive' (0.05) and 'Moderate' (0.10) implementations are considered less likely given the historical context and short timeframe. 'Take no action' (0.10) is also less likely given the President's initial executive order.

questions.title: Will the federal hiring freeze be lifted for at least one cabinet-level department by March 15, 2025?
questions.background: Alongside other measures targeting the federal workforce, President Trump issued a memorandum on January 20, 2025, instituting a government-wide hiring freeze. The order bars the filling of any vacant civilian position and the creation of any new positions. The memorandum states the freeze will be lifted for most agencies after a plan is developed within 90 days to "reduce the size of the Federal Government's workforce." However, the administration faces competing pressures. While the freeze supports the goal of shrinking government, certain departments may need staffing to implement the president's policy agenda, potentially creating pressure to grant exceptions and lift the freeze sooner for specific agencies.
questions.question_text: Will the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) or the White House formally announce that the general hiring freeze implemented on January 20, 2025, has been lifted for at least one cabinet-level department by March 15, 2025? (This does not include the exemptions for national security, military, or public safety positions outlined in the original order).
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of 2025-01-22, a federal hiring freeze was instituted by President Trump, going into effect on January 20, 2025. This order prevents the filling of vacant civilian positions and the creation of any new positions across the federal government. This freeze is part of a broader effort by the Trump administration to reduce the size of the federal workforce. The Office of Management and Budget (OMB) and the Office of Personnel Management (OPM) issued a memo on January 8, 2025, providing guidance on the freeze. This memo stated that job offers made and accepted prior to January 20, 2025, with an unconfirmed start date or a start date later than February 8, 2025, are revoked. Offers with a start date earlier than February 8, 2025, could remain. The Department of Government Efficiency, led by Elon Musk, is also working on recommendations to cut the size of the federal workforce, reduce regulations, and improve efficiency. Key stakeholders include President Trump, OMB, OPM, cabinet-level departments, federal employees, unions, and the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE). The regulatory framework is a Presidential Memorandum or Executive Order. (Source: Trump administration says it's revoking some government job offers - Business Insider, published January 22, 2025)

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include White House Press Releases/Statements, Office of Management and Budget (OMB) Official Statements/Guidance, Office of Personnel Management (OPM) Directives, and the Federal Register. Agency-specific announcements may also provide information. Agencies are required to report monthly on job offers, new hires, departures, and head count. Formal announcements regarding the lifting of the freeze for any department would likely be made publicly and immediately by the White House or OMB. New information will be available on official White House channels (whitehouse.gov), OMB and OPM websites, and major news outlets covering federal government policy. (Source: Trump administration says it's revoking some government job offers - Business Insider, published January 22, 2025)

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of 2025-01-22, the federal hiring freeze is currently in effect across all non-exempt civilian positions. There is no indication from available sources that the freeze has been lifted for any cabinet-level department. The question pertains to a future event, so there is no direct 'prior period' outcome for this exact question. The background of the question states that the memorandum indicates the freeze will be lifted for most agencies after a plan is developed within 90 days to 'reduce the size of the Federal Government's workforce.' The question asks if the freeze will be lifted for at least one department before this general 90-day period (by March 15, 2025).

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution more likely (freeze lifted for at least one cabinet-level department by March 15, 2025) include: an urgent and unforeseen need for personnel in a specific cabinet-level department to address a national crisis, implement a critical policy, or maintain essential services; successful lobbying by a cabinet secretary or influential political figure for an early exemption; or an unexpectedly severe negative impact of the freeze on a department's ability to function. Factors that make a NO resolution more likely (freeze not lifted for any cabinet-level department by March 15, 2025) include: a strong commitment from the administration to the overall goal of reducing the federal workforce; a preference to adhere to the 90-day review process before making decisions about lifting the freeze; a desire to maintain consistent political messaging regarding reducing government size; or the existing exemptions for national security, military, and public safety positions being deemed sufficient to cover immediate critical needs.

## Sources
- https://www.businessinsider.com/some-federal-worker-job-offers-revoked-memo-2025-1
research_flags: HALLUCINATED_FACTS: The question's background states: 'The memorandum states the freeze will be lifted for most agencies after a plan is developed within 90 days to "reduce the size of the Federal Government's workforce."' While the implementation of the freeze and the general goal of workforce reduction are corroborated, the specific detail about the freeze being lifted after a '90-day plan' has not been independently corroborated by direct official sources (Presidential Memorandum, White House press release, or OMB statement) in the research conducted as of 2025-01-22. It is possible this detail comes from a separate, unlocated official document or a secondary interpretation.
questions.resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, by March 15, 2025, at 11:59 PM ET, the White House (whitehouse.gov) or the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) (omb.gov) formally announces that the general federal hiring freeze, implemented on January 20, 2025, has been lifted for at least one cabinet-level department. Otherwise, the question resolves NO. Definitions: Formal Announcement: An official press release, memorandum, executive order, or public statement published on whitehouse.gov (specifically, the "Briefing Room" or "Statements and Releases" sections) or omb.gov (specifically, the "Statements and Releases" or "Policy" sections). Cabinet-level Department: Refers to any of the fifteen executive departments of the U.S. federal government. A current list can be found on the USA.gov website: https://www.usa.gov/federal-agencies/cabinet-level-departments. Hiring Freeze Lifted: The announcement must explicitly state that the general hiring freeze is no longer in effect for at least one cabinet-level department. This does not include the existing exemptions for national security, military, or public safety positions outlined in the original January 20, 2025, order. The lifting must apply to general hiring within the department as a whole, not just a specific office or bureau within it.
questions.resolution_source: The primary resolution sources will be the official websites of the U.S. government: White House official website: https://www.whitehouse.gov/ (specifically, the "Briefing Room" or "Statements and Releases" sections). Office of Management and Budget official website: https://www.omb.gov/ (specifically, the "Statements and Releases" or "Policy" sections). In the event of ambiguity, delay, or unavailability of these primary sources, major reputable news outlets (e.g., Reuters, Associated Press, The New York Times, The Wall Street Journal, Politico) reporting directly on White House or OMB announcements will be used for confirmation.
questions.fine_print: Partial Lifting: If the freeze is lifted for only a portion of a cabinet-level department (e.g., a specific office, agency, or bureau within a department), but not the department as a recognized entity, this question will resolve NO. The lifting must apply to the department as a whole. Reversal: If an announcement lifting the freeze is made and then subsequently reversed before March 15, 2025, the question will resolve based on the latest official status as of the resolution date. If the freeze is lifted and then reversed after March 15, 2025, it will still resolve YES. Subordinate Action: The announcement must originate from the White House or the Office of Management and Budget. An announcement from an individual cabinet department, without explicit White House or OMB endorsement or publication on their official channels, will not be sufficient for a YES resolution. Source Unavailable/Delayed: If the primary resolution sources (whitehouse.gov, omb.gov) are temporarily unavailable or significantly delayed in publishing relevant information by the resolution date, reputable secondary sources (e.g., major news outlets citing official White House/OMB statements) will be used to determine the outcome. Government Shutdown: A government shutdown that delays official publications will not alter the resolution date. If no qualifying announcement is made by the deadline, the question will resolve NO. Different Legal Form: The announcement does not need to be a specific "memorandum" or "executive order." Any formal public statement from the White House or OMB that explicitly lifts the general hiring freeze for at least one cabinet-level department will suffice.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: Historically, federal hiring freezes have been implemented by various administrations, often early in their terms. Their duration and the speed at which exemptions or full liftings occur can vary. Examples include Nixon (1973), Carter (1977), Reagan (1981), Clinton (1993), and Trump (2017). The 2017 Trump administration freeze lasted 90 days before a general lifting, with agencies tasked to develop workforce reduction plans. The current question's deadline of March 15, 2025, is approximately 54 days after the January 20, 2025, freeze. The question asks if the freeze will be lifted for at least one department *before* the general 90-day review period. While targeted exemptions or adjustments for critical needs have occurred, a full, early, and explicit lifting for a specific cabinet-level department before the general review period is relatively infrequent. Therefore, the base rate for a 'YES' resolution, specifically for an explicit lifting for at least one cabinet-level department before the general 90-day review period, is estimated to be low (e.g., 0.1-0.3), with 0.2 as a central estimate.

questions.title: Will the US Secretary of State visit Greenland or Denmark to discuss Arctic security before April 1, 2025?
questions.background:
As of early 2025, the strategic importance of the Arctic continues to grow due to climate change opening new sea routes and escalating resource competition among the US, Russia, and China. Greenland, a semi-autonomous territory of the Kingdom of Denmark, is central to this dynamic due to its geographic location and significant natural resources.

During his first term, President Trump expressed a desire for the U.S. to purchase Greenland, an idea that was publicly dismissed by Danish and Greenlandic officials in 2019. While the proposal was dropped, the underlying strategic rationale has not disappeared. Key figures in foreign policy circles continue to highlight the need for a more robust U.S. posture in the Arctic to counter Russian and Chinese influence.

With a new administration setting its foreign policy agenda, it remains to be seen whether the U.S. will pursue a more assertive strategy in the region. An official visit by the Secretary of State to either Copenhagen or Nuuk, with Greenland as the explicit focus, would be a strong early indicator of the administration's intent. Such a visit could lay the groundwork for a new strategic cooperation agreement or, potentially, revive the controversial purchase proposal. However, the administration may choose to prioritize other global issues or pursue a more cautious diplomatic approach, making such a high-level visit uncertain.
questions.question_text: Will the US Secretary of State officially visit Greenland or Denmark to specifically discuss Arctic security or the status of Greenland before April 1, 2025?
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of January 2, 2025, Antony Blinken is the outgoing U.S. Secretary of State. His known travel schedule, announced for January 5, 2025, includes visits to South Korea, Japan, and France, with no mention of Greenland or Denmark. A new U.S. presidential administration is set to take office in January 2025. Senator Marco Rubio is the expected nominee for Secretary of State under the incoming administration, with Senate confirmation hearings and votes anticipated in mid-to-late January 2025. Therefore, the relevant Secretary of State for most of the forecasting period (January 20, 2025 - April 1, 2025) would be the incoming Secretary. The strategic importance of the Arctic, including Greenland, continues to be a significant geopolitical concern due to climate change, opening new sea routes, and competition for resources and influence among global powers like the U.S., Russia, and China. Greenland is a self-governing territory within the Kingdom of Denmark. While it has significant autonomy over domestic affairs, foreign policy and defense remain under Danish responsibility. During his first term, President Donald Trump publicly expressed interest in the U.S. purchasing Greenland in 2019, which was met with a firm rejection from both Danish and Greenlandic officials. The U.S. and Denmark have a defense agreement dating back to 1951, allowing U.S. military facilities in Greenland, notably the Pituffik Space Base. Denmark and Greenland have consistently affirmed Greenland's sovereignty and their joint responsibility for its security, expressing a desire for self-determination and increased international engagement within the framework of the Kingdom of Denmark. Denmark has also been increasing its own defense investments in the Arctic and enhancing cooperation with NATO allies in the region. Key stakeholders include the U.S. Secretary of State, the U.S. President, the Danish and Greenlandic governments, and NATO. The legal framework includes Greenland's Self-Government Act (2009) and the 1951 U.S.-Denmark Defense Agreement.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include official press releases, travel announcements, and policy statements from the U.S. Department of State (state.gov) and The White House (whitehouse.gov). The Danish Ministry of Foreign Affairs (um.dk) and Ministry of Defence (fmn.dk), as well as the Government of Greenland (naalakkersuisut.gl), provide official statements and policy documents. Major news outlets (e.g., Reuters, AP, BBC, New York Times, Washington Post) are crucial for reporting on diplomatic activities. Think tanks and academic institutions specializing in Arctic affairs also provide analysis. Official government websites are updated as events occur. News outlets publish daily. Policy documents are published periodically. New information regarding a visit would be announced by the U.S. Department of State or the White House, or by the Danish or Greenlandic governments, and reported by major news organizations.

## Recent Numbers and Events
This is a binary question, so current numbers are not applicable. There is no evidence from strictly filtered search results (published on or before 2025-01-02) of a U.S. Secretary of State officially visiting Greenland or Denmark specifically to discuss Arctic security or the status of Greenland between 2020 and 2024. The last known high-level U.S. engagement related to Greenland, prior to 2025-01-02, was President Trump's 2019 proposal to purchase the territory, which was rejected. As of 2025-01-02, specific projections for a Secretary of State visit by April 1, 2025, are not available.

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution more likely include: the incoming U.S. administration's documented historical interest in Greenland's strategic importance and potentially a more assertive approach to U.S. interests there; growing geopolitical competition in the Arctic from Russia and China, prompting the U.S. to strengthen its presence and alliances; Greenland's critical strategic value for missile defense, resources, and shipping routes; and the new administration's tendency to make high-profile diplomatic engagements early to signal foreign policy priorities. Factors that make a NO resolution more likely include: strong Danish and Greenlandic opposition to any actions undermining Greenland's sovereignty; potential prioritization of other global issues by the new administration; a cautious diplomatic approach to avoid alienating allies; logistical challenges and time constraints for a new Secretary of State within a short period; and the option to work through existing defense cooperation mechanisms rather than initiating a new high-level visit.

questions.resolution_criteria:
This question resolves YES if, before April 1, 2025, the U.S. Secretary of State officially visits Greenland or Denmark, and the visit's stated purpose or documented discussions explicitly include Arctic security or the status of Greenland. Otherwise, it resolves NO.

**Definitions:**
*   **U.S. Secretary of State**: The individual officially holding the title of United States Secretary of State, as confirmed by the U.S. Department of State's official website (state.gov) or The White House website (whitehouse.gov). This includes an acting Secretary of State if officially designated.
*   **Official visit**: A physical presence by the U.S. Secretary of State in Greenland or Denmark that is publicly announced as an official visit by the U.S. Department of State, The White House, the Danish Ministry of Foreign Affairs, or the Government of Greenland. The visit must include at least one official meeting with a head of government (Prime Minister of Denmark or Premier of Greenland), a foreign minister (Danish Minister of Foreign Affairs or Greenlandic Minister of Foreign Affairs), or a defense minister (Danish Minister of Defence). A stopover at an airport without an official meeting as defined above does not count.
*   **Greenland or Denmark**: The visit must take place within the internationally recognized geographical boundaries of the Kingdom of Denmark, specifically the country of Denmark (e.g., Copenhagen) or the self-governing territory of Greenland (e.g., Nuuk).
*   **Specifically discuss Arctic security or the status of Greenland**: The official purpose of the visit, or the topics discussed during the official meetings, must explicitly mention "Arctic security," "Arctic cooperation," "Greenland's status," "Greenland's future," "Greenland's strategic importance," or similar terms indicating a focus on these topics. This must be evident in official press releases, joint statements, meeting readouts, or official transcripts published by the U.S. Department of State, The White House, the Danish Ministry of Foreign Affairs, or the Government of Greenland. General discussions about bilateral relations without specific mention of these topics will not count.

questions.fine_print:
**Reversal**: If an official visit is announced and then cancelled before April 1, 2025, it will not count as a YES. If the visit occurs and then its purpose is later re-characterized, the initial official statements regarding its purpose will be used for resolution.
**Subordinate Action**: A visit by any U.S. official other than the officially designated U.S. Secretary of State (including Deputy Secretary of State, Under Secretary, or Special Envoy) will not count.
**Source Unavailability**: If the primary official sources (state.gov, whitehouse.gov, um.dk, naalakkersuisut.gl) are temporarily unavailable, resolution will be delayed until they become available. If they remain permanently unavailable, resolution will rely on corroborating reports from at least three of the specified major reputable news outlets (Reuters, AP, New York Times, Washington Post).
**Government Shutdown**: A U.S. government shutdown that delays official publications will delay resolution until official information becomes available. The resolution date will be extended accordingly.
**Different Legal Form**: The visit must be an "official visit" as defined above. Other forms of engagement (e.g., virtual meetings, phone calls, attendance at multilateral conferences not hosted in Greenland or Denmark, private visits) will not count.

questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of January 2, 2025, Donald Trump is the President of the United States, having won the 2024 presidential election. The 'new US administration' refers to his second term. Greenland is a self-governing territory within the Kingdom of Denmark, with Denmark retaining control over foreign affairs, defense, and monetary policy. The Arctic region, including Greenland, is of increasing strategic importance due to renewed geopolitical competition, particularly from Russia and China, and the potential for new shipping routes and access to critical natural resources. The US maintains a military presence in Greenland at Pituffik Space Base (formerly Thule Air Base), a key installation for ballistic missile defense and Arctic surveillance, established under a 1951 defense agreement with Denmark.

The US has a long-standing strategic interest in Greenland, with historical attempts to acquire the territory, including discussions after the Alaska purchase in 1867, a proposed territorial trade in 1910 (rejected), and a formal offer of $100 million in gold by the Truman administration in 1946 (rejected by Denmark). During his first term in 2019, President Trump publicly expressed interest in purchasing Greenland, which was firmly rejected by both Denmark and Greenland. This renewed interest significantly heightened international attention on Greenland's strategic significance.

Greenland has been on a path toward greater self-determination since gaining home rule in 1979 and the right to declare full independence via a referendum in 2009. All major Greenlandic political parties express a desire for eventual independence, though they differ on the timeline and method. In 2024, the US Department of Defense released an Arctic Strategy emphasizing the need for enhanced domain awareness, communications, intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance capabilities in the region, and increased cooperation with allies.

Key stakeholders include the United States (Trump Administration), driven by national security interests, Arctic geopolitical competition, and access to critical minerals. Denmark, as the sovereign power, seeks to maintain the unity of the Kingdom, strengthen Arctic defense with allies, and respects Greenland's right to self-determination, but firmly rejects any sale or annexation. Greenland's government (Naalakkersuisut) and parliament (Inatsisartut) seek increased autonomy and eventual independence, are open to economic cooperation, but not interested in being purchased or annexed. Russia and China are seen as competitors in the Arctic, and NATO Allies generally support Danish sovereignty.

The regulatory/legal framework includes the Greenland Self-Governance Act (2009), which grants extensive autonomy and the right to self-determination, and the Kingdom of Denmark Constitution. The 1951 Defense of Greenland Agreement (and updates) governs the US military presence and acknowledges Danish sovereignty.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include official statements and press releases from the US White House, Department of State, and Department of Defense; official statements and press releases from the Danish Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Ministry of Defence; official statements and press releases from the Government of Greenland (Naalakkersuisut); reports and analyses from think tanks specializing in Arctic affairs, foreign policy, and national security (e.g., Arctic Institute, Wilson Center, Council on Foreign Relations); and news reporting from reputable international media outlets (e.g., Reuters, AP, BBC, New York Times, Politico). These sources are generally accessible via their official websites and major news archives.

Official statements are released as events warrant. Arctic strategies or policy reviews from the US or Denmark are typically published periodically (e.g., the US DOD Arctic Strategy was updated in 2024). Greenlandic parliamentary elections occur periodically (e.g., one was expected in March 2025, which would influence their government's stance).

New information is expected to become available through White House and State Department briefings and press conferences, joint communiques following diplomatic meetings between US, Danish, and Greenlandic officials, statements from the Danish and Greenlandic governments, international forums and conferences on Arctic affairs, and major news outlets covering foreign policy and Arctic developments.

## Recent Numbers and Events
This is an action selection question, so 'current numbers' are not directly applicable. In prior periods, past US attempts to purchase Greenland (1867, 1910, 1946, 2019) have all been rejected by Denmark and/or Greenland. The US has successfully maintained and updated its defense agreement for Pituffik Space Base without acquiring sovereignty. The US has engaged in diplomatic initiatives and strategic cooperation in the Arctic, including the 2024 DOD Arctic Strategy, without making a formal offer to purchase Greenland or proposing a major strategic partnership tied to resource or basing rights that would infringe on sovereignty.

The base rate for the US successfully purchasing Greenland is zero. The base rate for the US engaging in diplomatic initiatives and reaffirming strategic interest in the Arctic is high, as this is a consistent aspect of US foreign policy. The base rate for the US proposing strategic partnerships related to defense and resources is also relatively high, as this aligns with current geopolitical competition.

## Trends
Factors that make a 'bold move' (e.g., A. Make a formal offer to purchase Greenland or B. Propose a major strategic partnership tied to resource or basing rights) more likely include: the renewed Trump Administration's demonstrated interest in acquiring Greenland and willingness to pursue unconventional foreign policy actions; intensified geopolitical competition from Russia and China in the Arctic; high demand for Greenland's critical mineral resources; and perceived US security gaps in the Arctic that could prompt a push for stronger basing rights.

Factors that make a 'traditional/diplomatic approach' (e.g., C. Launch a high-level diplomatic initiative on Arctic security without major proposals, D. Publicly reaffirm US strategic interest without new proposals, E. Take no significant new action, or F. Take no action) more likely include: strong and consistent Danish and Greenlandic opposition to any sale or annexation; the importance of maintaining allied unity within NATO; the US 2024 Arctic Strategy's emphasis on cooperation; Greenland's economic dependence on Denmark; and the legal and international norms of sovereignty that would be violated by an aggressive acquisition.

questions.resolution_criteria:
General Definitions:
*   **US administration**: Refers to the Executive Branch of the United States government, including the President, Vice President, Executive Office of the President, and Cabinet-level departments (e.g., Department of State, Department of Defense). Actions must be publicly attributed to the US government or its authorized representatives.
*   **Greenland**: Refers to the self-governing territory within the Kingdom of Denmark.
*   **Publicly announced/initiated**: An action is considered publicly announced or initiated if it is reported by at least two independent, reputable international news organizations (e.g., Reuters, Associated Press, New York Times, BBC, Wall Street Journal, Politico) citing official sources, or if it is directly published on an official US government website (e.g., White House, Department of State, Department of Defense), or an official Danish or Greenlandic government website.
*   **Before April 1, 2025**: The action must be publicly announced or formally initiated by 23:59 ET on March 31, 2025.

Resolution Order: The question resolves to the first option in the following ordered list for which its criteria are met. If multiple options' criteria are met, the one appearing earliest in this list takes precedence.

A. Make a formal offer to purchase Greenland
*   Resolves as Option A if, before April 1, 2025, the US administration publicly announces a formal offer to the government of Denmark or Greenland to transfer sovereignty over Greenland to the United States in exchange for monetary compensation or other specified consideration. This must be an explicit offer for purchase or territorial exchange, not merely an expression of interest or a general discussion about Greenland's future.

B. Propose a major strategic partnership tied to resource or basing rights
*   Resolves as Option B if, before April 1, 2025, the US administration publicly proposes to the government of Denmark or Greenland a new, major strategic partnership that explicitly includes:
    *   Significantly expanded US access to Greenlandic natural resources (e.g., critical minerals, rare earths) through new agreements or concessions, OR
    *   New or significantly expanded US military basing rights, facilities, or operational areas in Greenland beyond the scope of the existing 1951 Defense of Greenland Agreement and its subsequent updates.
*   This proposal must go beyond routine defense cooperation or general economic development initiatives and directly link the partnership to specific resource or basing rights.

C. Launch a high-level diplomatic initiative on Arctic security without major proposals
*   Resolves as Option C if, before April 1, 2025, the US administration publicly launches a new, high-level diplomatic initiative specifically focused on Arctic security involving the US Secretary of State, Secretary of Defense, or the President, with their Danish and/or Greenlandic counterparts. This initiative must involve new formal meetings, negotiations, or a structured dialogue.
*   This option resolves only if the conditions for Option A or B are *not* met. The initiative must *not* include a formal offer to purchase Greenland or a proposal for a major strategic partnership tied to resource or basing rights.

D. Publicly reaffirm US strategic interest without new proposals
*   Resolves as Option D if, before April 1, 2025, the US administration (President, Vice President, Secretary of State, or Secretary of Defense) issues a public statement explicitly reaffirming the US's strategic interest in Greenland or the broader Arctic region.
*   This option resolves only if the conditions for Option A, B, or C are *not* met. The reaffirmation must *not* be accompanied by any of the actions described in Options A, B, or C. It would be a general statement of policy or intent, without initiating new formal processes or specific offers.

E. Take no significant new action
*   Resolves as Option E if, before April 1, 2025, none of the conditions for Options A, B, C, or D are met. This includes situations where there might be routine diplomatic engagements, general statements about Arctic cooperation, or administrative updates to existing agreements that do not introduce new policy directions or significant changes regarding Greenland.

F. Take no action
*   Resolves as Option F if, before April 1, 2025, there are no public statements, diplomatic meetings, or policy announcements by the US administration (President, Vice President, Secretary of State, Secretary of Defense, or their direct spokespersons) specifically concerning Greenland's status, resources, or US basing rights, beyond routine administrative updates to existing agreements (e.g., maintenance contracts for Pituffik Space Base).
*   This option resolves only if the conditions for Options A, B, C, D, or E are *not* met.

questions.fine_print:
*   **Partial Actions**: If an action does not fully meet the defined criteria for an option, that option's criteria are not met. The question will then proceed to evaluate the next lower-ranked option in the resolution order.
*   **Reversed Actions**: The resolution is based on an action being publicly announced or formally initiated within the specified timeframe (before April 1, 2025). Any reversal or change in policy *after* March 31, 2025, will not affect the resolution. If an action is announced and then reversed *within* the resolution period, the most significant action that was publicly announced and initiated will be considered for resolution. If the reversal completely negates the initial action such that it never truly "took place" (e.g., an offer is immediately withdrawn before it can be formally considered), then the initial action's criteria may not be considered met.
*   **Actions by Subordinates**: Actions by the Secretary of State, Secretary of Defense, or other high-ranking officials with explicit or implicit authority to act on behalf of the administration in foreign policy matters will count. Actions by lower-level officials or non-governmental entities will not count unless explicitly endorsed or adopted by the US administration.
*   **Source Unavailability**: If a primary resolution source is temporarily unavailable, attempts will be made to access archived versions (e.g., via the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine) or to rely on corroborating reports from at least two independent, reputable international news organizations (as listed above) that cite official sources. If the information remains unavailable or ambiguous after reasonable efforts, the question will be resolved by Metaculus administrators based on the best available evidence.
*   **Government Shutdown**: In the event of a government shutdown delaying official publications, the resolution will rely on reports from at least two independent, reputable international news organizations (as listed above) that cite official sources or provide credible evidence of the action having been taken within the specified timeframe. If no such credible reports emerge, the action will not be considered to have met the criteria.
*   **Different Legal Forms**: The resolution criteria focus on the *substance* and *public announcement* of the action, rather than its specific legal form (e.g., treaty, executive agreement, memorandum of understanding). As long as the substance of the action aligns with the definitions provided for each option and is publicly announced or formally initiated by the US administration, it will count, regardless of the precise legal instrument used.
questions.base_rate_reasoning:
The following base rates reflect the approximate historical frequency of such actions being the *primary* policy action within a similar short timeframe, considering the hierarchy of options:

*   **A. Make a formal offer to purchase Greenland**: 0.01 (Historically, only one formal offer was made by the US in 1946, which was rejected. While President Trump expressed interest in 2019, it did not escalate to a formal offer.)
*   **B. Propose a major strategic partnership tied to resource or basing rights**: 0.05 (The US maintains defense agreements and engages in Arctic cooperation, but a *new, major proposal* specifically for expanded resource or basing rights beyond existing frameworks is a significant and less common step.)
*   **C. Launch a high-level diplomatic initiative on Arctic security without major proposals**: 0.30 (High-level diplomatic engagement on security issues is a regular feature of US foreign policy, aligning with the 2024 DOD Arctic Strategy.)
*   **D. Publicly reaffirm US strategic interest without new proposals**: 0.40 (Public statements reaffirming strategic interests are a very common and routine aspect of US foreign policy.)
*   **E. Take no significant new action**: 0.20 (This implies routine engagement but nothing that qualifies as a more significant action (A, B, C, or D). This is a plausible outcome if the administration is focused elsewhere or opts for a very low-key approach.)
*   **F. Take no action**: 0.04 (Complete absence of public engagement on a strategically important region is highly unlikely, but distinct from routine, non-significant actions.)

questions.title: Will the US Secretary of State or National Security Advisor visit Panama before April 1, 2025, to formally discuss the Canal?
questions.background:
In late 2024, following his election victory, President Trump made public statements suggesting the U.S. should reclaim control of the Panama Canal, which was transferred to Panama in 1999 under the Torrijos-Carter Treaties. Trump cited concerns over alleged Chinese influence and unfair transit fees for U.S. vessels. The Panamanian government, led by President José Raúl Mulino, immediately and firmly rejected these assertions, with the foreign minister calling Panamanian sovereignty over the canal "nonnegotiable".

As of early January 2025, the diplomatic situation is tense. The Trump administration has not yet clarified whether the President's remarks represent a forthcoming policy shift or were simply rhetorical. A key indicator of the administration's seriousness would be a high-level diplomatic visit to Panama to formally open discussions on the topic. Such a visit by the Secretary of State or National Security Advisor would signal a significant prioritization of the issue, while their absence might suggest the administration is letting the controversy subside. It remains to be seen whether diplomatic action will follow the president's strong words.
questions.question_text: Will the US Secretary of State or National Security Advisor visit Panama before April 1, 2025, to formally discuss the Panama Canal treaty or operations?
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of early January 2025, diplomatic tensions exist between the United States and Panama following recent public statements by U.S. President-elect Donald J. Trump. Trump has suggested the U.S. should reclaim control of the Panama Canal, citing concerns over alleged Chinese influence and unfair transit fees for U.S. vessels. He indicated that if Panama does not make changes, the U.S. would demand the canal's return. The Panamanian government has vehemently rejected these assertions. Panama's foreign minister, Javier Martínez-Acha, publicly declared that Panamanian sovereignty over the canal is "nonnegotiable" and affirmed, "The canal belongs to the Panamanians and it will continue to be that way." The Panama Canal was constructed by the United States in the early 20th century. Control of the canal was formally transferred from the U.S. to Panama on December 31, 1999, under the Torrijos-Carter Treaties. These treaties were signed in 1977 by U.S. President Jimmy Carter and Panamanian leader General Omar Torrijos, and subsequently ratified by the U.S. Senate in 1978. The treaties established the permanent neutrality of the Canal, ensuring its availability for peaceful transit by vessels of all nations, and granted the U.S. the permanent right to defend this neutrality. President-elect Trump has previously criticized the transfer, calling it "a terrible mistake" and a "foolish gift." His recent statements in late 2024/early 2025 have brought this long-settled issue back into public discourse. Key stakeholders include the incoming Trump Administration (President-elect Trump, Secretary of State, National Security Advisor), the Government of Panama (President José Raúl Mulino, Foreign Minister Javier Martínez-Acha, Panama Canal Authority), China (due to allegations of influence), and the global shipping industry. The regulatory framework is primarily the Torrijos-Carter Treaties of 1977, which govern the Canal's transfer, neutrality, and U.S. defense rights.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include official statements and press releases from the U.S. Department of State, the National Security Council, the White House, the Panamanian Ministry of Foreign Affairs, and the Presidency of Panama. These would confirm any visits, itineraries, and formal discussion topics. Transcripts of press briefings from the White House and U.S. State Department are also relevant. Reputable international news outlets (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, The New York Times, The Washington Post, CNN) would report on high-level diplomatic visits and their stated purposes. These sources can be accessed via official government websites (e.g., state.gov, whitehouse.gov, presidencia.gob.pa, mire.gob.pa) and archives of news wire services and major media organizations. Official statements and press releases are typically published as events unfold or shortly thereafter; there is no fixed schedule for diplomatic visits or policy announcements. New information would primarily become available on the official websites and social media channels of the U.S. Department of State, the White House, the Panamanian Ministry of Foreign Affairs, and through reporting from major international news agencies.

## Recent Numbers and Events
This is a binary question regarding the occurrence of an event, so a 'current number' is not applicable. The U.S. relinquished control of the Panama Canal to Panama on December 31, 1999. Since this transfer, there have been no formal discussions between the U.S. and Panama concerning the U.S. reclaiming control of the Canal. As of 2025-01-02, there are no public projections or forecasts from official sources regarding a high-level U.S. visit to Panama specifically to discuss the Canal. The current situation is a recent development stemming from President-elect Trump's statements. The Panamanian government's immediate and strong rejection of any negotiation on sovereignty suggests a significant obstacle to such formal discussions.

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution more likely (i.e., a visit occurs): Sustained pressure from the incoming U.S. administration, particularly if President-elect Trump continues to emphasize the desire for changes regarding the Canal's control or operations. Escalation of perceived Chinese influence, should the U.S. administration present new concerns about a national security threat. A desire for formal policy clarification by the U.S. administration. Panamanian willingness to engage on broader bilateral issues, even if to reiterate its stance on the Canal. Factors that make a NO resolution more likely (i.e., no visit occurs): Panama's unwavering stance on sovereignty, as its government has firmly rejected any negotiations. Prioritization of other pressing foreign policy matters by the incoming U.S. administration. The possibility that President-elect Trump's statements are primarily rhetorical, not necessarily leading to immediate high-level diplomatic action. The risk of significant diplomatic backlash and damage to U.S.-Panama relations from initiating such discussions. A lack of a clear or viable diplomatic path for formal discussions on altering the Canal's control.

## Sources
- https://www.nytimes.com/2025/01/02/us/politics/carter-panama-canal.html
questions.resolution_criteria:
This question will resolve YES if, between January 2, 2025, and March 31, 2025, inclusive, either the U.S. Secretary of State or the U.S. National Security Advisor makes an official visit to Panama, and during this visit, they formally discuss the Panama Canal treaty or operations with a representative of the Panamanian government. Otherwise, the question will resolve NO.

Definitions:
U.S. Secretary of State: The individual officially serving as the head of the U.S. Department of State.
U.S. National Security Advisor: The individual officially serving as the chief advisor to the President of the United States on national security issues.
Official visit to Panama: A planned diplomatic trip to Panama by the U.S. Secretary of State or National Security Advisor, publicly announced by the U.S. Department of State or the White House, or by the Panamanian Ministry of Foreign Affairs or Presidency of Panama. The visit must include the physical presence of the Secretary of State or National Security Advisor on Panamanian territory. A brief stopover (e.g., for refueling) without a formal meeting with Panamanian government officials will not constitute an "official visit."
Formally discuss: The Panama Canal treaty or operations must be explicitly mentioned as a topic of discussion in official readouts, press conferences, joint statements, or other public communications from the U.S. Department of State, the White House, the Panamanian Ministry of Foreign Affairs, or the Presidency of Panama, pertaining to the visit. General discussions about bilateral relations or regional security without specific mention of the Canal's treaty or operations will not count.
Representative of the Panamanian government: This includes the President of Panama, Vice President, Minister of Foreign Affairs, or any other cabinet-level minister or senior official explicitly designated by the Panamanian government to engage in such discussions.
