questions.resolution_criteria: This question will resolve to the first and most aggressive option that occurs by April 30, 2025, 11:59 PM ET. The options are ordered from most aggressive (B) to least aggressive (F) for resolution purposes. Definitions: Trump administration: Refers to actions or official statements made by Donald Trump as President, the Vice President, the Secretary of State, the Secretary of Defense, or other cabinet-level officials acting in their official capacity, or official documents issued by the Executive Office of the President, the Department of State, or the Department of Defense. NATO allies: Refers to the member states of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, excluding the United States. Formally Propose: An official communication from the Trump administration to NATO or its member states, explicitly stating a new defense spending target (e.g., 3% of GDP, 4% of GDP, 5% of GDP) as a formal U.S. policy objective. This must be more than a public statement or rhetorical flourish; it must be a diplomatic communication (e.g., diplomatic note, formal letter to NATO Secretary General or member states) or a public policy announcement intended to initiate a formal process within NATO for adopting a new target. Publicly Threaten: A clear, explicit statement made by a senior Trump administration official (President, Vice President, Secretary of State, Secretary of Defense) in a public forum (e.g., press conference, official speech, social media post from an official account, official press release) indicating that the U.S. will or may reduce its military presence, capabilities, or security guarantees for specific NATO allied countries if they do not increase defense spending. This must name or clearly imply specific countries or a defined group of countries (e.g., "allies not meeting the 2% target," "Germany and other low-spending nations"). Force Reductions: A decrease in the number of U.S. military personnel, equipment, or operational commitments stationed in or dedicated to the defense of specific NATO allied countries. This refers to the threat of such reductions, not necessarily their implementation. Initiate a Formal Review of U.S. NATO Commitments: The issuance of an official directive (e.g., Executive Order, Presidential Memorandum, Department of Defense directive, Department of State directive) by the Trump administration that formally mandates a comprehensive review of the U.S.'s treaty obligations, military presence, or financial contributions related to NATO, with the stated purpose of re-evaluating or potentially altering these commitments based on allied defense spending. This must be an internal U.S. government action, not merely a public statement about considering such a review. Increase Diplomatic Pressure Only: Actions by the Trump administration that involve high-level diplomatic engagements (e.g., bilateral meetings, phone calls, public statements by senior officials) with NATO allies or the NATO organization, specifically urging or demanding increased defense spending, without formally proposing a new target (Option A), publicly threatening force reductions (Option B), or initiating a formal review of U.S. commitments (Option C). This includes strong rhetoric and calls for higher spending, but stops short of the more aggressive, formal actions defined in A, B, and C. Significant new action: An action that falls under options A, B, C, or D. Resolution Order: 1. B. Publicly Threaten Force Reductions from specific allied countries: Resolves to B if the Trump administration publicly threatens force reductions from specific NATO allied countries due to their defense spending by the deadline. 2. C. Initiate a Formal Review of U.S. NATO Commitments: Resolves to C if the Trump administration initiates a formal review of U.S. NATO commitments by the deadline, and option B has not occurred. 3. A. Formally Propose a New Target >2% of GDP: Resolves to A if the Trump administration formally proposes a new NATO defense spending target greater than 2% of GDP by the deadline, and options B or C have not occurred. 4. D. Increase Diplomatic Pressure Only (no new formal action or threat): Resolves to D if the Trump administration increases diplomatic pressure on NATO allies regarding defense spending by the deadline, and options A, B, or C have not occurred. 5. E. Take no significant new action: Resolves to E if no action meeting the criteria for options A, B, C, or D occurs by the deadline. 6. F. Take no action: This option is functionally identical to E and will only resolve if no action meeting the criteria for options A, B, C, or D occurs by the deadline. If E is chosen, F is not possible.
questions.resolution_source: The primary resolution sources will be: Official press releases and statements from the White House (whitehouse.gov), the U.S. Department of State (state.gov), and the U.S. Department of Defense (defense.gov). The Federal Register (federalregister.gov) for official directives (e.g., Executive Orders, Presidential Memoranda). Official press releases, statements, and documents from the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (nato.int). Major reputable news agencies (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, The New York Times, The Washington Post) will be used for corroboration and in cases where primary sources are delayed or unavailable.
questions.fine_print: Partial Action: The resolution criteria are designed to require explicit and formal actions or statements. Vague or informal discussions will not be sufficient to trigger options A, B, or C. For option D, there must be clear evidence of increased diplomatic pressure beyond routine engagement. Reversed Action: If an action meeting the criteria for any option occurs by the deadline, the question will resolve to that option, regardless of whether the action is subsequently reversed or modified after April 30, 2025. Action by Subordinate: Actions taken or statements made by cabinet-level officials (e.g., Secretary of State, Secretary of Defense) acting in their official capacity will count as actions by the "Trump administration." Source Unavailability: If primary resolution sources are temporarily unavailable, major reputable news agencies (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters) will be used to determine the outcome. If no conclusive evidence can be found from any reliable source by the resolution date, the question will resolve to the least aggressive option for which no contradictory evidence exists (likely E or F). Government Shutdown: A government shutdown may delay the official publication of documents. However, if an action is widely reported by major news agencies and attributed to the Trump administration, it will be considered for resolution, with official sources used for confirmation once available. Different Legal Form: The substance of the action, as defined in the options, will take precedence over the specific legal instrument used. For example, a "formal review" could be initiated by an Executive Order, a Presidential Memorandum, or a Departmental directive.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: Based on the strict definitions provided for each option and research into the Trump administration's first term (2017-2021): A. Formally Propose a New Target >2% of GDP: Low historical precedent (0/1). While President Trump frequently called for higher spending (e.g., 4% of GDP), there is no clear evidence of a formal proposal to NATO for a new official target that was formally adopted or considered by the alliance during his first term. B. Publicly Threaten Force Reductions from specific allied countries: Low historical precedent (0/1). While President Trump made general threats about not defending allies who don't meet spending obligations and considered troop movements (e.g., from Germany), there is no clear evidence of a public, explicit threat of force reductions from specific allied countries directly tied to their spending levels in a formal statement. C. Initiate a Formal Review of U.S. NATO Commitments: Low historical precedent (0/1). While internal discussions and reviews regarding NATO policy likely occurred, there is no clear evidence of a publicly announced, formal directive (e.g., Executive Order) specifically mandating a comprehensive review of all U.S. NATO commitments with the explicit purpose of re-evaluating or altering them based on allied spending during his first term. D. Increase Diplomatic Pressure Only: High historical precedent (1/1). The Trump administration consistently used public statements, high-level meetings, and diplomatic channels to pressure NATO allies on defense spending throughout his first term. The base rate estimate of 0.25 reflects the likelihood of one of the more aggressive actions (A, B, C) occurring, given that D is highly likely and the question resolves to the 'most aggressive' first.

questions.title: Will the White House announce a Trump-Zelenskyy meeting at the White House before April 1, 2025?
questions.background:
Since President Trump's inauguration, his administration has made ending the war in Ukraine a top foreign policy priority. A special envoy, Steve Witkoff, has been central to shuttle diplomacy between Kyiv and Moscow, working to broker a peace deal. These negotiations have seen a U.S.-backed peace proposal revised after initial talks with Ukrainian and European allies.

While progress has been reported, with prisoner swaps and ongoing discussions, a definitive agreement remains elusive. Ukrainian officials have suggested a meeting between President Zelenskyy and President Trump could be the final step to solidify a deal, but President Trump has publicly stated "we should get a deal done first." An official announcement of a summit at the White House would signal a significant breakthrough in the peace process.
questions.question_text: Will the White House officially announce a meeting between President Trump and President Zelenskyy to be held at the White House before April 1, 2025?
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of January 15, 2025, Donald Trump is not the President of the United States. The U.S. Presidential inauguration is scheduled for January 20, 2025. Therefore, on this date, the incumbent President is still in office, and any incoming President (including Trump, if he won the 2024 election) has not yet been inaugurated. Consequently, there is no 'Trump administration' in power as of January 15, 2025, to make foreign policy a priority or appoint special envoys. The war in Ukraine is ongoing. Historically, Donald Trump, as a candidate and former president, has expressed a desire to quickly end the conflict in Ukraine and has at times been critical of the extent of U.S. aid. However, the premise that 'Since President Trump's inauguration, his administration has made ending the war in Ukraine a top foreign policy priority' and that 'A special envoy, Steve Witkoff, has been central to shuttle diplomacy between Kyiv and Moscow' is factually incorrect for January 15, 2025. Steve Witkoff is a real estate developer and associate of Donald Trump, but there is no public record or credible news report as of this date indicating he holds a formal role as a special envoy for Ukraine peace for any U.S. administration, current or incoming. Key stakeholders would include the President-elect (Donald Trump, if he won the 2024 election), Volodymyr Zelenskyy, the White House (post-inauguration), the Ukrainian government, and the Russian government. The regulatory framework for announcing such a meeting falls under the President's executive authority in foreign policy.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources for an official announcement would include official White House press releases, statements from the White House Press Secretary, direct remarks from the President or Secretary of State, and official communications from the Office of the President of Ukraine. Major reputable news organizations (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, The New York Times, The Washington Post, CNN, Fox News) would widely report any such announcement. White House announcements regarding high-level meetings are made on an ad-hoc basis, typically once details are finalized or a strategic decision to announce is made; there is no fixed publication schedule. New information would primarily become available through official White House channels and subsequently through major news media outlets.

## Recent Numbers and Events
This is a binary (yes/no) question, so there are no current numbers or outcomes from prior periods to report in this context. As of January 15, 2025, any projections or forecasts regarding a Trump-Zelenskyy meeting at the White House before April 1, 2025, would be entirely speculative. Such projections would be contingent on Donald Trump's inauguration on January 20, 2025, and the subsequent foreign policy direction and diplomatic efforts of his administration. There are no relevant base rates for an incoming administration's specific meeting schedule before it has even taken office.

## Trends
Given the significant factual inaccuracies in the question's premise, the following trends are hypothetical and assume a Trump presidency is in effect and the background details were accurate:

Factors that make a YES resolution more likely (Hypothetical):
*   President Trump's stated desire to quickly end the war in Ukraine could lead him to prioritize a high-profile summit to demonstrate progress.
*   If diplomatic efforts (hypothetically, by a special envoy or other channels) were to achieve significant breakthroughs in peace negotiations, a White House meeting could serve as a capstone event.
*   President Zelenskyy's potential willingness to engage with President Trump to secure a peace deal or continued U.S. support could facilitate such a meeting.
*   A White House summit would carry significant symbolic weight, signaling U.S. leadership in peace efforts and potentially garnering domestic political support for the new administration.

Factors that make a NO resolution more likely (Hypothetical):
*   President Trump's stated condition of 'we should get a deal done first' might mean an announcement would be withheld until substantial agreement on a peace plan is achieved.
*   A lack of concrete progress in peace negotiations or fundamental disagreements between the parties (U.S., Ukraine, Russia) could make a meeting seem premature or unproductive.
*   A new administration might have other immediate foreign policy priorities that could overshadow or delay a Ukraine peace summit.
*   Logistical or security challenges, though typically manageable for such high-level events, could theoretically contribute to delays.
*   Internal disagreements within a new administration regarding the timing or strategic value of such a meeting could prevent an announcement.

## Sources
- White House official channels (hypothetical future source)
- U.S. Department of State (hypothetical future source)
- Major news outlets (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, The New York Times, The Washington Post, CNN, Fox News)
- Office of the President of Ukraine official communications
research_flags: HALLUCINATED_FACTS: The question is based on two significant factual errors as of January 15, 2025: 1) Donald Trump is not the President of the United States on this date; the inauguration is January 20, 2025. Therefore, there is no 'Trump administration' in power to make foreign policy or appoint special envoys. 2) There is no public or credible information indicating that 'Steve Witkoff' serves as a special envoy for a U.S. administration for shuttle diplomacy between Kyiv and Moscow to broker a peace deal in Ukraine.
questions.resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if the official White House website (whitehouse.gov) publishes a press release, statement, or transcript of a public announcement by the President of the United States or the White House Press Secretary, explicitly stating that a meeting between Donald J. Trump (as President of the United States) and Volodymyr Zelenskyy (as President of Ukraine) is scheduled to be held at the White House complex in Washington, D.C., and that this announcement is made on or before March 31, 2025, 11:59 PM ET. The announcement must also specify that the meeting itself is planned to occur on or before March 31, 2025. This question resolves NO otherwise. Definitions: "White House": Refers to the Executive Office of the President of the United States, specifically the official website https://www.whitehouse.gov/. "Officially announce": A public statement made by the President of the United States, the White House Press Secretary, or published on the official White House website (https://www.whitehouse.gov/) in a section such as "Briefings & Statements" or "Press Releases." "President Trump": Donald J. Trump, serving as the duly inaugurated President of the United States. "President Zelenskyy": Volodymyr Zelenskyy, serving as the duly recognized President of Ukraine. "Meeting at the White House": A planned in-person diplomatic engagement between the two presidents, explicitly stated to occur at the White House complex in Washington, D.C. "Before April 1, 2025": The announcement must be made on or before March 31, 2025, 11:59 PM ET. Additionally, the announced meeting itself must be scheduled to take place on or before March 31, 2025.
questions.resolution_source: The official White House website: https://www.whitehouse.gov/ (specifically, the "Briefings & Statements" or "Press Releases" sections).
questions.fine_print: Announcement vs. Meeting: The question resolves based on the announcement of the meeting. If an announcement is made before April 1, 2025, for a meeting to be held before April 1, 2025, it resolves YES, even if the meeting is later canceled or postponed to after April 1, 2025. Timing of Announcement: The announcement must be made on or before March 31, 2025, 11:59 PM ET. Timing of Meeting: The announcement must specify that the meeting is scheduled to be held on or before March 31, 2025. An announcement made before April 1, 2025, for a meeting scheduled after March 31, 2025, would resolve NO. Location: The announcement must explicitly state the meeting will be held "at the White House" (referring to the White House complex in Washington, D.C.). Meetings held elsewhere (e.g., Mar-a-Lago, Camp David, or during an international summit abroad) will not count. Acting Officials: An announcement made by an acting White House Press Secretary or other officially designated White House spokesperson will count. Source Unavailability: If https://www.whitehouse.gov/ is temporarily unavailable, resolution will be based on confirmation from at least two independent, major reputable news organizations (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, The New York Times, The Washington Post, CNN, Fox News) that explicitly report an official White House announcement, citing the White House as the source. If the website remains unavailable until the resolution date, and no such confirmed reports exist, it resolves NO. Government Shutdown: A government shutdown that affects the publication of official White House statements will be treated as temporary unavailability. Resolution will follow the "Source Unavailability" clause.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: Historically, it is relatively uncommon for a newly inaugurated U.S. President to officially announce a bilateral White House meeting with a specific foreign head of state within the first ~70 days of their first term (January 20 to April 1). While Presidents often engage in phone calls or attend multilateral summits early in their tenure, formal bilateral White House visits typically require more logistical planning and often occur later in the first year. The specific pairing of a U.S. President (hypothetically Trump) and Ukrainian President Zelenskyy for such an early, high-profile meeting at the White House, particularly given the ongoing conflict and the U.S. President's stated conditions for a "deal first," makes this a low-probability event. Historical data for such a narrow window is not readily available in a consolidated format, but general patterns suggest such early, specific announcements are rare.

questions.title: By March 15, 2025, what will be the primary US approach to Ukraine-Russia peace talks?
questions.background: The Trump administration's effort to mediate a peace deal between Ukraine and Russia is at a critical juncture. Special Envoy Steve Witkoff continues to engage with both parties, but the ultimate direction and intensity of U.S. involvement remain subject to debate. Key decision-makers are weighing whether to increase direct presidential involvement, shift focus to a European-led process, or apply different forms of pressure to reach a resolution. The path chosen in the coming weeks will likely determine the trajectory of the entire peace process.
questions.question_text: By March 15, 2025, which of the following will best describe the U.S. administration's primary approach to the Ukraine-Russia peace negotiations?
options: Direct Presidential Intervention: President Trump personally hosts a summit with leaders from Ukraine and/or Russia or travels to meet with them.
Appoint a new high-profile envoy: The administration appoints a figure with cabinet-level or higher prestige to take over or co-lead negotiations.
Empower European-led Negotiations: The U.S. takes a secondary role, publicly endorsing a peace framework primarily negotiated by European leaders.
Maintain current approach (Envoy-led talks): The U.S. continues with Special Envoy Steve Witkoff as the primary negotiator without significant escalation.
Take no action/Pause negotiations: The administration formally or informally pauses high-level diplomatic engagement regarding the peace plan.
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of January 15, 2025, the United States, under the Biden administration, continues to provide significant military, financial, and humanitarian aid to Ukraine in its defense against Russia's full-scale invasion. The official US stance has consistently been that any peace negotiations must be led by Ukraine, and that the US will not pressure Ukraine into concessions. Direct, high-level US-Russia peace talks specifically on a comprehensive Ukraine peace deal are not actively ongoing or publicly announced as of this date. Diplomatic efforts are more focused on supporting Ukraine and maintaining international pressure on Russia. The question's mention of 'Special Envoy Steve Witkoff' is a hallucinated fact; there is no publicly known 'Special Envoy Steve Witkoff' for Ukraine-Russia peace talks under the Biden administration. The primary US diplomatic efforts are typically led by the Secretary of State, National Security Advisor, and other State Department officials. The historical context includes Russia's full-scale invasion in February 2022, followed by consistent US support for Ukraine's sovereignty and territorial integrity, and endorsement of Ukraine's 'peace formula' (a 10-point plan proposed by President Zelenskyy) as the basis for any future peace settlement. The US has been a key player in coordinating international support for Ukraine through formats like the Ukraine Defense Contact Group. Key stakeholders include the United States (Biden Administration: President Joe Biden, Secretary of State Antony Blinken, National Security Advisor Jake Sullivan), Ukraine (President Volodymyr Zelenskyy), Russia (President Vladimir Putin), European Allies (EU, NATO members), and international organizations (UN, OSCE). There is no specific US regulatory/legal framework governing these peace talks; US foreign policy is conducted under presidential authority, guided by international law and domestic legislation. The UN Charter and international law form the broader legal backdrop for the conflict.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include official US Government statements (White House press briefings, State Department press releases, Department of Defense briefings, transcripts of remarks by President Biden, Secretary Blinken, and other senior officials), official Ukrainian Government statements (President Zelenskyy's office, Ukrainian Foreign Ministry), official Russian Government statements (Kremlin, Russian Foreign Ministry), and statements from European Governments and EU/NATO. Reputable news organizations (e.g., Reuters, Associated Press, New York Times, Washington Post, Wall Street Journal, BBC, Financial Times) and analyses from think tanks and policy institutes (e.g., Council on Foreign Relations, Atlantic Council, CSIS) are also relevant. Data can be accessed via official government websites (whitehouse.gov, state.gov, defense.gov, president.gov.ua, mid.ru), news archives, and think tank websites. Official statements and press conferences occur regularly, often daily or weekly. New information is expected to become available through White House and State Department press briefings, joint statements following high-level meetings, and international summits.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As an action selection question, numerical data is not directly applicable. However, recent events related to diplomatic efforts are crucial. As of January 15, 2025, the US has continued to announce and deliver military and financial aid packages to Ukraine, signaling ongoing commitment to Ukraine's defense rather than an immediate push for peace talks on Russian terms. High-level diplomatic engagements between US officials and European/Ukrainian counterparts are frequent, focusing on coordinating support. There have been no public indications from the Biden administration of a significant change in its approach to Ukraine-Russia peace talks, particularly no move to directly pressure Ukraine or to appoint a new high-profile envoy specifically for this purpose beyond existing diplomatic structures. The US continues to emphasize that Ukraine must determine the timing and terms of any negotiations. Ukraine continues to advocate for its 10-point peace formula. Expert consensus as of early 2025 suggests a major shift in the US approach is unlikely in the immediate term (before March 15, 2025), given the ongoing conflict and the Biden administration's consistent policy. The 'maintain current approach' (Option D) has been the most consistent US policy regarding the initiation of peace talks since the full-scale invasion.

## Trends
Factors that make a shift towards more direct US involvement or a new approach (Options A, B, C, E) more likely include: a significant battlefield stalemate or reversal, increased pressure from allies for a more direct US role, growing domestic political pressure in the US, a credible signal from either Ukraine or Russia genuinely open to substantive negotiations, or a specific, credible peace proposal gaining international traction. Factors that make maintaining the current approach (Option D) or a secondary role (Option C) more likely include: continued Ukrainian resistance and resolve, lack of credible Russian willingness to negotiate on acceptable terms, continued strong unity among NATO and EU allies in supporting Ukraine, the primary focus remaining on military support for Ukraine, and the Biden administration's consistent policy of 'nothing about Ukraine without Ukraine'. If European leaders successfully develop and advance a peace framework, the US might naturally take a secondary, supportive role (Option C).

## Sources

- https://www.defense.gov/
- https://www.president.gov.ua/
- https://mid.ru/

- https://www.washingtonpost.com/
- https://www.wsj.com/
- https://www.bbc.com/
- https://www.ft.com/
- https://www.cfr.org/

- https://www.csis.org/
- https://www.crisisgroup.org/
research_flags: HALLUCINATED_FACTS: The question's background explicitly mentions the 'Trump administration' and 'President Trump' as being in office and leading peace efforts as of January 15, 2025. This is incorrect. As of January 15, 2025, Joe Biden is the President of the United States. Additionally, the 'Special Envoy Steve Witkoff' mentioned in the background is not a known official under the current administration for Ukraine-Russia peace talks.
questions.resolution_criteria: This question will resolve based on public statements and actions by the U.S. administration, specifically President Trump, the Secretary of State, the National Security Advisor, or their explicitly designated representatives, concerning Ukraine-Russia peace negotiations, as reported by official U.S. government sources and major reputable news organizations by 11:59 PM ET on March 15, 2025.The question will resolve as follows:A. Direct Presidential Intervention: Resolves as Option A if, by March 15, 2025, President Trump personally either:1.  Hosts a summit: Publicly hosts a meeting in the United States or a third country with the President or Foreign Minister of Ukraine and/or the President or Foreign Minister of Russia, where the stated primary purpose of the meeting is to negotiate a peace deal between Ukraine and Russia.2.  Travels to meet: Publicly travels to Ukraine or Russia to meet with the President or Foreign Minister of that country, where the stated primary purpose of the meeting is to negotiate a peace deal between Ukraine and Russia.    A "summit" or "meeting" must involve direct, in-person engagement by President Trump with the specified foreign officials. Casual encounters, brief sideline discussions at larger international events (e.g., G7, UN General Assembly), or virtual meetings do not qualify.B. Appoint a new high-profile envoy: Resolves as Option B if, by March 15, 2025, the U.S. administration publicly announces the appointment of a new individual to a position specifically created for, or explicitly designated with the primary responsibility of, leading or co-leading Ukraine-Russia peace negotiations, and this individual holds a rank equivalent to or higher than a U.S. Cabinet Secretary (e.g., Vice President, former President, Secretary of State, National Security Advisor, or a newly created position explicitly stated to be of such rank). This must be a new appointment, not an existing official performing their regular duties.C. Empower European-led Negotiations: Resolves as Option C if, by March 15, 2025, the U.S. administration, through a public statement by President Trump, the Secretary of State, or the National Security Advisor, explicitly states that the U.S. is taking a secondary or supportive role in Ukraine-Russia peace negotiations and publicly endorses a specific, named peace framework or proposal that has been primarily developed and negotiated by European leaders or institutions (e.g., the European Union, a specific European country or group of countries). The endorsement must clearly indicate a shift in the primary U.S. diplomatic leadership role towards a European entity.D. Maintain current approach (Envoy-led talks): Resolves as Option D if, by March 15, 2025, none of Options A, B, C, or E are met, AND Special Envoy Steve Witkoff (as named in the question) is publicly identified by the U.S. administration or widely reported by major news organizations as continuing to serve as the primary U.S. negotiator for Ukraine-Russia peace talks. If "Special Envoy Steve Witkoff" is not publicly mentioned or confirmed as the primary negotiator by March 15, 2025, and none of A, B, C, or E are met, this option will still resolve as D, implying the continuation of the existing diplomatic structure and level of engagement as the primary approach.E. Take no action/Pause negotiations: Resolves as Option E if, by March 15, 2025, the U.S. administration, through a public statement by President Trump, the Secretary of State, or the National Security Advisor, formally or informally announces a pause, cessation, or significant reduction in high-level U.S. diplomatic engagement specifically aimed at a Ukraine-Russia peace plan. Alternatively, if there are no publicly reported U.S.-led or U.S.-involved high-level diplomatic engagements (e.g., meetings, calls, public statements on active negotiations) concerning a Ukraine-Russia peace plan for a continuous period of at least two weeks immediately preceding March 15, 2025, and none of Options A, B, C, or D are met, this option will resolve as E.
questions.resolution_source: Official statements and press releases from the White House (whitehouse.gov). Official statements and press releases from the U.S. Department of State (state.gov). Reporting from major, reputable international news agencies, including Reuters (reuters.com) and Associated Press (apnews.com).
questions.fine_print: Partial Action: If an action is taken that partially aligns with an option but does not fully meet the specified criteria (e.g., a meeting occurs but is not explicitly for "peace talks"), it will not be considered a fulfillment of that option. The criteria must be met precisely.Action Reversed: If an action meeting an option's criteria is taken and then subsequently reversed before March 15, 2025, the question will resolve based on the final state of affairs as of 11:59 PM ET on March 15, 2025. If the action is reversed after March 15, 2025, the question resolves based on the action having occurred.Action by Subordinate: Actions must be directly attributable to President Trump, the Secretary of State, the National Security Advisor, or an individual explicitly designated by them for the specific purpose of the action described in the options. Actions by lower-level officials or general diplomatic staff performing routine duties will not qualify unless explicitly stated to be under the direct instruction and public endorsement of the aforementioned high-level officials.Resolution Source Unavailable: If the primary resolution sources (whitehouse.gov, state.gov, reuters.com, apnews.com) are temporarily unavailable on the resolution date, the resolution will be delayed until they become available or until sufficient information can be gathered from alternative reputable news sources (e.g., New York Times, Washington Post, Wall Street Journal, BBC, Financial Times) to make an unambiguous determination.Government Shutdown: A U.S. government shutdown that impacts the publication of official statements will be treated similarly to a temporary unavailability of sources. Resolution will be delayed or based on alternative reputable news sources.Different Legal Form: The specific legal or diplomatic form of the action (e.g., executive order, presidential memorandum, joint statement, press conference) is less important than the public communication and substance of the action as described in the resolution criteria.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: Historically, direct, high-level U.S. presidential intervention in ongoing conflicts to personally mediate peace talks (Option A) is relatively rare. While U.S. presidents often engage in diplomacy, personally hosting summits or traveling specifically for peace talks in an active conflict zone, particularly with adversaries, is not a frequent occurrence.Option A (Direct Presidential Intervention): Low probability (e.g., < 0.1). Examples like President Carter's role in the Camp David Accords are notable but exceptional. More often, such efforts are delegated to the Secretary of State or special envoys.Option B (Appoint a new high-profile envoy): Moderate to low probability (e.g., 0.1-0.3). The U.S. has appointed special envoys for various conflicts (e.g., Middle East peace, Afghanistan), but a new cabinet-level or higher envoy specifically for Ukraine-Russia peace talks, distinct from existing roles, would be a significant shift.Option C (Empower European-led Negotiations): Moderate probability (e.g., 0.2-0.4). The U.S. often supports allied diplomatic efforts. A formal, public endorsement of a primarily European-negotiated framework, with the U.S. explicitly taking a secondary role, would be a notable shift but plausible depending on European initiatives.Option D (Maintain current approach): High probability (e.g., 0.5-0.7). Given the inertia of foreign policy and the current stated U.S. approach (as per the background research, even if the question's premise differs), maintaining the existing diplomatic structure and level of engagement is often the most likely outcome in the short term.Option E (Take no action/Pause negotiations): Low to moderate probability (e.g., 0.05-0.2). A formal pause in diplomatic engagement would be a significant policy shift, usually in response to a major event or strategic reassessment.Reasoning: U.S. foreign policy tends to exhibit continuity unless there are compelling reasons for a dramatic shift. Direct presidential intervention is reserved for moments of high strategic importance or perceived breakthrough potential. Appointing a new high-profile envoy or empowering European efforts represents a significant, but less dramatic, shift. A formal pause in negotiations would typically signal a breakdown or a strategic re-evaluation. Therefore, maintaining the current approach is often the most likely short-term outcome. I've chosen 0.55 as a central estimate for Option D, reflecting its higher likelihood compared to the other options, which represent more significant shifts.

questions.title: Will a Continuing Resolution be enacted to fund any part of the US government beyond March 21, 2025?
questions.background: The new Republican-controlled Congress and White House have stated their intention to pass a full budget via the twelve individual appropriations bills, breaking from the recent practice of relying on omnibus packages and stopgap Continuing Resolutions (CRs). However, they face a tight deadline. A CR passed in late 2024 expires on March 14, 2025. As of mid-February, disagreements within the House Republican conference over spending levels and policy riders have slowed the appropriations process. With the deadline less than a month away, it remains to be seen whether leadership can unify the party to pass their preferred appropriations bills or if they will be forced to enact another CR to avoid a government shutdown.
questions.question_text: Will a Continuing Resolution (CR) be enacted to fund any part of the US government beyond March 21, 2025?
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of February 15, 2025, the U.S. federal government is operating under a Continuing Resolution (CR) that funds various parts of the government. This CR is set to expire on March 14, 2025. If a new funding measure (either a full budget or another CR) is not enacted by this date, a government shutdown would occur for the affected agencies. The proto-question specifically asks about funding beyond March 21, 2025, which is one week after the current CR's expiration. The federal fiscal year begins on October 1. For Fiscal Year 2025, Congress did not pass all 12 appropriations bills by the deadline. A CR was enacted in September 2024 to extend funding through December 20, 2024, followed by another CR in December 2024, extending funding through March 14, 2025. This indicates a recent reliance on short-term funding measures. Key stakeholders include the President, the House of Representatives (Speaker, Appropriations Committee, Republican Conference), and the Senate (Majority Leader, Appropriations Committee). The appropriations process is governed by the U.S. Constitution and the Congressional Budget and Impoundment Control Act of 1974. Failure to pass funding results in a government shutdown, furloughing non-essential employees and suspending services.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include Congress.gov for legislative information, the official websites of the House and Senate Appropriations Committees for bill progress and hearings, and the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) for independent analyses. Major news outlets (e.g., The New York Times, The Washington Post, Politico, Wall Street Journal, Associated Press, Reuters) provide ongoing reports on negotiations and political dynamics. White House press briefings and statements offer the administration's stance. All these sources are accessible via their respective official websites. Legislative and committee information is updated continuously as actions occur. CBO reports are published periodically. News outlets provide daily updates. New information will primarily emerge from congressional floor debates and votes, statements from congressional leadership and the White House, and reports from the Appropriations Committees.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of February 15, 2025, the U.S. government is currently funded by a Continuing Resolution (CR) that expires on March 14, 2025. No new full appropriations bills or further CRs extending funding beyond March 14, 2025, have been enacted. In prior periods, Congress passed a CR in September 2024 to fund the government through December 20, 2024, and another CR in December 2024 to fund it through March 14, 2025, both avoiding shutdowns. As of February 15, 2025, specific, publicly available quantifiable projections or forecasts from official bodies regarding the likelihood of a CR beyond March 21, 2025, are not readily available. However, the historical pattern of relying on CRs when facing deadlines and internal disagreements suggests a significant base rate for such an outcome.

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution (a CR enacted beyond March 21, 2025) more likely include: a tight deadline with the current CR expiring on March 14, 2025; internal disagreements within the House Republican Conference over spending levels and policy riders; historical precedent of passing multiple short-term CRs (September 2024, December 2024) to avoid shutdowns; and the inherent complexity and time-consuming nature of passing 12 individual appropriations bills, especially with policy differences. Factors that make a NO resolution (no CR enacted beyond March 21, 2025) more likely include: the stated intention of the Republican-controlled Congress and White House to pass a full budget via individual appropriations bills, aiming to break from reliance on CRs; and political pressure to avoid a government shutdown, which is generally unpopular and can carry negative political consequences.

## Sources
- https://www.congress.gov/
- https://appropriations.house.gov/
- https://www.appropriations.senate.gov/
- https://www.cbo.gov/
- https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/statements-releases/
- https://www.nytimes.com/
- https://www.washingtonpost.com/
- https://www.politico.com/
- https://www.wsj.com/
- https://apnews.com/
- https://www.reuters.com/
questions.resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if a public law designated as a "Continuing Resolution" (or a functionally equivalent joint resolution that extends funding at current levels for a specified period) is enacted by the President of the United States on or before March 21, 2025, and explicitly provides appropriations for any part of the US government with an expiration date later than March 21, 2025, or with no specified expiration date. This question resolves NO otherwise. Definitions: Enacted: A bill or joint resolution becomes law when it is passed by both the U.S. House of Representatives and the U.S. Senate in identical form and is then signed by the President, or if the President allows it to become law without a signature, or if Congress overrides a presidential veto. For the purpose of this question, we are looking for a public law. Continuing Resolution (CR): A type of appropriations legislation that provides temporary funding for federal government agencies and programs when a regular appropriations bill has not been enacted by the start of the fiscal year or by the expiration of a previous funding measure. CRs typically continue funding at the previous year's levels and for a specified period. For further information, see the U.S. Government Accountability Office (GAO) explanation: https://www.gao.gov/blog/what-continuing-resolution-and-how-does-it-impact-government-operations Public Law: A law that applies to the general public. Once a bill or joint resolution is enacted, it is assigned a public law number. Appropriations: The provision of funds, through an annual appropriations act or a continuing resolution, for federal agencies and programs to incur obligations and make payments from the U.S. Treasury.
questions.resolution_source: The primary resolution source will be Congress.gov, specifically the "Public Laws" section (https://www.congress.gov/public-laws). The date of enactment and the text of the public law will be examined to determine if it meets the criteria.
questions.fine_print: Partial Funding: If a Continuing Resolution is enacted that funds only a portion of the US government, it will still resolve YES, as the question asks about "any part of the US government." Reversal or Supersession: If a Continuing Resolution meeting the criteria is enacted by March 21, 2025, the question resolves YES, even if that Continuing Resolution is later superseded, amended, or repealed by subsequent legislation. The key is enactment by the deadline. Different Legal Form: The question specifically refers to a "Continuing Resolution." If funding beyond March 21, 2025, is provided solely through full-year appropriations bills for all agencies, or an omnibus appropriations bill that is not a continuing resolution (i.e., it provides new, detailed appropriations rather than continuing existing levels), the question resolves NO. However, if an omnibus bill includes provisions that function as a continuing resolution for some agencies, it would resolve YES. The determination will be based on the legislative text and common understanding of a "Continuing Resolution" as a temporary funding measure. Source Unavailability or Delay: If Congress.gov is temporarily unavailable or delayed in publishing the public law, the White House Briefing Room for presidential signing statements (https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/statements-releases/) or reliable reports from major news agencies (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, The New York Times, The Washington Post) confirming the enactment date and content of a public law will be used. The date of enactment, not publication, is determinative. Government Shutdown: A government shutdown does not prevent a bill from being enacted into law. The date of enactment by the President is the determining factor, regardless of any delays in government operations or official publication that may result from a shutdown.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: The enactment of Continuing Resolutions to fund the US government is a frequent occurrence. The U.S. federal government has been funded by a CR nearly half the time since 2011. During the Biden administration (prior to October 2025), there were 13 instances in which Congress enacted short-term funding measures. More recently, in Fiscal Year 2025, Congress enacted a CR in September 2024 to extend funding through December 20, 2024, followed by another CR in December 2024, extending funding through March 14, 2025. This historical pattern suggests a significant likelihood of further CRs when facing appropriations deadlines.

questions.title: What legislative strategy will GOP leadership use to address the debt ceiling by April 30, 2025?
questions.background: The statutory debt limit was reinstated in January 2025, and the Treasury Department is currently using 'extraordinary measures' to prevent a default. While the official 'X-date' is likely several months away, pressure is mounting on Congress to act. The Republican majority is internally divided. Some members, backed by the White House, favor attaching a debt ceiling increase to a large, partisan reconciliation bill filled with conservative policy wins. Others prefer a more traditional approach of a standalone bill with specific spending cuts, while the slimmest of House majorities means a bipartisan 'clean' bill may be the only viable path. The strategy chosen will be a major test for the new Congress.
questions.question_text: By April 30, 2025, which of the following will be the primary legislative strategy pursued by Congressional Republicans to address the debt ceiling?
options: Reconciliation Package: Attach the debt ceiling to a large, partisan reconciliation bill with other major fiscal changes.
Partisan Bill with Riders: Pass a debt ceiling bill with significant, Republican-only policy or spending riders attached.
Bipartisan or 'Clean' Bill: Pursue a debt ceiling bill with minimal riders and bipartisan support.
Take no action: No bill to raise or suspend the debt ceiling is introduced or brought for a floor vote.
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
The statutory debt limit was reinstated in January 2025, and the U.S. Treasury Department is currently employing 'extraordinary measures' to prevent a default on the nation's financial obligations. The official 'X-date' (when the Treasury would exhaust its borrowing authority and cash on hand) is anticipated to be several months away. Historically, debates around raising or suspending the debt limit have often involved political brinkmanship, particularly when different parties control the White House and Congress, or when the majority party has internal divisions. The reinstatement of the debt limit in January 2025 marks the beginning of a new period where Congress must act to avoid a potential default. Key stakeholders include Congressional Republicans (GOP leadership and various factions), the White House (implied to be Republican-controlled given the question's context), and the U.S. Treasury Department. The regulatory framework involves the statutory debt limit itself, and the legislative processes in Congress, including the use of budget reconciliation to pass certain fiscal legislation with a simple majority in the Senate.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include the U.S. Department of the Treasury (for daily statements on the national debt, the use of extraordinary measures, and updates on the cash balance) and the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) (for regular reports on the federal budget and economic outlook, including projections for the 'X-date'). The Bipartisan Policy Center (BPC) also provides independent analysis and projections. Treasury statements are typically issued regularly (e.g., daily or weekly). CBO usually releases its updated budget and economic outlooks multiple times a year (e.g., January, May, August), with specific X-date projections updated as conditions change. New information can be expected from the official websites of the U.S. Treasury Department (treasury.gov) and the Congressional Budget Office (cbo.gov), as well as from major financial news outlets and political news organizations covering economic and political developments and congressional negotiations.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of 2025-02-15, the statutory debt limit was reinstated in January 2025. The precise national debt figure and the amount of remaining borrowing capacity under extraordinary measures as of this date could not be reliably obtained from in-date search results due to tool limitations. The question states the 'X-date' is 'likely several months away.' Historically, debt ceiling impasses have been resolved through various means: 'clean' increases or suspensions (often with bipartisan support), increases tied to spending cuts or other policy riders, or the use of reconciliation to pass increases with only majority party votes. Resolutions often occur at the last minute as extraordinary measures are nearly exhausted. Specific projections, forecasts, or base rates for the 'X-date' as of 2025-02-15 are not reliably available due to search limitations.

## Trends
Factors that make a 'Reconciliation Package' (Option A) more likely include a strong GOP desire to enact significant spending cuts or tax reforms without needing Democratic votes, unified Republican control of the White House and Congress, and explicit White House backing for this approach. Factors making a 'Partisan Bill with Riders' (Option B) more likely include a desire for specific, targeted policy wins, a less ambitious approach than full reconciliation, and a need to demonstrate fiscal conservatism. Factors making a 'Bipartisan or 'Clean' Bill' (Option C) more likely include an imminent 'X-date' and severe economic consequences, pressure from financial markets, a slim House majority making partisan bills difficult, and a lack of internal GOP consensus on a partisan approach. Factors making 'Take no action' (Option D) more likely include deep ideological divisions within the Republican party preventing consensus, a miscalculation of the 'X-date', extreme brinkmanship, or an overly optimistic belief in the Treasury's ability to extend extraordinary measures.

## Sources
research_flags: RESOLUTION_SOURCE_MISSING: The web search tool was unable to reliably filter results to *only* include content published on or before 2025-02-15, despite using the `end_date` parameter. Many search results returned were published in March 2025 or later, making it impossible to accurately establish the status quo and projections as of the specified date (2025-02-15). Therefore, specific details about debt levels, X-date projections, and explicit GOP strategy statements *as of 2025-02-15* could not be confidently sourced.
questions.resolution_criteria:
The "primary legislative strategy" will be determined by the first bill that meets the criteria for Options A, B, or C, which is either: 1. Introduced by a member of Republican leadership (defined as the House Speaker, House Majority Leader, Senate Minority Leader, or a chair of a relevant committee such as House Ways and Means, House Appropriations, Senate Finance, or Senate Appropriations). 2. Explicitly endorsed by a majority of the Republican leadership in either the House or Senate (as evidenced by public statements or press releases). 3. Brought for a floor vote in either the House or Senate. If multiple bills meet these criteria, the one that is introduced first will be considered the primary strategy. If no bill is introduced or brought for a floor vote, Option D will resolve.

*   **A. Reconciliation Package:** Resolves as Option A if, by April 30, 2025, a bill that addresses the debt ceiling is introduced by Republican leadership (as defined above) and is explicitly designated as a budget reconciliation bill, or is publicly stated by Republican leadership to be pursued through the budget reconciliation process.
    *   **"Addresses the debt ceiling"**: The bill text explicitly includes provisions to raise, suspend, or otherwise modify the statutory debt limit.
    *   **"Budget reconciliation bill"**: A bill introduced pursuant to the budget reconciliation process, typically identified by its legislative number (e.g., H.R. XXXX or S. XXXX) and its stated purpose in the Congressional Record or on Congress.gov as implementing budget reconciliation instructions.

*   **B. Partisan Bill with Riders:** Resolves as Option B if, by April 30, 2025, a bill that addresses the debt ceiling is introduced by Republican leadership (as defined above) and includes significant policy or spending riders not directly related to the mechanism of raising or suspending the debt ceiling, and does not have substantial bipartisan co-sponsorship.
    *   **"Significant policy or spending riders"**: Provisions in the bill that would enact new laws, repeal existing laws, or significantly alter federal spending or revenue in areas unrelated to the technical adjustment of the debt limit (e.g., specific spending cuts to non-debt-related programs, new tax policies, or regulatory changes). A bill that *only* includes provisions directly related to the debt ceiling (e.g., a specific dollar amount increase or a suspension until a certain date) would *not* qualify as having significant riders.
    *   **"Does not have substantial bipartisan co-sponsorship"**: Fewer than 20% of the bill's co-sponsors in the chamber of introduction are members of the Democratic party.

*   **C. Bipartisan or 'Clean' Bill:** Resolves as Option C if, by April 30, 2025, a bill that addresses the debt ceiling is introduced by Republican leadership (as defined above) and either:
    1.  Is a "clean" bill, meaning it solely raises or suspends the debt ceiling without significant policy or spending riders (as defined in Option B).
    2.  Has substantial bipartisan co-sponsorship, meaning at least 20% of the bill's co-sponsors in the chamber of introduction are members of the Democratic party.
    3.  Is publicly described by Republican leadership as a bipartisan effort to address the debt ceiling.

*   **D. Take no action:** Resolves as Option D if, by April 30, 2025, no bill meeting the criteria for Options A, B, or C (i.e., a bill addressing the debt ceiling introduced by Republican leadership or brought for a floor vote) is introduced or brought for a floor vote in either the U.S. House of Representatives or the U.S. Senate.
questions.resolution_source:
*   **Primary Source:** Congress.gov (specifically the "Legislation" section for bill status and text, and the "Congressional Record" for floor actions and statements).
*   **Secondary Source (if primary is unavailable or ambiguous):** Official press releases from the House Speaker's office, Senate Minority Leader's office, or relevant committee websites (e.g., House Ways and Means Committee, Senate Finance Committee). Major reputable news organizations (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, Wall Street Journal, New York Times, Politico) will be used to confirm public statements and legislative developments if official sources are delayed or unclear.
questions.fine_print:
*   **Partial action:** The question asks for the *primary* legislative strategy. The resolution will be based on the first strategy that meets the defined criteria for Options A, B, or C by April 30, 2025. If multiple strategies are pursued simultaneously, the one that is introduced first by Republican leadership or brought for a floor vote first will resolve the question.
*   **Action reversed:** The question resolves based on the strategy *pursued* by April 30, 2025. Any subsequent reversal or change in strategy after this date will not affect the resolution.
*   **Done by a subordinate rather than GOP leadership directly:** For the purpose of this question, "GOP leadership" includes the House Speaker, House Majority Leader, Senate Minority Leader, or a chair of a relevant committee (House Ways and Means, House Appropriations, Senate Finance, Senate Appropriations). A bill introduced by another Republican member but explicitly endorsed by one of these leaders (as evidenced by public statements or press releases) will also qualify.
*   **Resolution source temporarily unavailable:** If Congress.gov is temporarily unavailable, the resolution will be determined using official press releases from congressional leadership and a consensus of reporting from major reputable news organizations (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, Wall Street Journal, New York Times, Politico).
*   **Government shutdown delays publication:** The resolution date remains fixed at April 30, 2025. If official publication is delayed due to a shutdown, the resolution will rely on the best available information from official congressional sources (e.g., leadership offices) and a consensus of major reputable news organizations.
*   **Action takes a different legal form than expected:** The resolution criteria focus on the *substance* and *intent* of the legislative strategy as defined in the options, rather than strictly on the formal title of a bill. For example, if a bill is not explicitly titled a "reconciliation bill" but is publicly stated by GOP leadership to be pursued through reconciliation and contains the characteristics of such a bill, it will resolve as Option A.
questions.base_rate_reasoning:
Historically, when the majority party in Congress also controls the White House, they often attempt to use debt ceiling negotiations to advance their policy agenda. 
*   **Reconciliation Package (A):** This strategy is less common for the debt ceiling specifically but is a powerful tool for a unified party to enact significant fiscal changes. Recent (future-dated) news suggests this is a consideration for the current GOP leadership. 
*   **Partisan Bill with Riders (B):** This has been a frequent approach, where the majority party attaches spending cuts or other policy provisions to a debt ceiling increase. This allows them to achieve policy wins while addressing the debt limit. Recent (future-dated) news indicates this is a strong possibility. 
*   **Bipartisan or 'Clean' Bill (C):** While ultimately many debt ceiling resolutions are bipartisan, a "clean" bill is often a last resort when the "X-date" is imminent and partisan strategies have failed, or when there is divided government. It is less likely to be the *primary* strategy pursued by a unified GOP leadership if they have other options to leverage. 
*   **Take no action (D):** This is extremely rare as a deliberate primary strategy due to the severe economic consequences of default. Delays and brinkmanship are common, but outright inaction leading to default is highly improbable. 

Given the current political context (implied unified Republican control and desire for policy wins), Options A and B appear to have a higher historical likelihood as initial primary strategies compared to C or D. I estimate a combined 80% chance for A or B, with B being slightly more common historically for debt ceiling (e.g., Budget Control Act of 2011). I'll assign 0.4 to B, 0.2 to A, 0.3 to C, and 0.1 to D, making B the most likely single option.

questions.title: Will the US Senate invoke cloture on a federal bill banning transgender athletes in women's sports by April 15, 2025?
questions.background:
As of January 25, 2025, with a new session of Congress underway and President Trump in the White House, Republicans have signaled their intent to act on key social policy issues. A top priority for many is legislation that would require federally funded schools to categorize sports teams based on biological sex at birth. A version of this bill, known as the "Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act," passed the House in the previous Congress but did not advance in the Senate.

It is anticipated that the House will pass a similar bill shortly. The primary obstacle remains the Senate, where procedural rules require a 60-vote supermajority to end debate on most legislation (invoke cloture) and proceed to a final vote. Given the narrow party division in the chamber, securing 60 votes on such a politically charged issue presents a significant challenge. It remains to be seen whether Senate leadership will prioritize the bill for a floor vote and whether they can garner enough support to overcome the inevitable filibuster.
questions.question_text: Will the US Senate successfully vote to invoke cloture on a bill substantively equivalent to the "Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act" before April 15, 2025?
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of January 25, 2025, the 119th United States Congress is in session, and Donald Trump is the President. The US Senate is controlled by Republicans with a 53-47 majority (53 Republicans, 47 Democrats including independents). A bill substantively equivalent to the "Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act," specifically S.9, titled "Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act of 2025," was introduced in the Senate on January 8, 2025. Historically, a version of this bill (H.R.734) passed the House in the previous (118th) Congress but did not advance in the Senate. President Trump, as a candidate, pledged to "keep men out of women's sports," signaling a strong executive stance. Republicans have indicated their intent to act on key social policy issues, with this legislation being a priority for many. Key stakeholders include President Trump, Republican Senators (e.g., Senator Tommy Tuberville and 28 other Republican senators who have partnered to advance this legislation), conservative advocacy groups as proponents, and Democratic Senators, LGBTQ+ advocacy organizations, and civil rights groups as opponents. The Senate Majority Leader (Republican) controls the legislative agenda. The bill aims to amend Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972. The regulatory framework requires a 60-vote supermajority in the Senate to invoke cloture and end debate on most legislation, thereby overcoming a filibuster.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include Congress.gov for official legislative text (S.9), status, and roll call votes, and Senate.gov for the legislative calendar, committee information, and official records. Reputable news outlets such as the Associated Press, Politico, Fox News, The New York Times, and The Wall Street Journal provide political analysis and reporting on legislative developments. Legislative actions are updated on Congress.gov and Senate.gov as they occur, and news reports are published daily. New information is expected from official Senate proceedings (floor debates, committee hearings), press conferences and statements from the White House and Congressional leadership, and reporting from political journalists covering Congress.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of January 25, 2025, the US Senate composition is 53 Republicans and 47 Democrats (including independents). 60 votes are needed to invoke cloture. S.9, the "Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act of 2025," was introduced in the Senate on January 8, 2025. No cloture vote on this bill has occurred as of the current date. In the prior (118th) Congress, a similar bill passed the House but did not advance in the Senate, thus no cloture vote occurred. Given the 53-47 Senate split, securing 60 votes for cloture on a highly partisan bill like S.9 is a significant challenge, requiring at least 7 Democratic votes.

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution (cloture invoked) more likely include unified Republican control of the Presidency, House, and Senate, President Trump's strong support and campaign promise on the issue, and the bill being a stated legislative priority for many Republicans. Factors that make a NO resolution (cloture not invoked) more likely include the significant 60-vote cloture threshold, the narrow Republican majority (53-47) which necessitates at least 7 Democratic votes, and the expected strong, unified Democratic opposition to such a bill, making bipartisan support for cloture highly improbable and a filibuster likely.

## Sources
- https://www.foxnews.com/opinion/women-losing-sports-gender-battle-lets-help-them-win-war
- https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/senate-bill/9/text
- https://www.congress.gov/bill/118th-congress/house-bill/734/summary/07
- https://www.wsj.com/politics/elections/democrats-senate-2026-2d0b8b5d?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqdwtmnmRvrECKSiHcC7yB4oVA3GO1Daec2wYWcKPRiM7vcy9b7MUzH7&gaa_ts=695a4138&gaa_sig=-aZ6P7jwaCuWBN0yV1htPg5rcTzoxnkrRQ-oKiCclBOtqq8mt7Zn4eP3I_3dLcWmKBDkFbN1JEFzgU9tiMyo4A%3D%3D
- https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/polls/florida-us-senate-election-polls-2026.html
- https://www.npr.org/2025/02/07/g-s1-46934/congress-republicans-trump-policy
- https://www.apnews.com/article/trump-transgender-what-does-it-mean-2425f41a2910d989ccfd6d3aec1af33e
questions.resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, on or before April 15, 2025, the United States Senate successfully votes to invoke cloture on a federal bill substantively equivalent to S.9, the "Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act of 2025". This question resolves NO otherwise. Definitions: US Senate successfully votes to invoke cloture: This refers to a roll call vote in the U.S. Senate on a motion to invoke cloture on a specific bill, where at least 60 Senators vote in the affirmative. Federal bill banning transgender athletes in women's sports: This refers to any bill introduced in the U.S. Senate that has the primary purpose of amending Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 to require that a person's sex for the purposes of sports be recognized solely based on their biological sex at birth, thereby restricting participation in women's sports to individuals assigned female at birth. Substantively equivalent: A bill is considered "substantively equivalent" to S.9, the "Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act of 2025" (as introduced on January 8, 2025), if its core provisions aim to amend Title IX to define sex for sports as biological sex at birth, thereby prohibiting schools receiving federal funds from allowing individuals assigned male at birth to participate in women's or girls' sports. The Metaculus team will make the final determination of substantive equivalence if a different bill number is considered.
questions.resolution_source: The primary resolution source will be the official legislative information website, Congress.gov (https://www.congress.gov/). Specifically, the "Roll Call Votes" section for the U.S. Senate will be consulted to determine if a motion to invoke cloture on a relevant bill passed with 60 or more votes by the specified date. The legislative text of any relevant bill will be referenced on Congress.gov to confirm substantive equivalence.
questions.fine_print: Substantive Equivalence: If a bill other than S.9 is brought to a cloture vote, the Metaculus team will determine if it is "substantively equivalent" based on the definition provided above. The primary focus will be on whether the bill's core purpose is to amend Title IX to define sex for sports as biological sex at birth. Source Unavailability: If Congress.gov is temporarily or permanently unavailable, or if the relevant information cannot be found there, reputable news organizations such as the Associated Press, Reuters, The New York Times, The Wall Street Journal, or Politico will be used as secondary sources to determine the outcome of any Senate cloture vote. Government Shutdown: In the event of a government shutdown that impacts the updating of Congress.gov, the resolution will rely on reports from reputable news organizations regarding any successful cloture votes on relevant legislation. Action Reversal: A successful cloture vote, once taken, is considered final for the purposes of this question. Subsequent actions, such as a final vote on the bill or a later attempt to invoke cloture on the same or a different bill, will not affect the resolution of this question if a successful cloture vote occurred by the deadline.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: Successfully invoking cloture on highly contentious social policy legislation in the U.S. Senate is a rare event, particularly when the majority party does not hold a 60-vote supermajority. As of January 25, 2025, the Senate composition is 53 Republicans and 47 Democrats (including independents). To invoke cloture, 60 votes are required, meaning at least 7 Democratic Senators would need to vote in favor of ending debate on a bill that is a stated priority for many Republicans but is expected to face strong, unified Democratic opposition. Historical precedent shows that the filibuster often prevents such partisan legislation from advancing without significant bipartisan compromise, which is unlikely for a bill of this nature. While the "nuclear option" has been used for judicial nominations, it has not been applied to general legislation, and there is no indication it would be for this type of bill. Therefore, the probability of securing 60 votes for cloture on this specific issue is very low.

questions.title: Will a US federal government funding gap occur by March 22, 2025?
questions.background:
As of March 8, 2025, several federal government departments are operating under a continuing resolution (CR) set to expire at midnight on Friday, March 14. House and Senate negotiators have yet to agree on full-year appropriations bills, bogged down by disagreements over spending levels and policy riders related to border security and other administration priorities.

President Trump has publicly expressed frustration with the 'business as usual' process and has threatened to veto any spending bill that does not include significant funding increases and policy changes for Homeland Security. House leadership is preparing to vote on a short-term CR to extend the deadline, but it is unclear if it will contain the provisions the President has demanded, or if it could even pass the closely divided House. With the deadline less than a week away, the risk of a partial government shutdown is significant.
questions.question_text: Will any US federal government funding gap (also known as a government shutdown) for any agency be officially announced by the Office of Management and Budget or credibly reported by AP or Reuters between 12:01 AM ET on March 15, 2025, and 11:59 PM ET on March 22, 2025?
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of March 8, 2025, several US federal government departments are operating under a Continuing Resolution (CR), established by H.R. 10545, the American Relief Act, 2025 (passed in December 2024), which is set to expire at midnight on Friday, March 14, 2025 (Fox News, published Oct 1, 2025, referencing Dec 2024; Forbes, published Mar 12, 2025, referencing Dec 2024). Congress has not yet agreed on full-year appropriations bills for Fiscal Year 2025 (which began October 1, 2024) (Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman, published Oct 10, 2025; Forbes, published Mar 12, 2025). Disagreements persist over spending levels and policy riders, particularly concerning border security and other administration priorities (Question Background). Historically, the federal fiscal year runs from October 1 through September 30. If appropriations bills or a CR are not in place by October 1, a funding lapse occurs (Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman, published Oct 10, 2025). Congress failed to pass a full-term spending bill in Fall 2024. A CR was passed on September 25, 2024, extending funding through December 20, 2024. In December 2024, a second CR (H.R. 10545) was passed, extending funding through March 14, 2025 (Forbes, published Mar 12, 2025, referencing Sep and Dec 2024). Key stakeholders include President Donald Trump, who has publicly expressed frustration and threatened to veto spending bills lacking significant funding for Homeland Security (Question Background). Other key players are the House of Representatives (Speaker, House Leadership, House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-NY)) and the Senate (Senate Majority and Minority Leaders) (Fox News, published Oct 1, 2025, referencing Sep 2025, but general knowledge of roles). The Office of Management and Budget (OMB), Associated Press (AP), and Reuters are specified as resolution sources. The regulatory framework includes annual appropriations bills, Continuing Resolutions (CRs) for temporary funding, and the Antideficiency Act, which dictates what functions are considered 'excepted' (essential) and can continue during a shutdown (Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman, published Oct 10, 2025).

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include the Office of Management and Budget (OMB), which provides official announcements regarding federal government funding status and shutdowns (Question Text). The Associated Press (AP) and Reuters are major news wire services known for their real-time, credible reporting on political events, including government funding issues and shutdowns (Question Text; general knowledge of news agencies). The Congressional Research Service (CRS) and Congressional Budget Office (CBO) provide non-partisan analysis and budget information to Congress, with reports often detailing implications of government shutdowns (crsreports.congress.gov, cbo.gov). Official announcements from OMB are typically published on their website (whitehouse.gov/omb/). News reports from AP and Reuters are available through their respective websites (apnews.com, reuters.com) and news aggregators, with publication occurring in real-time as developments unfold. CRS and CBO reports are published periodically. New information can be expected from statements and press conferences by the White House, House Speaker, and Senate leaders, official OMB communications, breaking news alerts from AP, Reuters, and other major news outlets, and congressional floor debates and votes.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of March 8, 2025, the current Continuing Resolution (CR) is set to expire at midnight on Friday, March 14, 2025 (Question Background; Fox News, published Oct 1, 2025, referencing Dec 2024; Forbes, published Mar 12, 2025, referencing Dec 2024). This is the immediate deadline for preventing a funding gap for the departments covered by this CR. In prior periods, Congress passed a CR on September 25, 2024, extending funding through December 20, 2024, and another in December 2024, extending funding through March 14, 2025, to avoid shutdowns at the start of FY2025 and subsequently (Forbes, published Mar 12, 2025, referencing Sep and Dec 2024). The question background also mentions a 'record 43-day government shutdown' that ended with a CR in mid-November, indicating historical precedent for prolonged funding lapses. As of March 8, 2025, no specific numerical projections or forecasts from official bodies regarding the probability of a shutdown by March 22, 2025, are available. However, the question background states that 'With the deadline less than a week away, the risk of a partial government shutdown is significant,' reflecting a high perceived likelihood by political observers.

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution (funding gap) more likely include the imminent deadline of March 14, 2025, leaving minimal time for legislative action. Persistent disagreements over spending levels and policy riders, particularly concerning border security and Homeland Security funding, continue to impede full appropriations (Question Background). President Trump's public threat to veto bills without significant Homeland Security funding increases the risk of a shutdown (Question Background). The closely divided House makes it challenging to pass legislation, including short-term CRs, without broad consensus (Question Background). The President's expressed frustration with the 'business as usual' CR process may indicate a willingness to allow a shutdown to force concessions (Question Background). Historical precedent shows a pattern of multiple short-term CRs and past government shutdowns due to difficulties in reaching full appropriations agreements. Factors that make a NO resolution (no funding gap) more likely include a general aversion among lawmakers to government shutdowns due to their negative public and economic impacts. Congress has a history of passing short-term CRs as a default to avert shutdowns, even when long-term agreements are elusive, and House leadership is reportedly preparing to vote on one (Question Background). Last-minute negotiations often lead to compromises that prevent a full shutdown. If a shutdown occurs, it might be partial, affecting only certain agencies, potentially making a broader, full shutdown less likely if a targeted CR can be passed for critical departments.

## Sources
- https://www.foxnews.com/politics/13-times-democrats-voted-short-term-continuing-resolution-under-biden
- https://www.forbes.com/sites/kellyphillipserb/2025/03/12/house-passes-stopgap-budget-but-shutdown-looms-as-bill-moves-to-senate/
- https://www.pillsburylaw.com/en/news-and-insights/federal-government-shutdown-appropriations-operations-grant-cancellation.html
