- https://www.whitehouse.gov/omb/
- https://apnews.com/
- https://www.reuters.com/
- https://crsreports.congress.gov/
- https://www.cbo.gov/
questions.resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, between 12:01 AM Eastern Time (ET) on March 15, 2025, and 11:59 PM ET on March 22, 2025, a US federal government funding gap for any agency is officially announced by the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) or credibly reported by the Associated Press (AP) or Reuters. Otherwise, this question resolves NO. A "US federal government funding gap" (also known as a "government shutdown") is defined as a lapse in appropriations for one or more US federal government agencies or departments, resulting in the cessation of non-essential government operations and the furlough of non-essential federal employees. A funding gap for even a single agency or department will be sufficient for a YES resolution.
questions.resolution_source: The primary resolution sources will be: Official announcements or guidance published on the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) website (whitehouse.gov/omb/). Specifically, the "Memoranda" or "Miscellaneous" sections under "Information & Resources" will be checked for relevant documents. News reports from the Associated Press (AP) published on apnews.com. News reports from Reuters published on reuters.com. In case of conflicting reports, official OMB announcements will take precedence. If OMB does not issue a specific announcement, the consensus of credible reporting from AP and Reuters will be used.
questions.fine_print: Partial Shutdowns: A funding gap affecting only a portion of the federal government (e.g., specific agencies or departments) will be sufficient for a YES resolution, as the question specifies "for any agency." Reversal of Action: If a funding gap begins within the specified timeframe and is subsequently resolved (e.g., by the passage of a new continuing resolution or appropriations bill) before the end of the resolution period, it will still resolve YES, as the question asks if a funding gap *occurs*. Subordinate Announcements: For the purpose of this question, an "official announcement by the Office of Management and Budget" refers to a formal public statement, memorandum, or guidance issued by OMB. Reports from AP or Reuters will be considered credible if they explicitly state that a funding gap has occurred, regardless of the specific government official quoted, as long as the reporting is consistent with their journalistic standards. Source Unavailability: If the primary resolution sources (OMB, AP News, Reuters) are temporarily unavailable or do not provide clear information, other highly credible news organizations (e.g., The New York Times, The Washington Post, Wall Street Journal) or official government records (e.g., Congressional Record, federalregister.gov) will be consulted to determine if a funding gap occurred. Government Shutdown Delays: If a government shutdown itself delays the official publication of an OMB announcement or impacts the real-time reporting capabilities of AP or Reuters, the resolution will be based on the earliest credible information available that confirms the occurrence of a funding gap within the specified timeframe. Different Legal Form: The specific legislative mechanism that leads to a funding gap (e.g., failure to pass a full appropriations bill, failure to pass a continuing resolution) is not critical to the resolution. The key is the *outcome*: a lapse in appropriations leading to a cessation of non-essential government operations.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: Since fiscal year 1977, the US federal government has experienced approximately 20-21 funding gaps or shutdowns, averaging less than one per year. However, this historical frequency is for the entire year. More recently, there has been a trend of Congress relying on short-term continuing resolutions, leading to multiple deadlines and increased risk of funding gaps within a single fiscal year. For instance, in Fiscal Year 2025, a CR was passed in September 2024, extending funding through December 2024, and another in December 2024, extending funding through March 14, 2025. This pattern suggests a heightened probability of a funding gap around these specific deadlines compared to a random week in the year. Given the specific 7-day window and the immediate preceding deadline, a base rate of 0.1 (10% chance) is a rough estimate reflecting the elevated, but not guaranteed, risk.

questions.title: What will be President Trump's action on the first FY2025 continuing resolution passed by Congress in March 2025?
questions.background:
To avert a partial government shutdown on March 14, 2025, the House and Senate are negotiating a continuing resolution (CR) to extend government funding. The key uncertainty is President Trump's position. He has repeatedly demanded that any spending bill must include his administration's 'DOGE' (Deterrence of Global Enemies) provisions, which would add significant funding for border security and defense initiatives while cutting funds for international organizations.

As of March 8, 2025, the draft legislation circulating in the House does not fully meet these demands, setting up a potential conflict between Congress and the White House. It remains to be seen what version of the bill will pass, and whether the President will use his veto power to force the issue. This question asks what action the President will ultimately take on the first CR that lands on his desk this month, with resolution by March 25, 2025.
questions.question_text: Which of the following will be President Trump's ultimate action regarding the first continuing resolution funding the government for any part of Fiscal Year 2025 that is passed by both houses of Congress on or after March 8, 2025?
options: Veto the Continuing Resolution
Sign a CR that includes the core 'DOGE' provisions
Sign a 'clean' CR largely devoid of major policy provisions
Take no action, allowing the bill to become law without a signature
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of March 8, 2025, the U.S. government is operating under a continuing resolution (CR) set to expire on March 14, 2025, necessitating new funding legislation to avert a partial government shutdown. On this date, House Republicans unveiled a six-month stopgap funding bill (CR) aimed at funding the government through the end of Fiscal Year 2025 (September 30, 2025). President Donald Trump has publicly endorsed this House-proposed CR and is actively encouraging Republican support for its passage to prevent a shutdown. The proposed House bill largely maintains existing spending levels, includes an increase in funding for U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) at the Trump administration's request, and incorporates a $20 billion rescission for the Internal Revenue Service (IRS). Uncertainty exists regarding the bill's passage in the House, with House Democrats indicating opposition to a partisan funding plan and some expressing openness to a shutdown if President Trump continues actions they perceive as 'dismantling agencies.' Historically, Congress has frequently relied on CRs due to delays in passing full annual appropriations bills. During his previous presidency, Trump signed multiple CRs but also oversaw a 35-day government shutdown (December 2018-January 2019) over border wall funding, demonstrating a willingness to use shutdowns as leverage. The question's background references President Trump's 'DOGE' (Deterrence of Global Enemies) provisions, which call for significant funding for border security and defense while cutting international organizations. The current House bill includes increased ICE funding, aligning with border security, but it is not definitively confirmed if it fully satisfies the broader 'DOGE' provisions, particularly cuts to international organizations. Key stakeholders include President Trump, House Republicans (led by Speaker Mike Johnson), House Democrats (led by Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries), and the U.S. Senate. The legal framework involves Congress's power of the purse and the President's options to sign, veto, or allow legislation to become law.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include Congress.gov for official legislative text, voting records, and bill status; the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) for cost estimates and analyses; White House press briefings and statements for official administration positions; and major news outlets (e.g., Reuters, Associated Press, Washington Post) for real-time reporting and analysis. These sources are publicly accessible online. Legislative actions and presidential statements occur on irregular schedules, driven by political developments. New information will become available through official statements from the White House and congressional leaders, roll call votes in both chambers, and ongoing news coverage.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of March 8, 2025, the current government funding is set to expire on March 14, 2025. The House Republicans have unveiled a six-month CR to fund the government through September 2025. President Trump has publicly supported this bill. In prior periods, specifically during his previous administration, President Trump signed several continuing resolutions to avoid government shutdowns. However, he also presided over the longest government shutdown in U.S. history (35 days from December 2018 to January 2019) over funding for a border wall, demonstrating a willingness to use a shutdown as leverage for key policy demands. The current House bill includes increased ICE funding and IRS cuts, which align with some of the Trump administration's stated priorities, but it's not explicitly confirmed if it fully meets the 'DOGE' provisions as described in the question's background.

## Trends
Factors that make a Veto (Option A) more likely include: the final CR significantly deviates from President Trump's 'DOGE' provisions (especially regarding border security/defense funding or international organization cuts); a strategic calculation by Trump that a veto would enhance his negotiating leverage; or strong pressure from conservative factions to reject an insufficient bill. Factors that make Signing a CR that includes core 'DOGE' provisions (Option B) more likely include: the final CR substantially meets Trump's 'DOGE' demands (e.g., increased ICE funding); successful negotiations between the White House and Congress; or a desire to demonstrate legislative success and avoid a shutdown. Factors that make Signing a 'clean' CR largely devoid of major policy provisions (Option C) more likely include: intense political pressure to avoid a shutdown; Congress's inability to agree on a bill with significant 'DOGE' provisions, forcing a choice between a clean CR and a shutdown; or a strategic decision by Trump to defer contentious policy battles. Factors that make Taking no action, allowing the bill to become law without a signature (Option D) more likely are less common for critical legislation like a CR, as it would lead to a shutdown if Congress is in session. It could occur if Trump is ambivalent about the bill but not strongly opposed enough to veto, and Congress is prepared to remain in session to ensure its enactment.

## Sources
- https://www.reuters.com/world/us/us-house-republicans-unveil-six-month-stopgap-bill-avert-shutdown-2025-03-08/
- https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2025/03/08/government-shutdown-cr-congress-trump/
- http://democrats-appropriations.house.gov/news/press-releases/power-grab-full-year-continuing-resolution-allows-elon-musk-and-president-trump
- https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/what-to-know-as-the-deadline-to-avoid-a-partial-government-shutdown-creeps-closer
- https://www.npr.org/2025/03/08/g-s1-52764/house-republicans-spending-bill-government-shutdown-trump
- https://www.nbcnews.com/politics/congress/andy-kim-open-to-government-shutdown-trump-rcna191371
questions.resolution_criteria:
This question asks about President Trump's ultimate action on the first continuing resolution (CR) funding the government for any part of Fiscal Year 2025 (FY2025) that is passed by both houses of Congress on or after March 8, 2025.

*   **"Continuing Resolution (CR)"**: A legislative measure, typically a joint resolution or bill, that provides temporary funding for federal government agencies and programs when regular annual appropriations bills have not been enacted by the start of the fiscal year (October 1). For the purpose of this question, it refers specifically to the first such measure funding any part of FY2025 that is passed by both houses of Congress on or after March 8, 2025.
*   **"President Trump's ultimate action"**: Refers to the final action taken by President Trump on the specified CR. This includes signing the bill into law, formally vetoing the bill, or allowing it to become law without his signature.
*   **"Core 'DOGE' provisions"**: For the purpose of this question, a CR will be considered to include "core 'DOGE' provisions" if the enacted legislation includes *either* a net increase in funding for U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) *or* an explicit rescission of at least $20 billion from the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) compared to the previous fiscal year's enacted levels for those specific line items. This will be determined by comparing the relevant line items in the enacted CR to the corresponding enacted amounts for FY2024.
*   **"'Clean' CR largely devoid of major policy provisions"**: A CR that primarily extends funding at or near existing levels, without significant new policy riders, spending cuts beyond minor adjustments, or substantial increases/decreases to specific agencies or programs that would be considered major policy changes. For the purpose of this question, it means a CR that *does not* meet the criteria for "core 'DOGE' provisions" and *does not* include other significant policy riders or substantial changes to existing law.

The question will resolve as follows:

*   **A. Veto the Continuing Resolution**: Resolves as Option A if President Trump formally vetoes the first continuing resolution funding the government for any part of Fiscal Year 2025 that is passed by both houses of Congress on or after March 8, 2025, and this veto is announced by the White House.
*   **B. Sign a CR that includes the core 'DOGE' provisions**: Resolves as Option B if President Trump signs into law the first continuing resolution funding the government for any part of Fiscal Year 2025 that is passed by both houses of Congress on or after March 8, 2025, and the enacted legislation meets the criteria for including "core 'DOGE' provisions" as defined above.
*   **C. Sign a 'clean' CR largely devoid of major policy provisions**: Resolves as Option C if President Trump signs into law the first continuing resolution funding the government for any part of Fiscal Year 2025 that is passed by both houses of Congress on or after March 8, 2025, and the enacted legislation *does not* meet the criteria for Option B, and *does not* include other significant new policy riders or substantial changes to existing law beyond extending funding at or near existing levels.
*   **D. Take no action, allowing the bill to become law without a signature**: Resolves as Option D if the first continuing resolution funding the government for any part of Fiscal Year 2025 that is passed by both houses of Congress on or after March 8, 2025, becomes law without President Trump's signature. This occurs if the President does not sign or veto the bill within 10 days (excluding Sundays) of receiving it while Congress is in session.
questions.resolution_source: The official status of legislation and presidential actions will be monitored on `Congress.gov` (https://www.congress.gov/). Presidential statements, signing statements, or veto messages will be referenced on `Whitehouse.gov` (https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/ and https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefings-statements/). In case of ambiguity or delay in official sources, major reputable news outlets such as Reuters (`reuters.com`) or Associated Press (`apnews.com`) will be used to confirm presidential actions and the content of the enacted legislation.
questions.fine_print: Partial Action: The resolution criteria for Option B ("core 'DOGE' provisions") are defined to be specific and measurable. If the bill includes some elements but not others, it will resolve based on whether it explicitly meets the defined criteria for Option B. Action Reversed: The question asks for President Trump's "ultimate action." Once the President takes one of the specified actions (signs, vetoes, or allows to become law without signature), that action is considered final for the purpose of this question. Subsequent congressional action (e.g., overriding a veto) does not change the President's initial action. Done by Subordinate: Actions on legislation (signing, vetoing, or allowing to become law) are constitutionally reserved for the President. Actions by subordinates will not count. Resolution Source Unavailable: If `Congress.gov` or `Whitehouse.gov` are temporarily unavailable, resolution will be delayed until they are restored. If they become permanently unavailable or cease to publish the necessary information, reputable news sources (Reuters, AP) will be used as primary sources, with a note on the change. Government Shutdown Delays Publication: If a government shutdown delays official publication on `Congress.gov` or `Whitehouse.gov`, resolution will be based on the earliest verifiable public announcement by the White House or congressional leadership, as reported by major reputable news outlets (Reuters, AP), with official confirmation sought once government operations resume. Different Legal Form: The question specifically refers to a "continuing resolution." If Congress passes a different type of funding bill (e.g., a full appropriations bill) that serves the same purpose of funding the government for any part of FY2025 and is passed by both houses on or after March 8, 2025, it will be considered the "first continuing resolution" for the purpose of this question. The key is the *function* of the bill (funding the government temporarily for FY2025) rather than its precise legislative title, as long as it is a joint resolution or bill that requires presidential action.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: A. Veto the Continuing Resolution (0.10 - 0.20): Presidential vetoes of continuing resolutions are historically rare, as CRs are typically compromise measures to avoid government shutdowns. However, President Trump has demonstrated a willingness to use veto power and leverage shutdowns for policy demands (e.g., the 2018-2019 government shutdown over border wall funding). Given the specific context of his stated demands for 'DOGE' provisions, a veto is a plausible, though not common, outcome. B. Sign a CR that includes the core 'DOGE' provisions (0.50 - 0.70): Presidents frequently sign continuing resolutions. If a CR includes provisions aligned with the President's stated priorities, such as increased ICE funding or IRS rescissions as mentioned in the background, it is a highly probable outcome. C. Sign a 'clean' CR largely devoid of major policy provisions (0.20 - 0.40): This is also a common outcome, especially when there is strong political pressure to avoid a government shutdown and no consensus on significant policy riders. Presidents often sign "clean" CRs as a temporary measure to keep the government funded. D. Take no action, allowing the bill to become law without a signature (<0.01): Allowing a bill to become law without signature is a constitutional option, but it is extremely rare for critical funding legislation like a continuing resolution. For a CR, presidential inaction would typically lead to a government shutdown if Congress is in session, which is generally undesirable for both the President and Congress. The overall base rate estimate of 0.5 reflects the most likely outcome being a signed CR, with a slight leaning towards one with some of the President's priorities included, given the current political climate and the specifics of the House bill mentioned in the background.

questions.title: Will any government funding bill signed into law by April 15, 2025, include a rescission of at least $15 billion in IRS funding?
questions.background: The U.S. government is currently funded by a Continuing Resolution (CR) that expires at midnight on March 14, 2025. The Republican-controlled House has passed a bill to extend funding, but it includes several conservative policy priorities. A key provision is the clawback of approximately $20.2 billion allocated to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) by the Inflation Reduction Act. President Trump has voiced support for the House bill. However, the bill faces an uncertain future in the Senate, where Democrats have enough votes to filibuster and have previously opposed efforts to rescind IRS funds. With the deadline just days away, it remains to be seen whether this provision will survive negotiations or if it will be stripped out in a compromise to avert a government shutdown. This question asks whether, after the immediate deadline passes and potential further extensions are negotiated, the final legislation will contain this significant conservative policy win.
questions.question_text: Will any government funding legislation signed into law by April 15, 2025, which provides appropriations for any part of the U.S. government, include a rescission or de-appropriation of at least $15 billion from the Internal Revenue Service?
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
The U.S. government is currently funded by a Continuing Resolution (CR) that is set to expire at midnight on March 14, 2025. If new funding legislation is not enacted by this deadline, a government shutdown will occur. The Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) of 2022 initially appropriated approximately $80 billion in supplemental funding for the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) over a 10-year period, intended for modernization, improved taxpayer services, and enhanced enforcement. Since then, Congress has rescinded portions of this funding, including $1.4 billion by the Fiscal Responsibility Act of 2024 (June 2023) and an additional $20.2 billion in the 2024 Omnibus Appropriations package (March 2024). As of March 2025, the IRS reportedly had $25.3 billion remaining in its IRA operations funding account. A short-term funding bill is currently under consideration in Congress. The House of Representatives has either passed or is expected to consider a bill around March 8, 2025, that includes a provision to rescind approximately $20.2 billion from the IRS's Inflation Reduction Act funding. Key stakeholders include House Republicans, who strongly advocate for these cuts; Senate Democrats, who have historically opposed such cuts and possess filibuster power; and President Trump, who has expressed support for House Republican efforts to pass funding bills with conservative priorities. The legislative process requires identical bills to pass both chambers and be signed by the President to become law, with CRs serving as temporary measures to avoid shutdowns.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include Congress.gov for official legislative text, bill status, and voting records; the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) for cost estimates and analyses of proposed legislation; and the Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration (TIGTA) for reports on IRS operations and funding (e.g., an August 2025 report referenced March 2025 IRS funding data). Websites of the House and Senate Appropriations Committees provide details on proposed spending bills. The White House and Office of Management and Budget (OMB) are sources for presidential statements and budget proposals. Major news outlets such as Reuters, Law360, Forbes, Bloomberg Law News, Accounting Today, ABC News, and Politico are relevant for tracking legislative negotiations and political commentary. Legislative information on Congress.gov is updated continuously. CBO reports are released as relevant legislation moves through Congress. TIGTA reports are published periodically. News reporting is ongoing, with increased frequency around critical deadlines. New information will emerge from ongoing negotiations between House and Senate leadership, votes in both chambers, and statements from the White House. The final text of any signed legislation will be the definitive source.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of March 8, 2025, the House-passed (or soon-to-be-passed) funding bill includes a proposed rescission of approximately $20.2 billion from IRS funding. This amount exceeds the $15 billion threshold for the question. The Inflation Reduction Act originally provided $80 billion to the IRS. Prior rescissions from this funding totaled approximately $21.6 billion ($1.4 billion in June 2023 and $20.2 billion in March 2024). The remaining IRA IRS operations funding, as of March 2025, was $25.3 billion. The most critical recent event is the expiration of the current Continuing Resolution on March 14, 2025, which necessitates immediate congressional action. No explicit quantitative forecasts were found, but the political dynamics indicate intense negotiations.

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution (rescission of at least $15 billion) more likely include strong Republican unity in the House to cut IRS funding, President Trump's endorsement of conservative funding priorities, the leverage of the looming March 14 shutdown deadline, and the precedent of previous successful rescissions of IRA IRS funding. Factors that make a NO resolution (no rescission of at least $15 billion) more likely include strong Senate Democratic opposition, who can filibuster legislation and require bipartisan compromise, the overall need for a bipartisan compromise to avoid a government shutdown, the potential negative impact of further cuts on IRS operations (taxpayer services, enforcement, modernization), and the possibility of Congress resorting to a 'clean' Continuing Resolution without controversial policy riders if negotiations fail.

## Sources
- https://www.law360.com/tax-authority/amp/articles/2307894
- https://www.foxnews.com/politics/13-times-democrats-voted-short-term-continuing-resolution-under-biden
- https://www.forbes.com/sites/kellyphillipserb/2025/03/12/house-passes-stopgap-budget-but-shutdown-looms-as-bill-moves-to-senate/
- https://www.accountingtoday.com/news/irs-uses-inflation-reduction-act-funding-to-stay-open-during-shutdown
- https://www.accountingtoday.com/news/irs-advisory-council-blasts-defunding-of-agency
- https://news.bloombergtax.com/daily-tax-report/lawmakers-ax-11-6-billion-more-in-irs-funding-in-spending-plan
- https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/live-updates/trump-2nd-term-tariffs-ukraine?id=119377651&entryId=119448847&CID=WA
questions.resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if any public law signed by the President of the United States on or before April 15, 2025, that provides appropriations for any part of the U.S. government, includes a provision that explicitly rescinds or de-appropriates at least $15 billion from funds previously appropriated to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS). This question resolves NO otherwise. Definitions: Public Law: A law affecting the general public, enacted by Congress and signed by the President, or passed over a presidential veto. Identified by a Public Law number (e.g., P.L. 118-15). Signed into law: A bill becomes a public law when it has passed both chambers of Congress in identical form and has been signed by the President, or if Congress overrides a presidential veto. The date of signing by the President is the critical date. Government funding legislation: Any public law that includes provisions for new budget authority (appropriations) for any federal agency or program, or continues existing appropriations (e.g., a Continuing Resolution). Rescission or de-appropriation: A statutory cancellation of budget authority previously provided by Congress. This reduces the amount of funds available for obligation. The language must explicitly state a reduction or cancellation of funds from the IRS. Internal Revenue Service (IRS): The U.S. government agency responsible for tax collection and tax law enforcement. The rescinded funds must be specifically designated for the IRS. At least $15 billion: The cumulative amount of rescinded or de-appropriated funds from the IRS within a single qualifying public law must be equal to or greater than $15,000,000,000. The amount will be determined by the official text of the public law and, if necessary, the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) cost estimate for that specific provision.
questions.resolution_source: Congress.gov: For the official text and status of all public laws. URL: https://www.congress.gov/. Congressional Budget Office (CBO): If the exact amount of rescinded funds is not explicitly clear in the public law's text, or if there is ambiguity, the official CBO cost estimate for the relevant public law will be used to determine the amount of rescission from the IRS. URL: https://www.cbo.gov/
questions.fine_print: Partial Action: If a funding bill includes a rescission of IRS funding, but the amount is less than $15 billion, the question resolves NO. Reversed Action: Once a bill is signed into law, its provisions are considered final for the purpose of this question. Subsequent legislation that reverses or modifies the rescission after April 15, 2025, will not affect the resolution of this question. The resolution is based on the state of law as of April 15, 2025. Action by Subordinate: Only legislation signed into law by the President of the United States will count. Actions by executive agencies or individual departments do not qualify. Resolution Source Temporarily Unavailable: If Congress.gov or CBO.gov are temporarily unavailable, resolution will be delayed until they become accessible. If they become permanently unavailable, a consensus of reputable news sources (e.g., Reuters, Associated Press, New York Times, Wall Street Journal) reporting on the final text and impact of the legislation will be used. Government Shutdown: A government shutdown may delay the official publication of public laws or CBO reports. The resolution date will be extended if necessary to allow for the official publication of relevant documents. The deadline for the signing of the bill remains April 15, 2025. Different Legal Form: The legislation must be a "public law" that provides appropriations for the U.S. government. Executive orders, proclamations, or other non-legislative actions will not count. The rescission must be explicitly stated within the text of the public law.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: The background research indicates that $20.2 billion was rescinded from IRS funding in the 2024 Omnibus Appropriations package (March 2024). This single event meets the criteria of a rescission of at least $15 billion. While a single instance doesn't establish a long-term frequency, it demonstrates that such an event is politically and legislatively feasible and has occurred very recently. Given the ongoing political push for IRS funding cuts, the probability is not negligible. The base rate is therefore considered to be 0.25 (25%). This reflects that while it has happened, it's not a routine occurrence, but also not an unprecedented one.

questions.title: How will the U.S. government be funded after the March 14, 2025 deadline?
questions.background: The federal government's funding is set to expire at midnight on March 14, 2025. The House has passed a partisan funding bill with spending cuts and policy riders, which President Trump supports. However, it is widely seen as having little chance of passing the Senate in its current form. With the deadline looming, congressional leaders and the White House must decide whether to force a confrontation, seek a bipartisan compromise, or pass another short-term extension to buy more time. The path forward will depend on intense negotiations over the next several days, with a government shutdown remaining a distinct possibility if no agreement is reached.
questions.question_text: By March 28, 2025, which of the following will be the primary mechanism funding the U.S. government departments covered by the current Continuing Resolution, which expires at midnight ET on March 14, 2025?
options: A government shutdown is in effect
The House-passed CR (including IRS cuts) has become law
A new 'clean' short-term CR has become law
A full-year appropriations bill has become law
Take no action / other outcome
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of March 8, 2025, the U.S. federal government is operating under a Continuing Resolution (CR) that is set to expire at midnight on March 14, 2025. If no new funding legislation is enacted by this deadline, a government shutdown will occur for the departments and agencies covered by the expiring CR. Historically, the U.S. Congress is tasked with passing 12 individual appropriations bills annually to fund the government for the fiscal year, which begins on October 1. When these bills are not enacted on time, Congress often resorts to passing temporary funding measures, or Continuing Resolutions (CRs), to keep the government operating. This reliance on CRs has been a recurring pattern in recent years. Key stakeholders in these negotiations typically include the President, the Speaker of the House, the Senate Majority and Minority Leaders, and the chairs and ranking members of the House and Senate Appropriations Committees. The legal framework dictates that discretionary spending, which accounts for approximately one-third of the federal budget, requires annual authorization through appropriations bills or CRs. Mandatory spending programs, such as Social Security and Medicare, are funded outside this annual process and are generally not affected by these deadlines.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources for tracking government funding and legislative actions include the official websites of the U.S. House of Representatives and the U.S. Senate, particularly their respective Appropriations Committees. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO) provides cost estimates and analyses of proposed legislation. The Congressional Research Service (CRS) offers non-partisan reports on legislative processes and historical context. The White House website provides presidential statements and administration positions. Major news outlets specializing in U.S. politics and government (e.g., Politico, Reuters, Forbes) are crucial for real-time reporting on negotiations and legislative developments. These sources are generally accessible to the public via their websites. Congressional actions, such as bill introductions, votes, and committee markups, are recorded and made public immediately. CBO reports are published as needed. News outlets provide daily updates and breaking news. New information regarding the status of funding negotiations, proposed bills, and potential outcomes will primarily become available through public statements from congressional leaders and the White House, as well as through reporting from political journalists covering Capitol Hill.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of March 8, 2025, the critical event is the impending expiration of the current Continuing Resolution at midnight on March 14, 2025. No specific numbers regarding a newly passed House bill or its contents can be verified from sources published on or before March 8, 2025, based on the provided search results. Historically, government shutdowns have occurred due to impasses over funding, with notable examples including a 35-day shutdown from December 2018 to January 2019. Expert forecasts, based on general trends observed in the appropriations process, suggest a likelihood of continued 'legislative logjam' and reliance on CRs, indicating a challenging fiscal environment prone to crisis-driven appropriations.

## Trends
Factors that make a particular outcome more likely as of March 8, 2025:

*   **A. A government shutdown is in effect:** Increased partisan polarization and an inability to reach bipartisan consensus on spending levels or policy riders could lead to a lapse in funding. If either chamber or the President holds firm on specific demands, a shutdown becomes more probable.
*   **B. The House-passed CR (including IRS cuts) has become law:** This outcome would require significant bipartisan support in the Senate (60 votes to overcome a filibuster) or a change in Senate rules. Without verifiable information on such a bill's passage or widespread support as of March 8, 2025, this option's likelihood cannot be accurately assessed from the available data.
*   **C. A new 'clean' short-term CR has become law:** This is a common outcome when full appropriations or partisan bills fail, as it allows more time for negotiations without contentious policy riders. A mutual desire to avoid the negative consequences of a shutdown often pushes lawmakers toward this temporary solution.
*   **D. A full-year appropriations bill has become law:** This outcome is generally difficult to achieve by deadlines due to the complexity of negotiating 12 separate bills and inherent partisan disagreements on spending priorities. Given the short timeframe to the March 14 deadline, a comprehensive, full-year agreement is historically less likely.

Factors that make a particular outcome less likely as of March 8, 2025:

*   **A. A government shutdown is in effect (less likely if compromise is reached):** Public and political pressure to avoid the disruptions and economic impacts of a shutdown often motivate lawmakers to find a resolution, even if temporary.
*   **B. The House-passed CR (including IRS cuts) has become law (likelihood cannot be assessed without verifiable information):** As of March 8, 2025, the existence and specific contents of such a bill, as well as its support, are not verifiable from the provided search results. Therefore, its likelihood cannot be determined.
*   **C. A new 'clean' short-term CR has become law (less likely if parties remain entrenched):** If either party insists on including specific policy riders or significant spending changes, a 'clean' CR may fail to pass.
*   **D. A full-year appropriations bill has become law (highly unlikely):** The historical pattern of relying on CRs, coupled with the short timeframe and the inherent difficulties of bipartisan agreement on all 12 appropriations bills, makes a full-year resolution by March 14, 2025, highly improbable.

## Sources

As of the research date (March 8, 2025), the provided web search results do not contain any verifiable information published on or before this date to confirm that the House had already passed such a specific bill, that President Trump supported it, or the Senate's specific views on it. All specific reporting on these details in the search results was published after March 8, 2025 (e.g., March 10, March 12, 2025). Therefore, these specific claims in the question's background cannot be substantiated as of the research date, indicating potentially 'hallucinated facts' relative to the research timeframe.
questions.resolution_criteria:
This question will resolve based on the primary mechanism funding the U.S. government departments covered by the Continuing Resolution expiring at midnight on March 14, 2025.

*   **"Law"**: A bill becomes law when it is passed by both the U.S. House of Representatives and the U.S. Senate in identical form and is either signed by the President, or the President allows it to become law without signature, or Congress overrides a presidential veto. The official status of a bill can be tracked on congress.gov.
*   **"Primary mechanism funding"**: This refers to the legislative act that provides the legal authority for federal agencies to obligate and expend funds. If multiple funding mechanisms are in place, the "primary" one will be the one that covers the largest portion of the U.S. government departments covered by the current Continuing Resolution, or the one that is most comprehensive in scope.
*   **"U.S. government departments covered by the current Continuing Resolution"**: This refers to the federal agencies and programs whose funding is subject to annual appropriations and is currently operating under the CR expiring on March 14, 2025. This excludes mandatory spending programs like Social Security and Medicare.

**Resolves as Option A: A government shutdown is in effect**
*   If, as of 11:59 PM ET on March 14, 2025, and continuing through March 28, 2025, there is no new appropriations legislation (either a Continuing Resolution or a full-year appropriations bill) enacted into law to fund the U.S. government departments covered by the expiring March 14, 2025, Continuing Resolution. This will be evidenced by official statements from the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) or other relevant federal agencies, or by widespread reporting from reputable news sources (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters) confirming a lapse in appropriations for these departments, leading to a cessation of non-essential government operations.

**Resolves as Option B: The House-passed CR (including IRS cuts) has become law**
*   If, by 11:59 PM ET on March 28, 2025, a Continuing Resolution that originated in the U.S. House of Representatives and includes specific provisions for cuts to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) budget, has been enacted into law. The specific bill number and its contents (including IRS cuts) must be verifiable on congress.gov and it must be the primary mechanism funding the U.S. government departments covered by the expiring March 14, 2025, Continuing Resolution.

**Resolves as Option C: A new 'clean' short-term CR has become law**
*   If, by 11:59 PM ET on March 28, 2025, a new Continuing Resolution, which primarily extends funding for the U.S. government departments covered by the expiring March 14, 2025, CR without significant new policy riders or substantial changes to existing programs (i.e., a "clean" CR), has been enacted into law. This bill must be the primary mechanism funding the U.S. government departments covered by the expiring March 14, 2025, Continuing Resolution. The absence of significant new policy riders or substantial changes will be determined by reviewing the text of the enacted law on congress.gov.

**Resolves as Option D: A full-year appropriations bill has become law**
*   If, by 11:59 PM ET on March 28, 2025, one or more full-year appropriations bills (either individual appropriations bills or an omnibus/minibus package covering all or most of the annually appropriated federal departments) for Fiscal Year 2025 has been enacted into law, providing funding for the U.S. government departments covered by the expiring March 14, 2025, CR. This legislation must be the primary mechanism funding these government departments. The status of such bills will be verifiable on congress.gov.

**Resolves as Option E: Take no action / other outcome**
*   If none of the conditions for Options A, B, C, or D are met by 11:59 PM ET on March 28, 2025. This includes scenarios where a funding mechanism is enacted that does not fit the descriptions of B, C, or D, or if the primary funding mechanism is unclear or ambiguous.
questions.resolution_source: The primary resolution source will be congress.gov (https://www.congress.gov/), specifically the "Actions" and "Text" tabs for relevant legislation. In cases of a government shutdown, official statements from the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) (https://www.omb.gov/) or the relevant federal agencies' websites will be consulted. Reputable news sources (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, Politico) will be used as secondary sources to confirm widespread understanding and reporting of events, but congress.gov and omb.gov will be the definitive sources for legal status and official declarations.
questions.fine_print:
*   **Partial Action**: If a funding mechanism is enacted that only partially funds the U.S. government departments covered by the expiring CR, and it does not fit the descriptions of Options B, C, or D, it will resolve as Option E. If a partial shutdown occurs (i.e., some departments are funded while others are not), and no primary funding mechanism for the majority of the affected departments is in place, it will resolve as Option A.
*   **Action Reversed After Being Taken**: The resolution will be based on the status as of 11:59 PM ET on March 28, 2025. Any reversal of action after this date will not affect the resolution.
*   **Done by a Subordinate**: For a bill to become law, it must follow the constitutional process involving both chambers of Congress and the President. Actions by subordinates (e.g., agency heads) cannot enact legislation. Official statements regarding a shutdown from OMB or relevant agencies will be considered valid.
*   **Resolution Source Temporarily Unavailable**: If congress.gov or omb.gov are temporarily unavailable, reputable news sources (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, Politico) will be used to determine the outcome. Once the official sources become available, they will be consulted for final verification.
*   **Government Shutdown Delays Publication**: If a government shutdown delays the official publication of legislative actions or statements, the resolution will be based on the best available information from reputable news sources as of the resolution date. Once official information becomes available, it will be used for final verification.
*   **Action Takes Different Legal Form**: The options cover the most common legal forms of funding the government (CRs, full-year appropriations). If a different legal form is used that effectively funds the government departments covered by the expiring CR, and it does not clearly fit Options B, C, or D, it will resolve as Option E. The resolution will prioritize the *effect* of the action (funding the government) over its specific legal nomenclature, as long as it is a legislative act.
questions.base_rate_reasoning:
Based on historical trends over the last 10-15 years, the U.S. government frequently faces funding deadlines, often leading to temporary measures.

*   **Government Shutdowns (Option A)**: Government shutdowns have occurred periodically, notably a 16-day shutdown in 2013, a 35-day shutdown from December 2018 to January 2019, and a 43-day shutdown starting in October 2025 (as reported by various news sources). This indicates a recurring, though not annual, possibility when legislative impasses are severe.
*   **House-passed CR (including IRS cuts) (Option B)**: There is no clear historical precedent for a specific partisan House-passed CR with significant policy riders (like IRS cuts) becoming law by a tight deadline. Such bills often face significant hurdles in the Senate. This outcome is historically less frequent.
*   **New 'clean' short-term CR (Option C)**: This is a very common outcome. Congress frequently passes short-term Continuing Resolutions to extend funding and avoid shutdowns when full appropriations bills are not ready. News reports indicate multiple short-term CRs in 2022, 2024, and three consecutive CRs in fiscal year 2025. This is often the default mechanism to buy more time for negotiations.
*   **Full-year appropriations bill (Option D)**: While the goal is to pass full-year appropriations, they are often delayed past initial deadlines. The Black Chronicle reported that "Congress never passed a real budget in fiscal year 2025, instead punting forward the shutdown deadline via three consecutive CRs," suggesting that full-year appropriations by a deadline are less common than temporary measures.

Given these trends, a new 'clean' short-term CR (Option C) has the highest historical frequency by a deadline like March 14. A government shutdown (Option A) is also a significant, recurring possibility. Options B and D are historically less likely to occur by such a tight deadline. The base rate estimate of 0.6 reflects the high likelihood of a short-term CR based on past behavior, with other options having lower individual probabilities.

questions.title: Will Congress adopt a concurrent budget resolution with tax reconciliation instructions by May 15, 2025?
questions.background:
As of February 20, 2025, extending the expiring provisions of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) is a primary goal for the Trump administration and the Republican-controlled Congress. With most of the individual tax cuts set to sunset at the end of 2025, there is significant pressure to act this year.

The most likely legislative path for such a significant tax package is through the budget reconciliation process, which would allow the Senate to pass the bill with a simple majority and bypass a potential Democratic filibuster. However, before a reconciliation bill can be considered, both the House and Senate must first agree on and pass a concurrent budget resolution that includes 'reconciliation instructions.' These instructions direct the relevant committees (like the House Ways and Means Committee) to draft legislation that meets certain budgetary targets.

The process of drafting and passing a budget resolution can be complex and time-consuming, sometimes subject to delays from internal party disagreements over spending levels and the overall size of the deficit. While the Republican party is broadly aligned on the goal of extending the tax cuts, debates among different factions regarding fiscal responsibility could influence the timeline. It remains to be seen how quickly the new Congress can coalesce around a single budgetary framework.
questions.question_text: Will Congress adopt a concurrent budget resolution for Fiscal Year 2026 containing tax reconciliation instructions by May 15, 2025?
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of 2025-02-20, the Republican Party controls both the House of Representatives and the Senate. A primary legislative goal for the Republican-controlled Congress and the Trump administration is the extension of the expiring provisions of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA). Most individual tax cuts from the TCJA are scheduled to sunset at the end of 2025, creating significant pressure for legislative action in 2025. As of February 20, 2025, the House of Representatives had not yet adopted its budget resolution for Fiscal Year 2026. House Republicans were actively working towards a vote on their budget framework, which was expected to include reconciliation instructions. There were reports of internal disagreements among House Republicans regarding the specifics of the budget, particularly concerning spending levels and the overall size of the deficit. In the Senate, Budget Committee Chair Lindsey Graham (R-SC) introduced a separate budget resolution on February 12, 2025, indicating the Senate's initiation of its own budget process. The 2017 TCJA significantly altered the U.S. tax code, with many individual tax provisions designed to expire at the end of 2025. The expiration of these provisions would lead to an estimated $4.6 trillion in new revenue over the next decade if not extended, which Republicans view as a politically unpopular "tax increase." The budget reconciliation process has been a frequently used legislative tool in recent decades for passing significant fiscal legislation with simple majorities, particularly when one party controls both chambers of Congress. Republicans used it for the 2017 tax cuts, and Democrats for the COVID-19 relief package and the Inflation Reduction Act. Key stakeholders include President Donald Trump, House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-LA), House Budget Committee Chair Jodey Arrington (R-TX), and Senate Budget Committee Chair Lindsey Graham (R-SC). Divisions exist within the Republican party, particularly between fiscal conservatives (deficit hawks) and those prioritizing the extension of tax cuts. The budget reconciliation process is a special legislative procedure allowing certain budget-related legislation to pass the Senate with a simple majority. The concurrent budget resolution is the first step in this process, setting overall spending and revenue targets and including "reconciliation instructions" that direct specific committees to draft legislation achieving certain budgetary outcomes. This resolution is non-binding and does not become law, but it enables the subsequent reconciliation bill. The budget resolution in question is for Fiscal Year 2026, which begins on October 1, 2025.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include Congress.gov for official legislative information, bill text, status, and votes. The House Budget Committee Website and Senate Budget Committee Website are official sources for their respective budget resolutions, markups, reports, and press releases. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO) provides independent analyses of budget and economic issues, including cost estimates and baseline projections. Major news outlets (e.g., The New York Times, The Wall Street Journal, Politico, Axios, NPR, Fox News) offer real-time reporting, analysis, and statements from lawmakers. Think tanks and policy organizations (e.g., Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget, Tax Policy Center) provide detailed analysis. The President's Budget Request is typically released in February. CBO Baseline Projections are usually released multiple times a year. House and Senate Budget Committee markups occur as committees draft and approve resolutions. Floor votes are scheduled by chamber leadership. New information will become available through official press releases and statements from congressional leadership and committees, updates on Congress.gov, and reporting from congressional correspondents.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of 2025-02-20, no concurrent budget resolution for Fiscal Year 2026 with tax reconciliation instructions has been adopted by Congress. The House of Representatives had not yet passed its budget resolution, though it was anticipated to vote on a resolution in the coming days/weeks. The Senate Budget Committee Chair Lindsey Graham (R-SC) had introduced a budget resolution on February 12, 2025. Historically, the process of adopting a concurrent budget resolution is complex and often misses the statutory April 15 deadline. For example, the budget resolution enabling the 2017 TCJA was passed in October 2017. The Senate's "vote-a-rama" can significantly extend the time required. As of 2025-02-20, specific public projections or forecasts regarding the exact passage date of the FY2026 concurrent budget resolution with reconciliation instructions by May 15, 2025, are not widely available in the provided search results. However, the general sentiment from reporting indicates that passing such a resolution, especially with internal party divisions, is a challenging and often delayed process.

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution more likely (by May 15, 2025) include unified Republican control of Congress, the looming expiration of TCJA provisions creating urgency, presidential priority for extending tax cuts, and the use of reconciliation to bypass the Senate filibuster. An early start to the budget process, with the Senate introducing a resolution in mid-February and the House aiming for a vote around the same time, could theoretically allow for earlier completion. Factors that make a NO resolution more likely (by May 15, 2025) include internal Republican divisions over spending levels and the overall budget framework, potential differences between House and Senate approaches (e.g., baselines, scope of reconciliation package), procedural hurdles and delays inherent in the budget resolution process (e.g., Senate 'vote-a-ramas'), competing legislative priorities that could be tied into the budget resolution, and the historical tendency for Congress to miss the April 15 statutory deadline for budget resolutions.

## Sources
- https://www.forbes.com/sites/lynnmucenskikeck/2025/02/27/republicans-first-win-for-2025-federal-tax-reform/
- https://nypost.com/2025/02/24/us-news/house-gop-advances-budget-framework-for-border-energy-and-tax-priorities-for-final-vote/?utm_campaign=nypost&utm_medium=referral
- https://www.govexec.com/management/2025/02/house-republicans-overcome-own-members-doubts-push-through-sweeping-budget/403282/?oref=ge-featured-river-top
- https://www.stlpr.org/npr/2025-02-25/house-republicans-pass-budget-resolution-clearing-a-key-early-test-for-trump-agenda
- https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2025/02/25/trump-presidency-news/
- https://www.forbes.com/sites/andrewleahey/2025/03/17/trump-tax-cuts-explained-what-provisions-of-the-tcja-expire-in-2025/
- https://www.wcbu.org/npr-news/2025-02-25/reconciliation-is-the-key-to-unlocking-trumps-agenda-heres-how-it-works
- https://www.foxnews.com/politics/house-budget-chairman-reveals-how-republicans-will-pay-for-war-iran.amp
questions.resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, by 11:59 PM ET on May 15, 2025, both the U.S. House of Representatives and the U.S. Senate have passed the same concurrent budget resolution for Fiscal Year 2026, and that resolution includes specific reconciliation instructions directing one or more committees to report legislation that would change revenues (i.e., tax reconciliation instructions). This question resolves NO otherwise. Definitions: Concurrent Budget Resolution: A resolution agreed to by both the House and Senate that sets forth the congressional budget for the upcoming fiscal year(s). It does not require the President's signature and does not become law. It is identified by a unique number (e.g., S.Con.Res. 1 or H.Con.Res. 1). Tax Reconciliation Instructions: Directives within a concurrent budget resolution that instruct specific committees (e.g., House Ways and Means Committee, Senate Finance Committee) to report legislation that changes existing law to achieve specified changes in revenues, outlays, or the public debt limit. For this question, the instructions must explicitly refer to changes in revenues or taxes.
questions.resolution_source: The official legislative status and text of the concurrent budget resolution will be monitored on Congress.gov (https://www.congress.gov). The "Actions" tab for the relevant concurrent resolution will be checked to confirm passage by both chambers. The "Text" tab will be reviewed to verify the inclusion of tax reconciliation instructions.
questions.fine_print: Partial Action: If a concurrent budget resolution is adopted but does not contain tax reconciliation instructions, or if only one chamber adopts such a resolution, the question resolves NO. Reversal: If a concurrent budget resolution with tax reconciliation instructions is adopted by both chambers by the deadline, but is subsequently rescinded or amended after May 15, 2025, the question will still resolve YES. The resolution is based on the status as of the deadline. Subordinate Action: The adoption of a concurrent budget resolution requires a vote by the full House and Senate. Actions by committees or individual members do not count as adoption by Congress. Source Unavailability: If Congress.gov is temporarily unavailable at the resolution date, an attempt will be made to resolve the question using alternative official sources such as the Clerk of the House website (https://clerk.house.gov/) or the Secretary of the Senate website (https://www.senate.gov/legislative/clerk.htm). If these are also unavailable, the resolution date will be extended by 24 hours until the source becomes available. Government Shutdown: A government shutdown that delays the updating of Congress.gov or other official sources will result in the resolution date being extended until the necessary information is publicly available. Different Legal Form: The question specifically asks for a "concurrent budget resolution." If Congress adopts tax reconciliation instructions through a different legislative vehicle (e.g., a standalone bill, a joint resolution that becomes law), the question resolves NO. It must be a concurrent budget resolution.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: Historically, Congress frequently misses the statutory April 15 deadline for adopting a concurrent budget resolution. For example, the concurrent budget resolution that enabled the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act was not adopted until October 2017. While reconciliation has been used for significant legislation in various years (e.g., 2010, 2017, 2021, 2022), the adoption of the concurrent budget resolution with reconciliation instructions by May 15 of the fiscal year for which it applies appears to be uncommon. Based on the known delays and the specific example of the 2017 TCJA, the historical frequency of a "YES" resolution by May 15 is likely low.

questions.title: By April 30, 2025, what is the House's most significant action to extend the 2017 tax cuts?
questions.background:
The Trump administration has made extending the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) a cornerstone of its economic agenda for 2025. With Republican control of both chambers of Congress, the party is positioned to act decisively before the law's key provisions expire at the end of the year. However, as of February 20, 2025, the precise legislative strategy remains a subject of debate.

There are several paths leadership could take. An aggressive approach might involve introducing a comprehensive bill immediately to make the cuts permanent, signaling a clear and uncompromising stance. A more methodical strategy might focus first on passing a budget resolution to unlock the powerful reconciliation process. Alternatively, leaders could test the waters by introducing a bill to extend only the most popular provisions, or a short-term extension to buy more time for negotiation. The path chosen will be a key indicator of the administration's legislative priorities and its assessment of party unity.
questions.question_text: By April 30, 2025, what is the most significant legislative action taken by the House of Representatives to advance the extension of the 2017 tax cuts?
options: Introduce a bill for permanent and near-total extension
Introduce a bill for a partial or temporary extension
Pass a budget resolution with tax reconciliation instructions
Hold hearings or release a chairman's mark without introducing a bill
Take no significant action
Take no action
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of February 20, 2025, many key provisions of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) are set to expire at the end of 2025. These include lower federal income tax brackets, an increased standard deduction, and higher gift and estate tax exemptions. If these provisions are allowed to sunset, it is projected that over 60% of tax filers would face increased taxes. The Trump administration has made the extension of these tax cuts a central part of its economic agenda for 2025. (Source: Forbes, "2025’s Tax Time Bomb: GOP’s Last Chance To Prevent Economic Shock," published 2025-02-20). The TCJA, enacted in 2017, significantly altered the U.S. tax code. Many of its individual tax provisions were made temporary to comply with Senate budget rules (specifically, the Byrd Rule) which limit the long-term deficit impact of reconciliation bills. The impending expiration has created a "tax time bomb" scenario, prompting a push from Republicans to make these cuts permanent. (Source: FactCheck.org, "Both Sides Spin Who Would Benefit from Extending Trump Tax Cuts," published 2025-03-12 - Note: Published after 2025-02-20 but reflects the underlying context). Key stakeholders include House of Representatives Republicans, who hold a slim majority (218 Republicans to 215 Democrats as of January 20, 2025, indicating a very narrow margin for passing legislation; Source: The New York Times, "The House Republican Majority Is Down to Almost Nothing," published 2026-01-13 - Note: Published after 2025-02-20 but provides the most precise numbers for early 2025). The Trump Administration has prioritized making the 2017 tax cuts permanent and has advocated for additional tax breaks. (Source: Politico, "Trump and GOP leaders discuss using tariffs to pay for agenda," published 2025-02-27 - Note: Published after 2025-02-20 but reflects the ongoing agenda). The House Ways and Means Committee is the primary committee responsible for tax legislation. Senate Republicans also hold a majority (53-47 as of late 2025, suggesting a similar balance in early 2025; Source: Newsweek, "Five Senate Seats Most Likely to Flip from Republicans to Democrats in 2026," published 2025-12-27 - Note: Published after 2025-02-20 but indicates the likely control for early 2025). Democrats generally oppose extending the tax cuts, arguing they disproportionately benefit the wealthy and add to the national debt. The primary legislative tool for extending the tax cuts is expected to be budget reconciliation. This process allows certain budget-related bills to pass the Senate with a simple majority (51 votes) instead of the usual 60, bypassing a filibuster, but is subject to the "Byrd Rule." (Source: KUAR, WBHM, Peoria Public Radio, "Reconciliation is the key to unlocking Trump’s agenda. Here’s how it works," all published 2025-02-25 - Note: Published after 2025-02-20 but describes a stable legislative process).

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include Congress.gov for tracking federal legislation, the House Ways and Means Committee website for hearings and proposed tax legislation, and the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) and Joint Committee on Taxation (JCT) for non-partisan analyses of budgetary and economic effects. All listed sources are publicly accessible online. Legislative actions (bill introductions, committee votes, floor votes) are typically updated on Congress.gov and reported by news outlets immediately as they occur. CBO and JCT reports are published as legislative proposals are introduced. Committee schedules for hearings and markups are usually announced in advance on committee websites. New information will primarily emerge from announcements and legislative actions by the House Ways and Means Committee, floor debates and votes in the House of Representatives, statements and press conferences from House leadership and the Trump administration, and reports from the CBO and JCT on the budgetary impact of proposed legislation.

## Recent Numbers and Events
Many individual and business tax provisions from the 2017 TCJA are set to expire on December 31, 2025. Extending these expiring provisions is projected to cost approximately $4.5 trillion over the next decade (2025-2034). (Source: Forbes, "No Matter The Accounting, Extending The 2017 Tax Cuts Will Cost Over $4 Trillion," published 2025-02-24 - Note: Published after 2025-02-20 but reflects analysis available around the date). The 2017 TCJA was enacted with temporary provisions, and without action, these would expire as scheduled. Economic analyses from think tanks project significant increases in the federal deficit if the tax cuts are extended without corresponding spending cuts or revenue increases. For example, the Tax Foundation projected a $4.5 trillion decrease in federal tax revenue from 2025-2034 if the expiring TCJA provisions are extended. (Source: Tax Foundation, "Budget Reconciliation: Tracking the 2025 Trump Tax Cuts," published 2025-03-06 - Note: Published after 2025-02-20 but reflects analysis available around the date).

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution (significant action to extend tax cuts) more likely include: Republican control of the House and Senate, strong stated agenda from the Trump administration, the "tax hike" narrative if cuts expire, the availability of the budget reconciliation process to bypass the Senate filibuster, and arguments that tax cuts stimulate economic growth. Factors that make a NO resolution (no significant action or limited action) more likely include: High projected cost of extension ($4.5 trillion over 10 years) increasing the federal deficit, Democratic opposition arguing disproportionate benefits to the wealthy, intra-party divisions within the Republican party on scope and funding, Byrd Rule limitations in reconciliation, and the narrow Republican majorities requiring careful negotiation to avoid defections.

## Sources
- https://www.forbes.com/sites/steveforbes/2025/02/20/2025s-tax-time-bomb-gops-last-chance-to-prevent-economic-shock/
- https://www.factcheck.org/2025/03/both-sides-spin-who-would-benefit-from-extending-trump-tax-cuts/
- https://www.nytimes.com/2026/01/13/us/politics/congress-house-republicans-majority.html
- https://www.politico.com/live-updates/2025/02/27/congress/trump-tariffs-gop-agenda-budget-00206556
- https://www.newsweek.com/five-senate-seats-most-likely-to-flip-from-republicans-to-democrats-in-2026-11268917
- https://www.ualrpublicradio.org/npr-news/2025-02-25/reconciliation-is-the-key-to-unlocking-trumps-agenda-heres-how-it-works?_amp=true
- https://wbhm.org/npr-story/reconciliation-is-the-key-to-unlocking-trumps-agenda-heres-how-it-works/
- https://www.wcbu.org/npr-news/2025-02-25/reconciliation-is-the-key-to-unlocking-trumps-agenda-heres-how-it-works
- https://www.forbes.com/sites/howardgleckman/2025/02/24/no-matter-the-accounting-extending-the-2017-tax-cuts-will-cost-over-4-trillion/
- https://taxfoundation.org/research/all/federal/trump-tax-cuts-2025-budget-reconciliation/
research_flags: HALLUCINATED_FACTS: Several key pieces of information, such as the precise House/Senate party control numbers for early 2025, the exact details of the House budget resolution, and detailed explanations of the reconciliation process, are sourced from articles published *just after* the 2025-02-20 cutoff date (e.g., Feb 24, Feb 25, Feb 26, Feb 27, March 3, March 6, March 12, 2025). While these dates are very close to the cutoff and likely reflect the prevailing understanding and ongoing developments as of 2025-02-20, they technically fall outside the strict 'as of' constraint. This is flagged as 'hallucinated facts' because the precise details from these future-dated articles cannot be definitively confirmed as 'known' on 2025-02-20, even if the general trends and intentions were clear.
questions.resolution_criteria:
This question asks for the most significant legislative action taken by the U.S. House of Representatives by April 30, 2025, 11:59 PM ET, to advance the extension of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA). The question will resolve to the option corresponding to the highest-ranking action on the following list that meets its criteria. If multiple actions meeting the criteria for different options occur, the question resolves to the option highest on the list (A > C > B > D > E > F).

*   **A. Introduce a bill for permanent and near-total extension:** Resolves as A if, by the resolution date, a bill is formally introduced in the U.S. House of Representatives that proposes to extend, without a specified expiration date, at least the following three provisions of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act:
    1.  Individual income tax rates (maintaining the rates enacted by the TCJA).
    2.  The increased standard deduction.
    3.  The increased gift and estate tax exemptions.
    The bill must be primarily focused on extending these tax cuts.
*   **B. Introduce a bill for a partial or temporary extension:** Resolves as B if, by the resolution date, a bill is formally introduced in the U.S. House of Representatives that proposes to extend:
    *   Some, but not all, of the three provisions listed in Option A (individual income tax rates, increased standard deduction, increased gift and estate tax exemptions), without a specified expiration date; OR
    *   Any of the three provisions listed in Option A, or other expiring provisions of the TCJA, with a specified expiration date on or before December 31, 2034.
    This option resolves only if Option A's criteria are not met. The bill must be primarily focused on extending these tax cuts.
*   **C. Pass a budget resolution with tax reconciliation instructions:** Resolves as C if, by the resolution date, the U.S. House of Representatives passes a concurrent resolution on the budget for Fiscal Year 2026 or later that includes reconciliation instructions specifically directing the House Ways and Means Committee to report legislation making changes to tax law related to the extension of the 2017 TCJA provisions. This option resolves only if Option A or B's criteria are not met.
*   **D. Hold hearings or release a chairman's mark without introducing a bill:** Resolves as D if, by the resolution date, the House Ways and Means Committee:
    *   Holds at least one official hearing primarily focused on the extension of the 2017 TCJA provisions; OR
    *   Publicly releases a "chairman's mark" (draft legislative text) primarily focused on the extension of the 2017 TCJA provisions, without a corresponding bill being formally introduced in the House.
    This option resolves only if Option A, B, or C's criteria are not met.
*   **E. Take no significant action:** Resolves as E if, by the resolution date, none of the criteria for Options A, B, C, or D are met, but there is public discussion or statements from House leadership or the Trump administration indicating an intent to pursue extension of the 2017 tax cuts at a later date, or other minor actions (e.g., internal strategy meetings) occur that do not meet the thresholds of A-D.
*   **F. Take no action:** Resolves as F if, by the resolution date, none of the criteria for Options A, B, C, D, or E are met, meaning there is no discernible public action or stated intent from the House of Representatives or the Trump administration regarding the extension of the 2017 tax cuts.

**Definitions:**
*   **Bill**: A legislative proposal introduced in the U.S. House of Representatives.
*   **Formally introduced**: A bill is considered formally introduced when it is assigned a bill number (e.g., H.R. XXXX) and entered into the legislative record on Congress.gov.
*   **Primarily focused**: The main purpose and content of the bill, hearing, or chairman's mark must be the extension of the 2017 TCJA provisions, as determined by its title, summary, and legislative text.
*   **Without a specified expiration date**: The legislative text does not include a sunset clause or an explicit date by which the extended provisions would cease to be in effect.
*   **Concurrent resolution on the budget**: A legislative measure passed by both the House and Senate that sets forth the congressional budget for a fiscal year.
*   **Reconciliation instructions**: Directives included in a budget resolution that instruct specific committees to report legislation that changes existing law to achieve the spending and revenue targets set in the budget resolution.
*   **Official hearing**: A formal meeting of a committee or subcommittee to gather information, hear testimony, and consider legislative matters.
*   **Chairman's mark**: A draft bill or legislative text prepared by the committee chair for consideration during a committee markup.
questions.resolution_source: The primary resolution source will be **Congress.gov** (https://www.congress.gov/) for tracking bill introductions, legislative status, and budget resolutions. For details on hearings and chairman's marks, the **House Ways and Means Committee website** (e.g., https://waysandmeans.house.gov/) will be used. In cases where official sources are delayed or unclear, reputable news organizations (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, Politico, The New York Times, The Wall Street Journal) will be used as supplementary sources to confirm events.
questions.fine_print:
*   **Partial Action**: The resolution criteria for Options A and B explicitly define "permanent and near-total extension" and "partial or temporary extension," addressing different scopes and durations of action. The hierarchy of options ensures resolution to the most significant action.
*   **Action Reversed**: An action counts if it is formally taken by the resolution date. If an action is subsequently reversed or withdrawn *after* April 30, 2025, it will still count for the purpose of this question's resolution. If an action is reversed or withdrawn *before* April 30, 2025, it will not be considered for resolution unless it is still the most significant action standing by the resolution date.
*   **Action by Subordinate**: Actions taken by the House Ways and Means Committee (e.g., holding hearings, releasing a chairman's mark) or by individual House members (e.g., introducing a bill) are considered actions of the House of Representatives for the purpose of this question.
*   **Source Unavailable**: If Congress.gov or the House Ways and Means Committee website is temporarily unavailable, archived versions (e.g., via the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine) or comprehensive reporting from multiple reputable news organizations will be used.
*   **Government Shutdown**: In the event of a government shutdown that delays official publication on Congress.gov or the House Ways and Means Committee website, resolution will be based on the best available information from reputable news sources by the resolution date. If no definitive information is available by the resolution date, the question will resolve to the lowest applicable option (E or F).
*   **Different Legal Form**: The options cover the most likely legislative and pre-legislative actions. If the House takes a significant action to advance the extension of the 2017 tax cuts that does not fit neatly into Options A-D, it will be evaluated against the criteria for Option E ("Take no significant action") or F ("Take no action") based on its public visibility and impact.
questions.base_rate_reasoning:
Major tax legislation, including extensions of expiring provisions, is a recurring feature of the U.S. legislative landscape. While comprehensive overhauls like the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) may take longer to fully develop and pass (e.g., the TCJA bill was introduced in November 2017), significant initial actions often occur early in a new Congress or administration, especially when tax provisions are set to expire. For example:
*   In early 2001, following the presidential election, discussions and initial legislative steps for the Bush tax cuts (Economic Growth and Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2001) began, with President Bush pitching his tax-cut plan in February 2001.
*   In January 2024, the House Ways and Means Committee approved a significant bipartisan tax package that included child tax credit expansion and business tax breaks, which subsequently passed the House. This demonstrates that the House is capable of taking substantial legislative action on tax policy within the first few months of a year.

Given the strong stated agenda of the Trump administration to extend the 2017 tax cuts and Republican control of both chambers of Congress, there is a clear incentive and pathway for early action.

questions.title: Will a bill making tip income exempt from federal income tax pass a chamber of Congress by April 15, 2025?
questions.background:
During the 2024 campaign, President Trump made a popular pledge to eliminate federal income taxes on tips for service industry workers, a promise he reiterated upon taking office. As of mid-January 2025, he has stated he will work with Congress in the "coming weeks" to pass legislation.
