The proposal has some bipartisan appeal, as similar ideas were floated by Democrats during the campaign.

However, the new administration faces a busy legislative calendar and very narrow Republican majorities in both the House and Senate. Any legislative effort could be slowed by procedural hurdles, disagreements over the details (e.g., income caps for eligibility), or by being attached to a larger, more controversial tax package. It remains to be seen whether this popular promise can be translated into a swift legislative victory.
questions.question_text: Will a bill that makes tip income exempt from federal income tax pass at least one chamber of the U.S. Congress by April 15, 2025?
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of January 15, 2025, federal income tax is generally levied on tip income. President Donald Trump, following his 2024 campaign pledge, has expressed his intention to eliminate federal income taxes on tips for service industry workers. He has indicated a plan to work with Congress in the "coming weeks" to pass relevant legislation. A bill titled the "No Tax on Tips Act" was introduced in the U.S. Senate in January 2025 by Senator Ted Cruz (R-Texas) and a bipartisan group of co-sponsors, including Senators Jacky Rosen (D-Nevada) and Catherine Cortez Masto (D-Nevada). The concept of exempting tip income from federal taxation gained significant traction during the 2024 presidential campaign. Any change to federal income tax on tips would require an act of Congress to amend the Internal Revenue Code, involving introduction, committee review, and floor votes in both the House and Senate. Key stakeholders include President Trump, the Republican and Democratic parties in Congress, service industry workers, hospitality associations, and the Treasury Department/IRS. The 119th U.S. Congress convened in early January 2025, with Republicans holding narrow majorities in both the House and Senate.

## Data and Information
The primary source for tracking the legislative status of a bill, including its introduction, committee progress, and passage through either chamber of Congress, is Congress.gov. Official websites for the U.S. House of Representatives and the U.S. Senate also provide legislative calendars, committee hearing schedules, and floor vote information. The Congressional Record offers a daily account of debates and proceedings. News outlets such as Associated Press, Reuters, Politico, The New York Times, and The Wall Street Journal will report on legislative developments and statements from key political figures. Legislative actions are typically updated on official government websites shortly after they occur, and news reports follow these developments.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of January 15, 2025, a bill titled the "No Tax on Tips Act" was introduced in the U.S. Senate in January 2025. However, no bill making tip income exempt from federal income tax had passed either chamber of the U.S. Congress by this date. News reports discussing the Senate's passage of such a bill are dated May 2025, indicating that the event had not occurred by the specified resolution date of April 15, 2025. There is no historical precedent of a similar federal bill passing a chamber in recent prior periods. Specific projections or forecasts regarding the likelihood of this particular bill passing a chamber by April 15, 2025, are not readily available as of today's date, but the legislative environment is characterized by a busy calendar and narrow party majorities.

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution more likely: Presidential priority and strong advocacy from President Trump. Bipartisan co-sponsorship of the introduced "No Tax on Tips Act" in the Senate, suggesting potential for broader support. General public popularity of tax relief for service industry workers, which could create political pressure. Arguments that the exemption could stimulate the service industry and increase workers' take-home pay. Factors that make a NO resolution more likely: A busy legislative calendar for the new administration and Congress. Narrow Republican majorities in both the House and Senate, making it challenging to pass legislation without significant bipartisan consensus or overcoming procedural hurdles. Potential for disagreements over specific details of the bill, such as income caps for eligibility or the scope of qualifying tips. The possibility of the bill being attached to a larger, more controversial tax package, increasing its complexity and potential for opposition. Concerns over the fiscal impact of reduced federal revenue from the tax exemption.

## Sources
- https://www.aol.com/heres-much-youll-pay-taxes-091502675.html
- https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2025/may/21/senate-passes-no-tax-on-tips-bill
- https://www.huffpost.com/entry/senate-passes-trump-campaign-promise-bipartisan-support_n_682d02a1e4b060af36950fef?origin=top-ad-recirc
- https://www.nbcnews.com/politics/congress/senate-unexpectedly-passes-no-tax-tips-act-unanimous-vote-rcna208093
questions.resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if a bill that makes tip income exempt from federal income tax passes at least one chamber of the U.S. Congress by April 15, 2025, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, it resolves NO. For the purpose of this question: "Bill": A legislative proposal formally introduced in either the U.S. House of Representatives or the U.S. Senate, assigned a bill number (e.g., H.R. 123, S. 456). This also includes joint resolutions (H.J.Res. or S.J.Res.) that have the force of law. Concurrent resolutions (H.Con.Res. or S.Con.Res.) and simple resolutions (H.Res. or S.Res.) will NOT count. "Tip income exempt from federal income tax": The bill must explicitly state that tip income, or a portion thereof, is not subject to federal income tax under the Internal Revenue Code. It must amend or create a provision in the Internal Revenue Code to achieve this exemption. "Pass a chamber of Congress": A bill is considered to have "passed" a chamber if it receives a majority vote in either the U.S. House of Representatives or the U.S. Senate on a roll call vote or voice vote, and this passage is recorded in the official legislative record of that chamber. This includes passage by unanimous consent, if recorded as such. It does NOT include passage out of committee, or procedural votes that do not constitute final passage of the bill in that chamber. "By April 15, 2025": The vote for passage must occur on or before April 15, 2025, 11:59 PM ET.
questions.resolution_source: Congress.gov (www.congress.gov). The "Actions" tab for the relevant bill will be checked to confirm passage by either the House or Senate.
questions.fine_print: Partial Exemption: If a bill passes that only partially exempts tip income (e.g., only tips up to a certain amount, or only for certain service industry workers), it will still resolve YES, as long as some tip income is made exempt from federal income tax. Reversal: If a bill passes a chamber by the resolution date but is later reversed, repealed, or fails to pass the other chamber, it will still resolve YES. The resolution is based on the event occurring by the specified date. Different Legal Form: The action must be in the form of a bill (H.R. or S.) or a joint resolution (H.J.Res. or S.J.Res.) that has the force of law. Other legislative vehicles will not count. Source Unavailability: If Congress.gov is temporarily unavailable, official legislative records from the Clerk of the House (clerk.house.gov) or the Secretary of the Senate (www.senate.gov/legislative/Public_Records.htm) will be used. If all official government sources are unavailable for an extended period, reputable news sources (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, Politico, The New York Times, The Wall Street Journal) will be used to determine if a bill passed a chamber. Government Shutdown: A government shutdown that delays the updating of Congress.gov or other official sources will not extend the resolution date. The resolution will be based on the information available by the resolution date, using the fallback sources if necessary.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: While tax-related legislation, including bills providing tax credits or deductions, passes chambers of Congress with some regularity, bills that make a specific type of income entirely exempt from federal income tax are less common. The introduction of the "No Tax on Tips Act" with bipartisan co-sponsorship, coupled with presidential support, indicates a specific legislative push. However, the legislative calendar is busy, and narrow majorities exist. Considering the rarity of full income exemptions but the presence of a specific legislative vehicle and political will, a base rate reflecting a low but non-negligible probability is appropriate.

questions.title: What will be the Trump administration's first formal legislative proposal on worker tax cuts by March 31, 2025?
questions.background:
President Trump has publicly called for eliminating federal taxes on several sources of income, most notably tips, overtime pay, and Social Security benefits. While popular, these proposals carry significantly different fiscal costs and political risks. Eliminating the tax on tips is seen as the most politically straightforward, while changes to overtime pay are more complex, and altering the tax on Social Security benefits would be a major, and likely contentious, policy shift.

With a narrow governing majority, the administration must choose its strategy carefully. Will it pursue a single, popular item to secure a quick win, or will it bundle its priorities into a larger, more ambitious package? The first formal proposal will be a key signal of the administration's legislative strategy for the coming term.
questions.question_text: By March 31, 2025, which of the following will be the first legislative action on tax cuts for workers formally backed by the Trump administration?
options: Administration backs a bill to eliminate federal income tax on tips, overtime, and Social Security benefits
Administration backs a bill to eliminate federal income tax on tips and overtime pay only
Administration backs a bill to eliminate federal income tax on tips only
Administration backs a bill proposing targeted tax credits for service/tipped workers instead of a full tax exemption
Take no action to formally back a bill addressing these specific tax cuts
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of January 15, 2025, Donald Trump has won the presidential election and is preparing to assume office for his second term. His campaign included prominent pledges to implement significant tax cuts for workers. These proposals are currently in the realm of stated policy intentions and campaign promises rather than formalized legislative actions. Throughout his 2024 presidential campaign, Donald Trump consistently advocated for eliminating federal income taxes on several sources of income, most notably tips, overtime pay, and Social Security benefits. These pledges were a central component of his economic platform. Post-election, there have been indications from his incoming administration and Republican allies in Congress that these tax cuts will be a legislative priority. Trump's press secretary, Karoline Leavitt, has communicated that President Trump's tax priorities include "No tax on tips," "No tax on seniors' Social Security," and "No tax on overtime pay." These priorities were reportedly laid out to GOP lawmakers during a meeting at the White House, indicating a push towards legislative action. (Source: Newsweek, referencing events prior to Feb 7, 2025, https://www.newsweek.com/social-security-boost-proposed-millions-americans-2027678; The Associated Press, Jan 19, 2025, https://apnews.com/article/trump-second-term-policies-immigration-economy-tariffs-f9d55c34a95054cd224a0b295d88fbde) Trump has expressed a desire for these tax cuts to be included in a "one big beautiful bill" and has indicated that a budget reconciliation bill would be the preferred legislative vehicle to pass these measures with bare majorities in both the House and Senate. (Source: New York Post, referencing statements prior to Jan 31, 2025, https://nypost.com/2025/01/31/us-news/when-will-trump-end-taxes-on-tips-ot-and-social-security-white-house-says-gop-must-put-in-reconciliation-bill/) Key Stakeholders and Players Involved include the Trump Administration (President Donald Trump, his Treasury Secretary, and White House press staff), the U.S. Congress (Republican leadership in both the House and Senate, such as Speaker Mike Johnson and Senate Majority Leader John Thune), and various interest groups (representing service workers, retirees, etc.). Legislative proposals must be introduced in either the House of Representatives or the Senate. For tax-related bills, they typically originate in the House Ways and Means Committee. The budget reconciliation process is a procedural tool that allows certain budget-related legislation (including tax bills) to pass the Senate with a simple majority (51 votes) rather than the usual 60-vote threshold, making it a viable path for a new administration with a narrow governing majority.

## Data and Information
Relevant Data Sources include White House Official Channels (press briefings, official statements, legislative agendas), Congressional Records (Congress.gov for tracking bills, House Ways and Means Committee, Senate Finance Committee websites), and Major News Outlets (Associated Press, Reuters, New York Times, Wall Street Journal, Politico, Axios, Newsweek, New York Post). Think Tanks and Policy Organizations (e.g., Tax Foundation, Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget) will also provide analysis. White House announcements are made as events unfold, without a fixed schedule. Congressional legislative actions follow the legislative calendar, with bills introduced at various times. News outlets publish continuously. New information is expected from official White House press conferences and statements, the introduction of specific bills in the House or Senate, statements from House and Senate leadership regarding legislative priorities and strategies, and reporting from political journalists and news agencies covering Capitol Hill and the White House.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of January 15, 2025, the proposals to eliminate federal income tax on tips, overtime pay, and Social Security benefits are primarily high-profile campaign promises and stated policy intentions of the incoming Trump administration. There is no evidence of a specific, formally introduced legislative bill encompassing these exact tax cuts that has been formally backed by the Trump administration as of January 15, 2025. While some reports mention potential future legislative vehicles like a reconciliation bill, no concrete bill has been identified as formally backed by the administration by this date. During the 2024 presidential campaign, Trump consistently advocated for these tax cuts. Economists and policy analysts have expressed skepticism regarding the fiscal implications of these broad tax cuts, particularly concerning their impact on national debt and the solvency of Social Security. Trump has proposed offsetting the cost through increased tariffs, a funding mechanism that has drawn skepticism from some economists. (Source: Newsweek, referencing campaign promises prior to Feb 7, 2025, https://www.newsweek.com/social-security-boost-proposed-millions-americans-2027678)

## Trends
Factors that make a broader proposal (Options A or B) more likely include a strong presidential mandate from Trump's consistent campaigning on these specific tax cuts, a desire for a "big win" early in the term by bundling popular tax cuts, the potential use of budget reconciliation to pass with a simple majority, and the broad public popularity of tax cuts for workers. Factors that make a narrower proposal (Options C or D) or no action (Option E) more likely include significant fiscal cost concerns about national debt and Social Security solvency, political complexity and potential contention (especially regarding Social Security changes), a strategic prioritization to secure a quick legislative win with a less controversial item (e.g., tips only), potential congressional disagreement on the scope and funding, the possibility of opting for targeted tax credits (Option D) as a compromise, and the uncertainty regarding the definitive legislative vehicle and congressional commitment to a broad package as of January 15, 2025.

## Sources
- https://www.newsweek.com/social-security-boost-proposed-millions-americans-2027678
- https://apnews.com/article/trump-second-term-policies-immigration-economy-tariffs-f9d55c34a95054cd224a0b295d88fbde
- https://nypost.com/2025/01/31/us-news/when-will-trump-end-taxes-on-tips-ot-and-social-security-white-house-says-gop-must-put-in-reconciliation-bill/
questions.resolution_criteria:
This question asks which of the listed options will be the first legislative proposal formally backed by the Trump administration by March 31, 2025.

**Definitions:**

*   **"Formally backed by the Trump administration"**: A legislative proposal will be considered "formally backed by the Trump administration" if, by March 31, 2025, any of the following occurs:
    1.  The President, Vice President, White House Press Secretary, or the Secretary of the Treasury publicly states that the administration officially supports a specific bill (identified by bill number, e.g., H.R. 1234 or S. 567) that has been introduced in either the U.S. House of Representatives or the U.S. Senate. This statement must be made in an official capacity (e.g., White House press briefing, official White House statement, official remarks).
    2.  The Office of Management and Budget (OMB) issues a Statement of Administration Policy (SAP) on a specific bill that has been introduced in either the U.S. House of Representatives or the U.S. Senate, explicitly endorsing its passage.
    3.  The White House officially transmits a draft legislative text for a bill to Congress for introduction, and this transmission is publicly acknowledged by the White House or congressional leadership.
*   **"Legislative proposal"**: A bill formally introduced in either the U.S. House of Representatives or the U.S. Senate, assigned a bill number (e.g., H.R. 1234, S. 567), and made publicly available on Congress.gov.
*   **"Eliminate federal income tax on tips"**: A bill that explicitly removes or exempts "tips" (as defined by the Internal Revenue Service, e.g., IRS Publication 531) from federal income taxation.
*   **"Eliminate federal income tax on overtime pay"**: A bill that explicitly removes or exempts "overtime pay" (as defined by the Fair Labor Standards Act or similar federal statute) from federal income taxation.
*   **"Eliminate federal income tax on Social Security benefits"**: A bill that explicitly removes or exempts "Social Security benefits" (as defined by the Social Security Act) from federal income taxation.
*   **"Targeted tax credits for service/tipped workers"**: A bill that proposes new or expanded federal tax credits specifically for individuals who receive tips or work in service industries, rather than a full exemption of tips from income tax.

**Resolution Order:**
The "first" formal legislative proposal will be determined by the earliest timestamp of any of the "formally backed" events listed above. If multiple bills are formally backed on the same calendar day (Eastern Time), the one introduced earliest in Congress (as per Congress.gov) will take precedence. If introduced on the same day, the one with the lowest bill number will take precedence.

**Resolution Conditions for each option:**

*   **A. Administration backs a bill to eliminate federal income tax on tips, overtime, and Social Security benefits**: Resolves as A if the first legislative proposal formally backed by the Trump administration, as defined above, includes provisions to eliminate federal income tax on *all three* of tips, overtime pay, and Social Security benefits.
*   **B. Administration backs a bill to eliminate federal income tax on tips and overtime pay only**: Resolves as B if the first legislative proposal formally backed by the Trump administration includes provisions to eliminate federal income tax on *both* tips and overtime pay, *but does not* include provisions to eliminate federal income tax on Social Security benefits.
*   **C. Administration backs a bill to eliminate federal income tax on tips only**: Resolves as C if the first legislative proposal formally backed by the Trump administration includes provisions to eliminate federal income tax on *tips only*, and does not include provisions to eliminate federal income tax on overtime pay or Social Security benefits.
*   **D. Administration backs a bill proposing targeted tax credits for service/tipped workers instead of a full tax exemption**: Resolves as D if the first legislative proposal formally backed by the Trump administration proposes new or expanded federal tax credits specifically for service/tipped workers, *and does not* include provisions to eliminate federal income tax on tips, overtime pay, or Social Security benefits.
*   **E. Take no action to formally back a bill addressing these specific tax cuts**: Resolves as E if, by March 31, 2025, the Trump administration has not formally backed any legislative proposal matching the descriptions of options A, B, C, or D.
questions.resolution_source:
1.  **Congress.gov**: For tracking introduced bills, bill numbers, dates of introduction, and bill text.
2.  **White House official website (whitehouse.gov)**: For official statements, press briefings, and official White House documents.
3.  **Office of Management and Budget (OMB) website (whitehouse.gov/omb/)**: For Statements of Administration Policy (SAPs).
4.  **Major News Outlets**: Reputable news agencies such as the Associated Press (APNews.com) and Reuters (Reuters.com) will be used to confirm public statements and official backing, especially if not immediately available on official government websites.
questions.fine_print:
*   **Partial Action**: If a legislative proposal includes some but not all of the specified tax cuts for an option, it will be evaluated based on the most comprehensive match among the options. For example, a bill eliminating tax on tips and *some* overtime pay would still fall under Option B if it meets the criteria for both tips and overtime. If a bill includes elements of multiple options (e.g., tips only, but also a minor credit for service workers), the option that most accurately describes the *primary* and *most comprehensive* tax cut proposal will be chosen. If ambiguity remains, the option that aligns with the most significant fiscal impact of the proposed tax cuts will take precedence.
*   **Action Reversed**: If the administration formally backs a proposal, and then later reverses its stance or withdraws support, the question will still resolve based on the *first* formal backing by March 31, 2025.
*   **Action by Subordinate**: The definition of "formally backed by the Trump administration" explicitly includes statements by the President, Vice President, White House Press Secretary, or the Secretary of the Treasury, or official OMB SAPs, or official White House transmission of legislative text. Actions by other subordinates or unofficial statements will not count.
*   **Resolution Source Temporarily Unavailable**: If a primary resolution source (e.g., Congress.gov, WhiteHouse.gov) is temporarily unavailable, resolution will be delayed until the source becomes available or until sufficient corroborating evidence from other official sources (e.g., official press releases archived elsewhere, confirmed by multiple major news outlets citing official sources) allows for unambiguous resolution.
*   **Government Shutdown**: A government shutdown may delay the publication of official documents or updates to government websites. In such a case, resolution will be delayed until official information becomes available. If the shutdown prevents any formal backing from being officially recorded by the deadline, Option E will be considered.
*   **Different Legal Form**: If the administration pursues these tax cuts through a different legal form than a "bill" (e.g., an executive order or regulatory change), it will not count as a "legislative proposal" for the purpose of this question, and Option E would be more likely unless a bill is also formally backed. The question specifically refers to "legislative proposal."
questions.base_rate_reasoning:
Historically, it is common for new presidential administrations to formally back legislative proposals, especially on key campaign promises, early in their term. Tax reform and cuts are frequently high on the agenda.

*   **Trump Administration (First Term, 2017)**: The Trump administration made tax reform a central promise. The "Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017" (H.R.1) was a major legislative priority and was formally backed by the administration early in its term. While the bill was introduced in the House in November 2017, the administration had been actively working on and advocating for tax reform throughout the year.
*   **Obama Administration (2009)**: The Obama administration's first major legislative push included the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009, which contained significant tax provisions. This bill was formally backed and signed into law shortly after his inauguration.
*   **Bush Administration (2001)**: The Bush administration quickly moved to enact tax cuts, with the Economic Growth and Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2001 (EGTRRA) being a key early legislative priority.

Given these precedents, it is highly likely that the Trump administration, having campaigned heavily on these specific tax cuts, will formally back a legislative proposal addressing them by March 31, 2025. The question is more about *which* specific package of tax cuts will be prioritized. The base rate of 0.85 reflects a high likelihood of *any* formal backing (Options A, B, C, or D) rather than no action (Option E). The likelihood of Option E (No action) is estimated to be low (0.1-0.2), unless significant unforeseen political or economic obstacles arise that completely derail the legislative agenda for these specific cuts.

questions.title: Will Donald Trump post on his personal @realDonaldTrump account on X (formerly Twitter) by April 1, 2025?
questions.background: Since his ban was lifted in late 2022, Donald Trump has not personally used his X account, which has over 87 million followers. He has been exclusively posting on Truth Social, the platform owned by Trump Media & Technology Group (TMTG), to which he has contractual obligations. However, the reach of X is exponentially greater than Truth Social. With Elon Musk, the owner of X, now a presidential advisor, speculation is rampant about whether Trump will return to the platform that was once his primary megaphone, and what such a move would mean for the future of TMTG. A return could signal a shift in communication strategy, prioritizing mass audience engagement over business loyalty. It remains to be seen whether contractual hurdles or strategic preferences will keep him exclusively on Truth Social.
questions.question_text: Will Donald Trump post on his personal @realDonaldTrump account on X (formerly Twitter) by April 1, 2025, 23:59 ET?
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
Status Quo (as of 2025-01-15): Donald Trump's personal @realDonaldTrump account on X (formerly Twitter) was reinstated in late 2022 by Elon Musk. However, as of January 15, 2025, Donald Trump has not personally posted on this account. He has exclusively used Truth Social, the platform owned by Trump Media & Technology Group (TMTG), for his social media communications. He frequently shares screenshots of X posts on Truth Social, but does not originate new content directly on X. Historical Context and Recent Developments: January 6, 2021: Donald Trump was permanently banned from Twitter (now X) and other major social media platforms following the U.S. Capitol riot, citing concerns about the risk of further incitement of violence. 2022: Trump Media & Technology Group (TMTG) launched Truth Social in early 2022, positioning it as a "free speech" alternative to mainstream platforms. Trump became its most prominent user. Late 2022: Elon Musk acquired Twitter and, in November 2022, reinstated Donald Trump's account after conducting a poll. Since Reinstatement (late 2022) to 2025-01-15: Despite his account being active, Trump has maintained his primary social media presence on Truth Social, adhering to a contractual agreement that influences his posting behavior on other platforms. Key Stakeholders and Players Involved: Donald Trump: The former President and current subject of the question. His communication strategy and business interests are central to the outcome. X (formerly Twitter) / Elon Musk: X is the platform in question, offering a significantly larger global audience (over 87 million followers for Trump's account). Elon Musk, as owner of X, reinstated Trump's account and is reportedly a presidential advisor (as per the proto-question). Truth Social / Trump Media & Technology Group (TMTG): Truth Social is Trump's owned platform, where he has been exclusively active. TMTG's financial success and valuation are heavily tied to Trump's engagement on the platform. Trump holds a significant stake in TMTG. Regulatory/Legal Framework: Truth Social Exclusivity Clause: As of 2025-01-15, Donald Trump is bound by an agreement with Truth Social (TMTG) that runs until February 2025. This agreement stipulates that he must wait six hours after posting on Truth Social before he can post any "non-political communications" on other social media platforms. Crucially, the agreement grants him "sole discretion" to determine whether a post is "politically related." As a candidate for president, most or all of his social media posts could be deemed by him to be politically related. (Source: The Sun Chronicle, ABC News, Chicago Tribune, all published March 22, 2024, referencing an agreement running until February 2025).

## Data and Information
Relevant Data Sources: Donald Trump's @realDonaldTrump account on X: This is the primary resolution source. Activity (or lack thereof) on this account will directly answer the question. (Access: twitter.com/realDonaldTrump) Donald Trump's Truth Social account: His activity here provides context on his current communication habits. (Access: truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump) News Media and Political Analysis: Major news outlets, political commentators, and financial news (especially those covering TMTG) will report on any significant changes in Trump's social media strategy. Trump Media & Technology Group (TMTG) Financial Filings: Public filings could provide details or updates on Trump's contractual obligations or TMTG's performance, which might influence his decisions. Access: All listed sources are publicly accessible online. Publication Schedule: Social media posts are real-time. News articles are published as events occur. Corporate financial filings follow a regular schedule (e.g., quarterly reports). Where to Expect New Information: Any new posts by Donald Trump would appear directly on his X or Truth Social accounts. Official statements regarding his social media strategy or TMTG's business would likely come from his campaign, TMTG, or be reported by major news organizations.

## Recent Numbers and Events
Current Number (as of 2025-01-15): Number of personal posts on @realDonaldTrump on X: Zero, since his reinstatement in late 2022. Followers on X (@realDonaldTrump): Over 87 million. Followers on Truth Social (@realDonaldTrump): Approximately 9-10 million (exact figure as of 2025-01-15 is not precisely sourced, but this is the general magnitude). Outcome in Prior Periods: Since his X account was reinstated in late 2022, Donald Trump has not personally posted on it. His social media activity has been exclusively on Truth Social. Relevant Projections, Forecasts, or Base Rates: The proto-question notes "speculation is rampant" about his return to X, driven by its greater reach and Elon Musk's role. The contractual agreement with Truth Social, running until February 2025, is a critical near-term factor. Its expiration or modification could significantly alter the likelihood of him posting on X.

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution more likely (Donald Trump posts on X): Greater Audience Reach: X offers a significantly larger audience (over 87 million followers) compared to Truth Social, which could be a powerful incentive for political campaigning and message dissemination, especially during an election cycle. Expiration/Modification of Truth Social Contract: The current contractual agreement with Truth Social, which restricts his posting on other platforms, expires in February 2025. After this date, Trump may have more freedom to post on X without direct contractual impediments, or the terms could be renegotiated to be less restrictive. "Political Communications" Discretion: The existing contract allows Trump "sole discretion" to determine what constitutes a "political communication." He could potentially use this loophole to justify posting on X if he deems the content political. Elon Musk's Influence: Elon Musk's ownership of X and reported advisory role to Trump could create a favorable environment for Trump's return to the platform. Strategic Shift for Campaign: Trump's campaign might decide that the benefits of reaching a broader X audience outweigh the loyalty or financial implications for Truth Social. Factors that make a NO resolution more likely (Donald Trump does not post on X): Contractual Obligations to Truth Social: Even if the specific clause expires in February 2025, a broader understanding of his commitment to TMTG and Truth Social might still exist. A full return to X could be perceived as undermining his own platform. Loyalty and Financial Interest in TMTG: Trump has a significant financial stake in TMTG. His exclusive use of Truth Social is crucial for driving user engagement, advertising revenue, and the overall valuation of his media company. Posting on X could devalue TMTG. Established Communication Channel: Truth Social has served as his primary communication platform since its launch, allowing him direct, unfiltered access to his base. His team may prefer to maintain this established and controlled channel. Control Over Content and Moderation: Truth Social offers Trump complete control over content and moderation policies, a level of control he would not have on X. Avoiding "Big Tech" Narrative: Remaining exclusively on Truth Social aligns with the narrative of rejecting "Big Tech" censorship, which resonates with a portion of his base.

## Sources
- https://www.thesunchronicle.com/business/what-is-truth-social-donald-trumps-social-media-platform/article_fa1edf2e-e2e1-5b70-8bd6-391846e9cae8.html
- https://abcnews.go.com/Business/wireStory/truth-social-donald-trumps-social-media-platform-108387551
- https://www.chicagotribune.com/2024/03/22/trump-social-media/
questions.resolution_criteria: Resolves YES if, at any point between 2025-01-15 00:00 ET and 2025-04-01 23:59 ET, Donald Trump's personal account, @realDonaldTrump, on X (formerly Twitter) publishes an original post. An "original post" is defined as any content (text, image, video, or link) that appears on the @realDonaldTrump timeline and is directly authored by the account, not a retweet, quote tweet, or reply to another user's post. Posts that are clearly identified as being made by staff (e.g., including "-Staff" or similar attribution) will NOT count as personal posts. Posts that are subsequently deleted will still count as a YES resolution if they were publicly visible on the timeline for any duration. Resolves NO otherwise.
questions.resolution_source: The primary resolution source will be the official X (formerly Twitter) account of Donald Trump: https://twitter.com/realDonaldTrump. In the event of ambiguity or dispute, reputable news organizations (e.g., Reuters, Associated Press, New York Times, Wall Street Journal) reporting on Donald Trump's social media activity will be consulted as secondary sources.
questions.fine_print: Account Suspension/Deletion: If the @realDonaldTrump account is permanently suspended or deleted before April 1, 2025, and no original posts meeting the criteria have been made, the question resolves NO. If the account is temporarily suspended but reinstated before the resolution date, the question remains active. Platform Name Change: If X changes its name again, the question still refers to the platform currently known as X where the @realDonaldTrump account resides. Technical Issues: If X experiences technical issues that prevent access to the account for an extended period around the resolution date, the resolution will be delayed until access is restored and the account's activity can be verified. Ambiguous Authorship: In cases where it is unclear whether a post is personal or by staff, the resolution will lean towards NO unless there is strong evidence (e.g., direct confirmation from Trump or his team, or a clear personal tone inconsistent with typical staff posts) indicating it is a personal post.
questions.base_rate_reasoning: Since his X account was reinstated in late 2022 up to today's date (2025-01-15), Donald Trump has not personally posted on his @realDonaldTrump account. Therefore, the historical frequency for this specific period is 0.0. Donald Trump has consistently used Truth Social as his primary platform due to contractual obligations and his ownership stake in TMTG. While speculation about his return to X is ongoing, his actions to date indicate a strong preference for Truth Social. The contractual agreement with Truth Social, which runs until February 2025, is a significant factor influencing this behavior.

questions.title: Will Donald Trump hold a formal, solo press conference in the White House's James S. Brady Press Briefing Room by March 15, 2025?
questions.background: Presidential press conferences from the White House briefing room are a long-standing tradition, offering a forum for the press to question the administration directly. During his first term, President Trump had a contentious relationship with the press corps and often preferred more informal interactions to structured press conferences. While he recently held a lengthy press conference at Mar-a-Lago as President-elect, it is an open question whether his administration will institutionalize formal, solo press conferences at the White House. Doing so would signal a willingness to engage with traditional media norms, while forgoing them would confirm a strategy of bypassing legacy media in favor of direct-to-supporter channels.
questions.question_text: Will Donald Trump hold a formal, solo press conference in the White House's James S. Brady Press Briefing Room by March 15, 2025?
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of 2025-01-15, Donald Trump is the President-elect of the United States, with his second inauguration scheduled for January 20, 2025. Presidential press conferences from the White House's James S. Brady Press Briefing Room are a long-standing tradition, offering a direct forum for media questioning. During his first term (2017-2021), President Trump maintained a contentious relationship with the press corps. He frequently opted for less formal interactions, such as impromptu Q&A sessions on the White House lawn, in the Oval Office, or aboard Air Force One, rather than structured, solo press conferences in the briefing room. One analysis indicates he held approximately 13 formal press conferences during his first term (Source: New York Post, published Feb 9, 2026, referencing first-term data). As President-elect, he has held press conferences at his Mar-a-Lago residence on December 16, 2024, and January 7, 2025, and also spoke to reporters before a New Year's Eve party there on December 31, 2024. Key stakeholders include Donald Trump, his incoming administration, the White House press corps, and various media organizations. There is no specific regulatory or legal framework dictating the frequency or format of presidential press conferences; it is largely a matter of presidential discretion and communication strategy.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include official White House announcements and schedules, transcripts and video recordings of presidential press conferences and media interactions, and reports from reputable news organizations and academic institutions that track presidential communications. These sources are publicly accessible via the White House website (once the new administration takes office) and the archives of major news outlets (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, New York Times, Washington Post, CNN, Fox News). Press conferences are unscheduled events, with transcripts and news reports typically published shortly after their occurrence. Analyses of presidential press engagement are published periodically by media watchdogs and academics. New information regarding press conferences will become available through White House Press Office announcements, live news coverage, and official White House social media channels.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of 2025-01-15, Donald Trump has held zero formal, solo press conferences in the White House's James S. Brady Press Briefing Room during his President-elect period or his nascent second term. In prior periods, specifically during his first term (2017-2021), he held an estimated 13 formal press conferences in the White House briefing room, a lower number compared to many previous presidents. Recent events as President-elect include press conferences at Mar-a-Lago on December 16, 2024, and January 7, 2025, and remarks to reporters on December 31, 2024, all outside the White House briefing room. Pre-inauguration analyses from late 2024 and early 2025 suggest a likely continuation of his first-term communication strategy, emphasizing direct channels and informal interactions over traditional formal press conferences (Source: The Hill, published Jan 1, 2025; CNN, published Dec 12, 2024; ABC News, published Nov 7, 2024).

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution (Donald Trump holds a formal, solo press conference in the White House's James S. Brady Press Briefing Room by March 15, 2025) more likely include: a strategic decision to project an image of traditional governance and engage with mainstream media to broaden appeal; pressure from advisors or political allies advocating for a more conventional communication approach; or the necessity to address a major national or international crisis with a formal presidential statement. Factors that make a NO resolution more likely include: Donald Trump's established preference for direct communication channels (e.g., social media, rallies) and informal Q&A sessions; his continued adversarial relationship with much of the mainstream media, leading him to avoid settings perceived as hostile; a belief that formal press conferences are not the most effective way to convey his message; or a strategy of delegating formal press interactions primarily to his White House Press Secretary.

## Sources
- https://www.thesunchronicle.com/news/nation_world/takeaways-from-trumps-mar-a-lago-press-conference/article_a22e43b3-42bc-571c-afb9-d3a164c13952.html
- https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/trump-make-remarks-mar-lago-2-weeks-taking/story?id=117416274
- https://www.bozemandailychronicle.com/ap_news/politics/trump-says-he-is-planning-to-attend-jimmy-carters-funeral/article_65f1bd66-ae8b-5acd-8985-c3c6edad4361.html
- https://www.chicagotribune.com/2024/12/31/trump-calls-it-the-center-of-the-universe-mar-a-lago-is-a-magnet-for-those-seeking-influence/
- https://nypost.com/2026/02/09/us-news/trump-nears-500-press-interactions-in-second-term-blowing-past-biden-data-show/
- https://thehill.com/opinion/white-house/5060011-democrats-self-recrimination-trump-election/
- https://www.cnn.com/2024/12/12/politics/video/the-lead-maggie-habberman-time-magazine-trump-cover-interview-jake-tapper
- https://abcnews.go.com/GMA/Shop/video/national-cash-back-day-115596047
questions.resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if Donald Trump holds a formal, solo press conference in the White House's James S. Brady Press Briefing Room by March 15, 2025, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, it resolves NO. For the purpose of this question: A "formal press conference" is defined as a scheduled event, announced by the White House Press Office, where President Donald Trump delivers opening remarks from a podium in the James S. Brady Press Briefing Room and subsequently takes questions from multiple credentialed journalists. This must be a dedicated event for the press, distinct from impromptu Q&A sessions, remarks made during other events (e.g., bill signings, cabinet meetings), or joint press conferences with other heads of state or officials. A "solo press conference" means that President Donald Trump is the sole principal speaker and answers questions alone, without other administration officials or foreign dignitaries sharing the podium or answering questions alongside him. The presence of the White House Press Secretary to introduce the President or conclude the event will not disqualify it as "solo." The "White House's James S. Brady Press Briefing Room" refers to the specific room located in the West Wing of the White House, identifiable by its distinctive setup (e.g., blue backdrop, presidential seal, podium).
questions.resolution_source: The primary resolution source will be official transcripts and video recordings published on the White House website (whitehouse.gov/briefings-statements/ or similar official section for press events). Secondary sources, to be used for corroboration or if the primary source is temporarily unavailable, include reports from major, reputable news organizations such as The Associated Press (AP), Reuters, The New York Times, The Washington Post, CNN, or Fox News, specifically their White House correspondent reports.
questions.fine_print: Partial Action: Not applicable. The event must meet all specified criteria to resolve as YES. Action Reversed: The question resolves based on whether the event *occurs* by the deadline. Any subsequent reversal or retraction of statements made during the press conference does not affect the resolution. Done by Subordinate: The press conference must be held by President Donald Trump himself. A press conference held by the White House Press Secretary or another administration official will not count towards a YES resolution. Source Temporarily Unavailable: If the primary source (whitehouse.gov) is temporarily unavailable, secondary sources will be used. Once the primary source becomes available, it will be consulted for final confirmation. Government Shutdown: A government shutdown may delay the publication of official transcripts on the White House website. In such a case, reputable news organizations (AP, Reuters, The New York Times, The Washington Post, CNN, Fox News) will serve as the primary resolution source until official transcripts become available. Different Legal Form: Not applicable. The question specifies a "formal, solo press conference" with clear definitional criteria.
questions.base_rate_reasoning:
During his first term (2017-2021), President Donald Trump held approximately 13 formal press conferences. Over a four-year (48-month) period, this averages to roughly 0.27 formal press conferences per month. For a two-month period (January 20, 2025, to March 15, 2025), the historical probability of at least one such event occurring, based on his first-term frequency, is approximately 47%. This is calculated by estimating the probability of *not* having a press conference in a given month (1 - 0.27 = 0.73) and then calculating the probability of *not* having one in two months (0.73^2 

0.53), leading to a 1 - 0.53 = 0.47 probability of at least one event. This suggests a historical base rate of around 0.47 (47%) for a YES resolution.

questions.title: What will be the primary channel for announcing major White House personnel nominations by April 1, 2025?
questions.background: A new presidential administration must make hundreds of key personnel appointments, including cabinet secretaries, ambassadors, and agency heads. The method chosen to first announce these nominations sets a tone for the administration's relationship with the public and the media. The choice pits the direct, unfiltered approach of personal social media against the traditional, institutional authority of official White House channels and formal events.
questions.question_text: By April 1, 2025, what will be the primary channel for the initial public announcement of major White House personnel nominations?
options: Posts from Donald Trump's personal social media accounts (e.g., Truth Social, X).
Official White House press releases or Press Secretary statements.
Formal press conferences or live-streamed events featuring Donald Trump.
Exclusive leaks or announcements through specific media outlets.
Take no action (i.e., fewer than three major nominations are announced).
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
As of January 15, 2025, Joe Biden is the sitting President of the United States. However, news reports indicate that Donald Trump is projected to be inaugurated as the 47th President on January 20, 2025. Therefore, a new presidential administration led by Donald Trump is expected to be in place by April 1, 2025, and will be responsible for making major personnel nominations. During his first term (2017-2021), Donald Trump frequently utilized his personal Twitter account (now X) as a primary, often instantaneous, channel for major announcements, including high-profile personnel nominations. These social media announcements were typically followed by more formal White House press releases or statements from the Press Secretary. This approach allowed him to communicate directly with the public, often bypassing traditional media filters. The Biden administration (2021-2025) has largely adhered to more traditional communication methods for announcing major personnel nominations, typically involving official White House press releases, statements from the Press Secretary, or formal announcements during press briefings or events. Social media channels are primarily used to amplify these official announcements rather than serving as the initial public announcement platform. Recent reports (as of December 2024 and January 2025) indicate that Donald Trump, in preparation for his projected second term, has been using "statements" and his personal social media platform, Truth Social, to announce various nominations for his incoming administration. For example, nominations for Secretary of State, Secretary of Homeland Security, various ambassadors, and other key roles have been communicated via these channels. Key stakeholders include Donald Trump, the White House Press Secretary/Communications Office, social media platforms (Truth Social, X), and traditional media outlets. The U.S. Constitution (Article II, Section 2, Clause 2) outlines the President's power to nominate and, with the "advice and consent" of the Senate, appoint various officers. There are no specific legal requirements dictating the *channel* through which these nominations must be initially announced to the public.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include official White House Websites (e.g., WhiteHouse.gov), presidential social media accounts (Donald Trump's Truth Social and X accounts), Congressional records for official nomination submissions to the Senate, and major news archives (e.g., Associated Press, Reuters, New York Times, Washington Post, CNN, Fox News). All listed sources are publicly accessible online. Nominations are announced as they are made by the President; there is no fixed publication schedule, but a new administration typically makes a flurry of initial cabinet and high-level appointments shortly after taking office. New information will likely become available through Donald Trump's personal social media (Truth Social, X), and official statements from the President-elect's transition team or the incoming White House Communications Office.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of January 15, 2025, for the *incoming* Trump administration, several nominations have already been announced via "statements" and Truth Social (e.g., for Secretary of State, Secretary of Homeland Security, various ambassadors, and other key roles). The question asks about the primary channel *by April 1, 2025*, implying a pattern of multiple announcements over this period. During Trump's first term (early 2017), he frequently used Twitter as the *initial* public announcement channel for many high-profile cabinet and personnel picks. The Biden administration (early 2021) primarily used official White House press releases and statements for initial announcements. Based on Trump's past behavior and his current (as of late 2024/early 2025) announcements for his projected second term, there is a strong indication he will continue to favor direct communication channels, particularly his own social media, for initial public announcements of major personnel nominations.

## Trends
Factors that make Option A (Posts from Donald Trump's personal social media accounts) more likely include President-elect Trump's established communication style, his consistent preference for direct communication, control of narrative, platform loyalty (e.g., Truth Social), and the speed of announcement. Factors that make Option B (Official White House press releases or Press Secretary statements) more likely include institutional norms, credibility and formality, and broader reach. Factors that make Option C (Formal press conferences or live-streamed events featuring Donald Trump) more likely include high-profile nominations, the opportunity for public spectacle, and setting a tone for the new administration. Factors that make Option D (Exclusive leaks or announcements through specific media outlets) more likely include testing public and political reactions, rewarding favored outlets, and strategic control over the initial framing of a nomination. Option E (Take no action) would only be likely if a new administration is not in place or if, for some unforeseen reason, fewer than three major nominations are announced by April 1, 2025. Given the projected inauguration on January 20, 2025, and the typical pace of appointments for a new administration, this option appears less likely unless there are significant delays or political gridlock.

## Sources
- https://www.foxnews.com/politics/trump-announces-more-nominations-including-kari-lake-director-voice-america-broadcast
- https://www.cbsnews.com/live-updates/trump-administration-nominees-2025/
- https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/interactive/2025/trump-appointee-tracker/
- https://www.forbes.com/sites/saradorn/2024/12/21/trumps-cabinet-and-key-jobs-david-fink-aaron-reitz-and-chad-mizelle-among-latest-nominees/
- https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2024/11/22/donald-trump-cabinet-picks/76508894007/
questions.resolution_criteria: To resolve this question, we will identify all "major White House personnel nominations" made by the incoming Trump administration between January 20, 2025 (inauguration day) and April 1, 2025, inclusive. A "major White House personnel nomination" is defined as any nomination requiring Senate confirmation for the following roles: Cabinet Secretaries (Heads of the 15 executive departments), Cabinet-level officials (e.g., White House Chief of Staff, Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency, Director of the Office of Management and Budget, U.S. Trade Representative, Director of National Intelligence, Ambassador to the United Nations), Heads of major independent agencies (e.g., Director of the FBI, Director of the CIA, Administrator of NASA), and Ambassadors to G7 countries (Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, United Kingdom) and the United Nations. The "initial public announcement" is the earliest verifiable public communication of the nomination. We will count the number of initial public announcements made through each of the following channels: A. Posts from Donald Trump's personal social media accounts (e.g., Truth Social, X). This includes posts made directly by Donald Trump or his verified official accounts on platforms like Truth Social (truthsocial.com) or X (x.com/realDonaldTrump). Retweets or shares of news articles about nominations will NOT count; the post must explicitly announce the nomination. B. Official White House press releases or Press Secretary statements. This includes formal press releases issued by the White House Press Office, official statements made by the White House Press Secretary during a briefing, or official statements published on the White House website (WhiteHouse.gov). C. Formal press conferences or live-streamed events featuring Donald Trump. This includes nominations first announced by Donald Trump himself during a scheduled press conference, a live-streamed event (e.g., from the White House, Trump Tower, or Mar-a-Lago), or a public rally. The announcement must be the *first* public mention. D. Exclusive leaks or announcements through specific media outlets. This includes instances where a nomination is first reported by a single major media outlet (e.g., New York Times, Wall Street Journal, Fox News, CNN, Politico, Axios, Associated Press, Reuters) as an exclusive, often citing unnamed sources, and this report demonstrably precedes any official or personal announcement from the administration or Donald Trump. Resolution Process: 1. Identify all major White House personnel nominations made between January 20, 2025, and April 1, 2025, inclusive. 2. For each nomination, determine the *initial public announcement channel* based on the earliest verifiable public record. 3. Tally the count for each option (A, B, C, D). 4. If fewer than three major nominations are announced by April 1, 2025, the question resolves as E. Take no action. 5. If three or more major nominations are announced: The option with the highest count of initial public announcements will be the resolution. Tie-breaking rule: In the event of a tie between two or more options for the highest count, the option that was the initial announcement channel for the *most senior* position will resolve the question. Seniority will be determined by the order listed in the "major White House personnel nomination" definition (Cabinet Secretaries > Cabinet-level officials > Heads of major independent agencies > Ambassadors to UN > Ambassadors to G7 countries). If a tie still persists, the question will resolve to the option with the earliest *average* date of initial announcement for the tied nominations. If a tie still persists, the question will resolve to the alphabetically first option among the tied options.
questions.resolution_source: WhiteHouse.gov; Donald Trump's official accounts on Truth Social (truthsocial.com) and X (x.com/realDonaldTrump); Major news archives including Associated Press (apnews.com), Reuters (reuters.com), The New York Times (nytimes.com), The Washington Post (washingtonpost.com), The Wall Street Journal (wsj.com), Politico (politico.com), CNN (cnn.com), Fox News (foxnews.com), and Axios (axios.com).
questions.fine_print: What if a nomination is announced but later withdrawn or rejected? The resolution will be based on the *initial public announcement channel* of the nomination, regardless of its subsequent fate. What if a nomination is announced by a non-official source (e.g., a campaign staffer, a family member) before any of the listed channels? Such announcements will not count towards any option unless they are explicitly an "exclusive leak" to a specific media outlet (Option D) or made by Donald Trump himself on his personal social media (Option A). The focus is on official or presidential channels as defined. What if the resolution source is temporarily unavailable? Reputable archived versions of the websites (e.g., through the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine - archive.org) or comprehensive reporting from multiple major news outlets will be used as fallback sources. What if a government shutdown delays official publications? The resolution will proceed based on the earliest verifiable public announcement, regardless of government operational status. If official channels are delayed, personal social media or media leaks may become the initial channel. What if the administration uses a new, unforeseen primary channel? If a significant number of initial announcements occur via a channel not covered by options A-D, and it clearly constitutes the "primary channel," the question will be re-evaluated by Metaculus administrators. However, the existing options are designed to cover the most probable communication methods. What if a nomination is announced simultaneously across multiple channels? The earliest verifiable timestamp will determine the channel. If timestamps are identical or effectively simultaneous, and it involves an official White House channel (B or C) and a personal social media channel (A), the official White House channel will take precedence. If it involves an exclusive leak (D) and another channel, the exclusive leak will only count if it was truly *exclusive* and *preceded* other announcements. If it's truly simultaneous across multiple *types* of channels (e.g., Trump posts on Truth Social at the exact moment a White House press release goes out), the question will be resolved to the option that aligns with the most formal channel (C > B > A > D).
questions.base_rate_reasoning: Based on historical precedents from recent administrations: Donald Trump's First Term (2017): During the transition and early months of his first administration, Donald Trump frequently used his personal Twitter account (now X) for initial announcements of high-profile personnel nominations. This was a significant departure from traditional norms. Examples include his announcements for Secretary of State (Rex Tillerson), Secretary of Energy (Rick Perry), and Administrator of the EPA (Scott Pruitt) via Twitter. This suggests a high likelihood for Option A. Joe Biden's Administration (2021): The Biden administration largely reverted to more traditional methods, primarily using official White House press releases and statements from the Press Secretary for initial announcements. Social media was typically used to amplify these official announcements. This suggests a high likelihood for Option B. Current (as of late 2024/early 2025) Trump announcements for projected second term: Recent reports indicate Donald Trump has already been using "statements" and Truth Social for initial announcements of various nominations for his incoming administration (e.g., Secretary of State, Secretary of Homeland Security, ambassadors). This reinforces the trend towards Option A and potentially Option C (if "statements" refer to formal events). Given Donald Trump's established communication patterns and recent pre-inauguration announcements, the base rate for Option A (personal social media) being the primary channel is estimated to be 0.6 - 0.7. This is based on his past behavior and current indications, which strongly favor direct social media communication for initial announcements. Option B (official press releases) would be lower, perhaps 0.2 - 0.3, as it represents a more traditional approach that Trump has often bypassed. Options C and D are likely lower still, perhaps 0.05 - 0.1 each, as formal press conferences are typically reserved for a few very high-profile announcements, and exclusive leaks, while they happen, are less consistently the *primary* channel for a majority of nominations.

questions.title: By June 1, 2025, what will be the Trump administration's next major action regarding the White House Press Corps?
questions.background:
The relationship between the second Trump administration and the White House press corps has been fraught with tension since Inauguration Day. The administration has vocally criticized the reporting of several major news outlets, accusing them of unfair coverage and bias.

In a departure from previous administrations, the White House has begun actively cultivating relationships with "new media," including podcasters, YouTubers, and social media influencers. It recently established a dedicated space for them, dubbed "Podcast Row," and has held press briefings exclusively for these newer outlets, bypassing the traditional press corps.

This has led to speculation that the administration may seek to fundamentally alter the long-standing conventions governing press access at the White House. While some commentators believe the current strategy of verbal criticism and parallel media engagement will continue, others argue that a more direct confrontation is inevitable. It remains to be seen whether the administration will take formal steps to curtail the access or influence of established news organizations.
questions.question_text: By June 1, 2025, which of the following will be the first and most significant action taken by the Trump administration regarding the White House Press Corps? If multiple actions occur simultaneously or on the same day, the most significant action will be determined by a Metaculus editor based on the impact on the White House Press Corps' ability to access information and perform its duties, with formal revocation of credentials (A) generally considered most significant, followed by formal control of seating (B), then suspension of briefings (C), and finally formal warnings (D).
options: Revoke the primary "hard pass" credentials of at least one journalist from a major news organization (AP, Reuters, NYT, WaPo, WSJ, NBC, ABC, CBS, or CNN).
Formally assume control of the briefing room seating chart from the White House Correspondents' Association (WHCA).
Suspend the daily White House Press Briefing for at least five consecutive business days.
Issue a formal, written warning of potential credential review to a specific journalist or news outlet.
Take no action
questions.background_research:
## Background and Context
The relationship between the second Trump administration and the traditional White House Press Corps is characterized by significant tension and open hostility as of 2025-03-03. The administration frequently criticizes established news outlets, labeling their reporting as "fake news" and biased. Concurrently, the administration is actively fostering relationships with "new media" figures, including podcasters, YouTubers, and social media influencers, and has created dedicated spaces and held exclusive briefings for them. Historically, the first Trump administration (2017-2021) set a precedent of verbal attacks, lawsuits against media companies, and elevating non-traditional news sources. This confrontational approach has escalated in the second term (commencing 2025). Key developments as of 2025-03-03 include an increased frequency of media exchanges (averaging 1.9 per workday in the first 100 days, though often critical of "legacy media"), active cultivation of "friendly influencers" through "New Media Press Briefings," and specific restrictions on traditional press access. In late February 2025, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt formally assumed direct control over the composition of the White House press pool, ending decades of deference to the White House Correspondents' Association (WHCA). Additionally, the Associated Press (AP) had its access to certain White House events limited in February 2025, leading to a lawsuit. The administration has also stopped publishing transcripts of President Trump's remarks. Key stakeholders include the Trump Administration (President Trump, Press Secretary Leavitt), traditional news organizations (AP, Reuters, NYT, WaPo, WSJ, NBC, ABC, CBS, CNN), "new media" figures, the White House Correspondents' Association (WHCA), press freedom advocacy groups, and the Judiciary. The legal framework is primarily governed by the First Amendment, which protects press freedom, with courts having affirmed that the White House cannot exclude journalists based on viewpoint if it grants access to others. The Secret Service is responsible for issuing "hard passes" for White House access.

## Data and Information
Relevant data sources include official White House communications (press briefings, statements, memos, social media posts), White House Correspondents' Association (WHCA) statements, reporting from major news outlets (AP, Reuters, NYT, WaPo, CNN, Fox News), and court documents related to press access lawsuits. These sources are publicly available through news archives, official government websites (if maintained), court public access systems (PACER), and organizational websites. White House press briefings are typically held daily or several times a week, though schedules can be altered. Official statements and memos are published on an ad-hoc basis. News reports are continuously published. Court filings and rulings are published as cases progress. New information is expected to become available through White House press briefings and gaggles, official White House announcements, President Trump's Truth Social account and official White House social media channels, major news wires and outlets, WHCA communications, and court dockets for any new or ongoing legal challenges.

## Recent Numbers and Events
As of 2025-03-03: President Trump averaged 1.9 media exchanges per workday in his first 100 days of the second administration, indicating frequent interactions with the press (Fox News, referencing early 2025 data). In late February 2025, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt formally took control of determining the composition of the White House press pool, ending the WHCA's long-standing role (New York Post, referencing late Feb 2025). The White House press office began limiting the access of Associated Press reporters and photographers to certain events in February 2025, leading to a lawsuit by the AP (Bloomberg Law, referencing Feb 2025).

## Trends
Factors that make a YES resolution (i.e., one of options A-D) more likely include: the Trump administration's continued and vocal hostility towards "legacy media"; the active cultivation and empowerment of "new media" figures, suggesting a strategic shift away from traditional press engagement; the precedent set by the administration's recent restrictive actions, such as controlling the press pool and limiting AP access; a desire for greater narrative control by limiting traditional press access; and potential legal rulings that might clarify or expand the administration's power regarding press access. Factors that make a NO resolution (Option E: Take no action) more likely include: the potential for significant public backlash and condemnation from media organizations and press freedom advocates; the risk of further costly and time-consuming legal challenges, as demonstrated by the ongoing AP lawsuit; strong opposition from the White House Correspondents' Association (WHCA), which would likely mobilize against further formal restrictions; the administration potentially prioritizing other policy goals over new confrontations with the press; and the possibility that the administration deems its current strategy of verbal criticism and parallel media engagement sufficient for its communication objectives.
